mDNS gateway

Multicast DNS (mDNS) gateway helps users to discover various servers such as printers and Apple TV,
across VLANs. mDNS gateway uses the reflection mechanism to achieve service discovery across VLANS.

This feature is supported on the 5420, 6200, 6300, 6400, 8100, 8325, 8360, 9300 and 10000 Switch
Series only.

Subtopics

mDNS gateway overview
Configuring mDNS gateway
mDNS gateway commands

mDNS gateway overview

Reflection mechanism

With the reflection mechanism, the mDNS packets received in one VLAN are reflected to all the other mDNS
gateway-enabled VLANs based on filters. Only the packets containing the following records are supported
for reflection:

•  PTR record—Contains service-name to service-instance-name mapping.

•  SRV record—Contains service-instance-name to UDP/TCP port number and hostname mapping.

•  TXT record—Contains more information about the service-instance, such as, vendor information.

•  A record—Contains hostname to host IP address mapping.

Public

mDNS gateway 948

Filters

Filters are used to control the service discovery both within and across VLANs. You can configure filter rules
in the service profiles based on service-name and service-instance-names. If a profile is configured for a
VLAN, then the filter rules in the profile will be used to filter packets transmitted out of the VLAN interface.

NOTE

Filtering is performed based on parameters extracted from the first record.

Example of mDNS service discovery

The following figure shows an example topology where mDNS gateway is useful. Consider the following:

•  Enable mDNS only on Switch 1 in VLAN 1, 2, and 3.

•  Create a configuration rule in Switch 1 for VLAN 3—No host in VLAN 3 must discover any external

printers.

When Host 1 in VLAN 3 sends an mDNS query to Switch 1, the query is reflected in VLAN 1 and VLAN 2.
The Wireless Printer 1 in VLAN 1 generates a response that the Switch 1 receives and reflects to VLAN 2
and not to VLAN 3, because a rule is configured for VLAN 3 to not allow any printer service.

However, Host 1 will still be able to access Wireless Printer 2, because it is present in the same VLAN 3.

Figure 1. Example of mDNS service discovery

Public

mDNS gateway overview 949

Limitations

Following are a few limitations when configuring mDNS gateway:

•  Filtering is performed only based on parameters extracted in the first mDNS record.

•  Filtering is applied only on the egress mDNS packets.

•  Only IPv4 mDNS packets are supported.

•  mDNS gateway is recommended for deployments where mDNS is enabled on lesser VLANs. This is

because the switch allows the mDNS packets to be reflected to a maximum of 256 VLANs for the 6300,
6400, 8325, 8100, 8360, 9300 and 10000 Switch Series, and 128 VLANs for the 5420 and 6200
Switch Series, in incremental order of VLAN IDs, and in the VLAN from where the packet was initiated.

•  mDNS packets are rate limited at 150 packets per second.

•  When switches are connected directly with each other, you must enable mDNS only on one switch to

prevent a reflection loop.

•  You must enable debug logging only for troubleshooting an issue. Enabling debug logging on a high

scale mDNS configuration might lead to high CPU utilization and the system may slow down.

Configuring mDNS gateway

Perform the following steps to configure mDNS gateway:

Procedure

1.  Create a service for the mDNS gateway with the mdns-sd service command. You can group multiple

service IDs into a single service. Add description to the service and create service IDs with the following
commands:

a.  Add a description to the service with the description command.

b.  Create unique service IDs with the id command.

2.  Create a profile to be applied on a VLAN with the mdns-sd profile command. Add rules to the profile

with the <sequence-number> command.

3.  Enable mDNS gateway on a VLAN with the mdns-sd command.

4.  Apply a profile on the VLAN with the mdns-sd apply-profile tx command.

5.  Enable mDNS gateway globally with the mdns-sd enable command.

Public

Configuring mDNS gateway 950

ges.

timer

mdns-sd

Public

mdns-sd 955

Syntax

mdns-sd

no mdns-sd

Description

Enables mDNS gateway on a VLAN interface.

The no form of this command disables mDNS gateway on a VLAN interface.

NOTE

This command is applicable only to VLAN interfaces.

The switch will not process mDNS packets until the mDNS gateway is enabled
globally.

Examples

Enabling mDNS gateway on VLAN 10:

switch(config)# interface vlan 10

switch(config-if-vlan)# mdns-sd

Disabling mDNS gateway on VLAN 10:

switch(config)# interface vlan 10

switch(config-if-vlan)# no mdns-sd

mdns-sd apply-profile tx

Syntax

mdns-sd apply-profile <PROFILE-NAME> tx

no mdns-sd apply-profile <PROFILE-NAME> tx

Description

Configures mDNS gateway profile on the VLAN interface. When a profile is applied in the transmit direction,
all the mDNS traffic transmitted on the VLAN interface will be filtered based on the rules specified in the
transmit profile.

The no form of this command deletes the profile configuration from the VLAN interface in the transmit
direction.

NOTE

This command is applicable only to VLAN interfaces.

When no profile is configured on an interface then the default action is permit.

Parameter

Description

Specifies the profile name. Maximum 32 characters.

<PROFILE‐NAME>

Examples

Configuring mDNS gateway profile on VLAN 10:

Public

mdns-sd apply-profile tx 957

switch(config)# interface vlan 10

switch(config-if-vlan)# mdns-sd

switch(config-if-vlan)# mdns-sd apply-profile student tx

Deleting mDNS gateway profile on VLAN 10:

switch(config)# interface vlan 10

switch(config-if-vlan)# no mdns-sd apply-profile student tx

mdns-sd enable

Syntax

Description

Enables mDNS gateway.

The no form of this command disables mDNS gateway. Once the no form of this command is executed, all
the SVI VLANs, even though enabled with mDNS gateway, will stop reflecting mDNS packets to the enabled
VLANs.

Examples

Enable mDNS gateway:

switch(config)# mdns-sd enable

Disable mDNS gateway:

switch(config)# no mdns-sd enable

sequence-number

Syntax

            <SEQUENCE_NUMBER> {permit | deny}

 {service-name <SERVICE-NAME> | service-instance-name <SERVICE-INSTANCE-

NAME>}

 {service-name <SERVICE-NAME> | service-instance-name <SERVICE-INSTANCE-

Description

Adds a filter rule to the service profile. The sequence number configured determines the priority with which
the rule is matched. Lower the sequence number, higher is the priority.

Following are the filter match parameters:

•  Service-name: mDNS packets are matched against the service IDs configured under the service name.

•  Service-instance-name: mDNS packets are matched against the service instance name present in the

mDNS packets.

When no match criteria is specified in the rule, then the rule can be matched against any mDNS packet. Once
the match is found then either the packet can be permitted or denied based on the action specified in the
rule.

The no form of this command deletes the filter configured in the service profile.

NOTE
When an mDNS packet does not match any of the filters configured in the profile,
then the packet is denied.

Public

sequence-number 964

Parameter

Description

Specifies the service name. Maximum 32 characters.

Specifies the service instance name. Maximum 128 characters.

<SERVICE‐NAME>

<SERVICE‐INSTANCE‐NAME>

Examples

Adding filter rules to a service profile:

switch(config)# mdns-sd profile student

switch(config-mdns-sd-profile)# 10 permit service-name default-appletv

switch(config-mdns-sd-profile)# 20 deny service-name default-appletv

service-instance-name office._pdl-datastream._tcp.local

switch(config-mdns-sd-profile)# 30 permit service-instance-name

library._pdl-datastream._tcp.local

switch(config-mdns-sd-profile)# 40 deny

Deleting filter rules to a service profile:

switch(config)# mdns-sd profile student

switch(config-mdns-sd-profile)# 10 permit service-name default-appletv

switch(config-mdns-sd-profile)# 20 deny service-name default-appletv

service-instance-name office._pdl-datastream._tcp.local

switch(config-mdns-sd-profile)# 30 permit service-instance-name

library._pdl-datastream._tcp.local

switch(config-mdns-sd-profile)# no 30 permit service-instance-name
library._pdl-datastream._tcp.local

switch(config-mdns-sd-profile)# 40 deny

Single fabric Multicast VXLAN (V4 and V6)

NOTE

The HPE Aruba Networking 8320 Switch Series do not support VXLAN.

IPv4 and IPv6 multicast forwarding for both L2 and L3 are supported with AOS-CX VXLAN/EVPN
deployments. Refer to the VXLAN Guide for more info on VXLAN/EVPN and overlays/underlays.

L2 multicast over VXLAN refers to deployments where the multicast sources/receivers are on the same
L2 subnet/VLAN and bridging is required between switches that function as VXLAN Tunnel End Points
(VTEPs), as seen in Figure 1:

Figure 1. L2 multicast over VXLAN

L3 multicast over VXLAN refers to deployments where the multicast sources/receivers are on different
subnets/VLANs and routing is required between VTEPs as seen in Figure 2.

Figure 2. L3 multicast over VXLAN

Public

Single fabric Multicast VXLAN (V4 and V6) 977

Protocol and feature details 978

Overlay and underlay multicast support
L2 multicast over VXLAN

EVPN VXLAN distributed anycast gateway

Broadcast, unknown unicast, multicast (BUM) traffic replication

About this task

AOS-CX supports efficient forwarding of multicast traffic in the VXLAN fabric. Both overlay and underlay
multicast is supported to optimize multicast in the fabric. AOS-CX supports the following modes in the
overlay multicast:

Procedure

1.  CX native mode using IGMP/MLD/PIM messages in the fabric.

2.  Standard based multicast with IGMP-MLD proxy.

Results

In the underlay, multicast traffic is either head and replicated (HER) or multicast replicated (underlay
multicast) in the fabric. PIM BIDIR underlay is used to optimize overlay multicast in the underlay. With this
optimized overlay, multicast traffic is efficiently forwarded with the help of underlay multicast.

Overlay and underlay multicast support

The AOS-CX native mode implementation runs native L2 and L3 multicast protocols over VXLAN tunnels,
this is done to make the configuration and debugging of overlay and underlay multicast simpler. With this
implementation, multicast traffic will not be part of regular BUM flows and will only be forwarded based on
PIM and IGMP or MLD forwarding needs. IGMP or MLD and PIM-SM (v4) support are added to VXLAN to
route and bridge traffic across the tunnels.

NOTE
8325H switches do not support Multicast VXLAN, GBP support, IGMP/MLD
Overlay, and IPDB in 10.15.xxx.

L2 multicast over VXLAN

By default, L2 multicast data streams are sent by the source connected VTEP to all other remote VTEPs
interested in the same VNI, even if there are devices that are non-receivers (devices that are not interested
in that multicast stream).

Figure 1. L2 multicast over VXLAN with non-receiver

NOTE

Many details presented in this section are applicable to both CX native mode
and standard based multicast mode. However, in CX native mode, IGMP/MLD/PIM
packets are exchanged between the VTEPs and in standard based multicast
mode, EVPN route messages are exchanged. If there are any route messages,
they are called out explicitly.

Subtopics

L2 multicast over VXLAN enabled with IGMP or MLD snooping
Split horizon and L2 multicast
VSX and L2 multicast

L2 multicast over VXLAN 980

L2 multicast over VXLAN enabled with IGMP or MLD snooping

IGMP or MLD snooping can be configured at the VLAN level with L2 multicast over VXLAN tunnels, this
will prevent the source connected VTEP from sending the multicast stream to VTEPs without any interested
receivers (non-receivers).

Figure 1. L2 multicast over VXLAN enabled with IGMP snooping

L2 multicast over VXLAN enabled with IGMP snooping has the following characteristics:

•

IGMP or MLD snooping, once enabled prevents flooding over the L2 VNI; multicast data is only
forwarded to ports where IGMP or MLD joins are received.

•  VTEPs have full meshed VXLAN tunnels between them.

•

IGMP or MLD snooping is enabled per VLAN; the VLAN is mapped with L2 VNIs.

•  Joins and leaves are learned over VTEPs.

•  Packets (data and control) are sent over VXLAN tunnels and regular physical ports.

•  All versions of IGMP or MLD snooping (v1/v2/v3) are supported over VXLAN.

•  The querier can be inside or outside the VXLAN network.

•  VSX is supported and can be L2 extended.

Public

L2 multicast over VXLAN enabled with IGMP or MLD s... 981

•  When IGMP or MLD snooping is enabled on a VLAN (L2 VNI), all the tunnels are added as forwarded

ports (forwarded tunnels) for control packets; the control packets are flooded to all VTEPs.

•  As the network is meshed, joins from a non-querier VTEP is sent to the source VTEP, even if is not the

querier.

•  Multicast data packets are sent from source VTEP to remote VTEPs.

•  Split horizon is implemented at the L2 level such that packets that ingress a VXLAN tunnel will not get

re-forwarded to other VTEPs.

Split horizon and L2 multicast

AOS-CX implements split horizon in VXLAN networks to prevent loops; ingressing traffic on a VXLAN tunnel
is not sent to another VXLAN tunnel. This can cause traffic loss to both unknown multicast and L2 multicast
as IGMP or MLD joins are sent to querier. The querier typically is the middle device that transmits packets
from one port to another. In case of IGMP or MLD over an L2 VXLAN tunnel, this is blocked by split horizon.
To prevent this, the receiver-connected VTEP forwards IGMP or MLD joins to all VTEPs irrespective of
whether they are queriers or not. This prevents tunnel hop as every source VTEP is only a single tunnel hop
away from every receiver and source data is sent to the receiver VTEP directly without the involvement of
the IGMP or MLD querier.

VSX and L2 multicast

In the AOS-CX implementation, two switches/VTEPs configured with Virtual Switching Extension (VSX) are
configured as a single logical VTEP. AOS-CX does not use EVPN route type 1 and 4 for load balancing and
multihoming; regular VSX-based syncing is used for those purposes. The VSX switches can be access or
distribution/core VTEPs in a campus network, and are typically VSX leaf switches in a data center.

There are two scenarios that need to be considered for an L2 VNI that is stretched across VTEPs where one
(or more) of the VTEPs have a VSX Link Aggregation Group (LAG).

•  Scenario 1: Receivers connected to VSX LAG and source on the VXLAN overlay network.

•  Scenario 2: Source connected to VSX LAG and receivers on the VXLAN overlay network.

Scenario 1: Receivers connected to VSX LAG and source on the VXLAN overlay network

There are two cases to consider: dual homed VSX LAG and single homed VSX LAG.

Subtopics

Dual-homed VSX LAG with receivers
Single-homed VSX LAG with receivers

Public

Split horizon and L2 multicast 982

Dual-homed VSX LAG with receivers

Figure 1. Dual-homed VSX LAG with receivers

The following operation is done in no particular order:

•

IGMP or MLD joins from receiver or an access switch are synced between the VSX switches over the ISL
link, as a result both VSX switches will learn the join from the VSX LAG link.

•  Data traffic from the multicast source through the VXLAN tunnel reaches one of the VSX switches.

•  Whichever VSX VTEP receives the traffic forwards it downstream.

Public

Dual-homed VSX LAG with receivers 983

•  When one of the VSX switches is the querier, the traffic is forwarded to the querier as well via Inter

Switch Link (ISL); otherwise there is no traffic on the ISL link.

•  Egress filtering rule prevents ingress data on an ISL port from going down the VSX LAG if it is

multi-homed.

Single-homed VSX LAG with receivers

Figure 1. Single-homed VSX LAG with receivers

The following operation is done in no particular order:

Public

Single-homed VSX LAG with receivers 984

•  The IGMP or MLD joins from a receiver or an access switch are synced between the VSX switches over

the ISL link, as a result both the VSX switches learn the join over the VSX LAG link.

•  The moment the VSX LAG becomes single homed, the ISL port is added as a flood port. This ensures

any data received by one of VSX VTEP is always forwarded to the peer

•  Data traffic from the multicast source through the VXLAN tunnel reaches one of VSX switches and is

forwarded via ISL to its peer.

•  Whichever VSX VTEP has its link up forwards the data downstream, either received directly or via ISL.

•  As the VSX LAG is single-homed, there is no egress filtering that prevents data going from ISL to VSX

LAG.

Scenario 2: Source connected to VSX LAG and receivers on the VXLAN overlay network

As shown below, the source can be directly connected to the VSX VTEPs or it could be connected via an L2
switch. In both the cases, data reaches one of the VSX VTEPs because of VSX hash.

Figure 2. Source connected to VSX LAG

Public

Single-homed VSX LAG with receivers 985

The following sequence can happen in no particular order:

•  One of the switches in the L2 network is elected as querier and unknown multicast are always sent to
the querier port. IGMP or MLD joins are always forwarded to VXLAN tunnels as well as to the querier
port.

•  Data traffic from the multicast source reaches the VSX switches either because they are in the querier

path or because of the VSX LAG hash (in case directly connected),

•

IGMP or MLD joins from receiver reaches one of the VSX switches. The joins are sent over ISL port to the
peer switch. The peer VSX switch does a MAC lookup to figure out whether the join is to be learned on
the ISL port or over the VXLAN tunnel.

•  Once the join is learned on the VXLAN tunnel, multicast data is sent over the VXLAN tunnel when

received from the VSX LAG. The path is the same for both a single-homed and a dual-homed VSX LAG.

Public

Single-homed VSX LAG with receivers 986

Recommended configuration on the VSX VTEPs

As shown in Figure 5, the receiver-connected VTEP has a VXLAN tunnel to the VSX logical VTEP. The joins
from receiver-connected VTEP are received by one of the VSX switches over the VXLAN tunnel and by
the other switch via ISL. The VTEP that receives over ISL is a de-capsulated packet and does not have the
VXLAN header. That switch needs to perform a MAC lookup and that MAC sometimes might not be available
to the VSX VTEP.

To avoid this condition, the following two commands are recommended on L2 connected VSX VTEPs:

•  redistribute local-mac

This command is used to enable Type-2 route advertisement for local MAC address of all EVPN enabled
VLANs.

•  redistribute local-svi

This command is used to enable Type-2 route advertisement for local IP address and MAC address of the
SVI interfaces corresponding to the EVPN enabled VLANs (only required if SVI is configured).

NOTE

This recommendation is not applicable when standard based multicast mode is
enabled in the fabric, for example, when IGMP-MLD proxy is enabled.

IGMP or MLD querier positioning

An IGMP or MLD querier is one of the routers in the network whose responsibility is to send periodic IGMP
or MLD queries. In regular IGMP or MLD operation, IGMP or MLD joins are forwarded to the IGMP or MLD
querier. In addition, unknown multicast data is also sent to the querier. That is the way that the querier
connects source and destination routers. Sending joins towards the querier is a must for the IGMP or MLD
snooping operation; however in a VXLAN network, because of split-horizon rules, data packets are not sent
across VTEPs. The querier does not need to be a centralized router in a VXLAN network as every IGMP or
MLD join from the receiver connected VTEP is always forwarded to all remote VTEPs with the same VNI.
In the AOS-CX implementation, there is no particular recommendation on where to place the querier in a
VXLAN network.

When standard based multicast (IGMP-MLD proxy) IGMP MLD messages are not exchanged over the fabric,
every VTEP acts as a proxy querier for the network that they serve. It must be ensured that the Querier role
must stay with the VTEP and the Querier role should not be configured on the SVIs of any access switches
or extended L3 routers attached to the Leaf nodes. For Querier redundancy, use VSX as Lead nodes.

Public

Recommended configuration on the VSX VTEPs 987

L3 multicast over VXLAN

For L3 Multicast over VXLAN, both centralized L3 gateway and distributed L3 gateway use cases are
supported.

Subtopics

Centralized L3 gateway
Distributed L3 gateways
Border VTEP to external (non overlay) network
VSX and L3 Multicast
VSX Border VTEP with L3 connectivity to external (non overlay) network

Centralized L3 gateway

As shown in Figure 6, PIM-SM is only enabled at the centralized L3 gateway VTEP which has VLAN mapped
to VNIs. The remaining switches function as L2 VTEPs. The L2 VTEPs may or may not have IGMP or MLD
snooping enabled.

•  This use case is simple to implement as PIM-SM is enabled only at the centralized L3 gateway VTEP.

•  Mroute entries are simple as routing is between Switch Virtual Interfaces (SVI)/Routed Only Ports (ROP)

to L2 VNIs.

•  The only difference is that the downstream VLANs are mapped to L2 VNIs; multicast data stream
packets are tunneled via L2 VNI from the centralized L3 gateway VTEP towards the L2 VTEPs.

Figure 1. Centralized L3 gateway

Public

L3 multicast over VXLAN 988

Distributed L3 gateways

•  For distributed L3 gateways (See Figure 7), AOS-CX only supports Symmetric IRB and multicast routing
by extension of the PIM-SM protocol in the VXLAN tunnel. Only PIM-SM protocol is supported with L3
multicast over VXLAN tunnels.

•  An L3 VNI is used to route traffic from one VLAN to another as the same VLAN/L2 VNI is not present
across all VTEPs. PIM creates a logical interface on L3 VNI when  router pim  is enabled on the
overlay VRF. This interface is created once the VXLAN EVPN tunnels are formed.

•  All VTEPs create their per-tenant VRF L3 VNI interface and exchange PIM messages (e.g. hellos and
joins) to other VTEPs through it. They form PIM neighbors via this interface; when the remote VTEP
requests traffic via PIM joins on this interface on the source-connected VTEP, a multicast route entry is
installed with the incoming interface as SVI interface (source-connected SVI) and Outgoing Interface List
(OIL) with an L3 VNI<#> interface.

Public

Distributed L3 gateways 989

•  On the receiver connected VTEP, a multicast route entry is created to route traffic coming from L3 VNI
to the SVI where the receivers are present. Incoming interface is L3 VNI#, with OIL as the receiver SVI.

Figure 1. Distributed L3 Gateways

Additional details include the following:

•  Symmetric IRB with full meshed L3 VNIs.

•  L2 VNIs can extended across switches.

•

IGMP or MLD joins may be present in some/all the VTEPs for a particular flow.

•

IGMP or MLD snooping will be enabled in VTEPs.

•  PIM-SM is enabled on all the VRFs on the different VTEPs.

•  PIM neighbors are formed across L3 VNIs.

•  RPs can be present on any of the VTEPs.

Public

Distributed L3 gateways 990

•  PIM control (join and prune) packets are sent on the other side of the L3 VNIs (where source is present)

for every IGMP or MLD join.

•  When the source connected VTEP sees a PIM join from an L3 VNI, it adds the L3 VNI as OIL.

•  Data traffic from the multicast source is flooded across L3VNIs, split horizon rules will prevent data from

flooding across VTEPs (see Figure 8).

•  Non-interested VTEPs will drop/prune the multicast data (see Figure 8).

•

In mixed mode there could be some L2VNIs that will be extended over VTEPS whereas L3 VNIs are
enabled in all the VTEPs (per VRF).

•  Mix of IGMP or MLD snooping and PIM-SM for intra VLAN and inter VLAN routing.

Figure 2. Distributed L3 Gateways with non-receiver, multicast data stream sent and pruned at
remote VTEP

In case there are no interested receivers at remote VTEPs, L3 VNI are not added as an outgoing interface
and data are pruned at the source-connected VTEP (See Figure 9).

Public

Distributed L3 gateways 991

Figure 3. Distributed L3 Gateways with non-receivers, multicast data stream pruned at source
VTEP

Border VTEP to external (non overlay) network

A border VTEP can be used to connect the VXLAN overlay network to external (non overlay) networks via
either L2 or L3, sources and receivers in both networks are able to send and receive multicast streams.

L3 multicast routing using PIM-SM between the VXLAN and external (non overlay) network is recommended
for most deployments as this provides clear demarcation between the two networks.

Figure 1. Border VTEP to external (non overlay) network

Public

Border VTEP to external (non overlay) network 992

VSX and L3 Multicast

The following actions are done for all VSX VTEPs with PIM-SM and/or IGMP or MLD enabled:

•  The control packets (PIM packets) are synced between the VSX switches

•  Unknown multicast packets are also synced between the VSX switches

•  One of the VSX switches forwards the packets to prevent duplicates

There are different combinations with VSX, with source inside the VXLAN overlay network and receivers on
external (non overlay) network and vice versa. The different ways a receiver or a source is connected to a
VSX border VTEP are:

Public

VSX and L3 Multicast 993

•  L2 VSX LAG

•  L2 VSX LAG with SVIs

•  ROP

•  Point-to-Point (P2P) SVIs

VSX Border VTEP with L3 connectivity to external (non overlay)
network

About this task

Figure 11 shows a border VTEP with VSX LAG and SVI connected to an L3 switch with receivers on the
external (non overlay) network.

Figure 1. VSX Border VTEP with VSX LAG to receiver on external (non overlay) network

Public

VSX Border VTEP with L3 connectivity to external (... 994

V
S
X

(
.
.
.

The following actions are taken:

Procedure

1.  PIM hello packets are synced between the VSX border VTEPs via L3 over ISL.

2.  PIM joins are received by both VSX switches from the VSX LAG with SVI, both switches send PIM joins to

the source-connected VTEP via L3 VNI.

3.  The source-connected VTEP adds L3 VNI as the OIL even if it gets two PIM joins.

4.  Data traffic from the multicast source is hashed from the source-connected VTEP to only one of the VSX

switches as a unicast packet over the VXLAN tunnel.

5.  The VSX switch that receives the multicast data stream forms the Mroute entry with the SVI connected

to VSX LAG as egress.

Public

VSX Border VTEP with L3 connectivity to external (... 995

6.  The data stream packet goes to the other switch via ISL.

7.  The egress filtering rule prevents duplicates as the second switch will have a bridge entry for the egress

SVI.

8.  The external L3 Switch routes to the receiver.

9.  A similar action occurs for a single-homed border VTEP with VSX LAG.

Results

Figure 12 shows a border VTEP with VSX LAG and SVI connected to an L3 switch with source on the
external (non overlay) network.

Figure 2. VSX Border VTEP with VSX LAG to source on external (non overlay) network

Public

VSX Border VTEP with L3 connectivity to external (... 996

The following actions are taken:

1.  PIM joins from the receiver-connected VTEP is only sent to one of the VSX border VTEP, this is synced

to the other VSX peer.

2.  PIM hello packets are synced between the VSX switches via L3 over ISL.

3.  Both VSX border VTEPs that receive the PIM join adds L3 VNI as OIL.

4.  Multicast data stream from the external network reaches both VSX border VTEPs via VSX LAG with SVI

followed by ISL.

5.  The primary VSX switch forwards the data to receiver-connected remote VTEP (applies to the HPE

Aruba Networking 8325, 8400, 9300, and 10000 Switch Series).

6.  When 1 of the VSX border VTEP gets data directly and not via ISL, it forwards to the receiver-connected

remote VTEP (applies to the HPE Aruba Networking 6400, 8100, and 8360 Switch Series).

Figure 13 shows a border VTEP with ROP/P2P SVIs connected to an L3 switch with source on the external
(non overlay) network.

Figure 3. VSX Border VTEP with ROP/P2P SVIs to source on external (non overlay) network

Public

VSX Border VTEP with L3 connectivity to external (... 997

The following actions are taken:

1.  PIM hello packets are synced between the VSX VTEPs via L3 over ISL.

2.  Both VTEPs send PIM joins to the external L3 switch via ROPs; the assumption is that the source has

Equal Cost Multi Pathing (ECMP) to both VTEPs.

3.  The external L3 switch adds two ROPs/P2P SVIs as OIL in its Mroute entry.

4.  Multicast data is sent to both border VTEPs.

5.  8325/8400—only VSX primary VTEP forwards.

6.  6400/8100/8360—only the VTEP that has pending joins forwards.

Public

VSX Border VTEP with L3 connectivity to external (... 998

7.

If the link from primary VTEP to the source goes down and primary becomes unreachable, then there
can be traffic loss. In such a scenario, it is recommended to connect an additional L3 link per tenant VRF
and enable PIM over them. This link is typically a P2P SVI between the VSX peers. Each tenant VRF
requires its own P2P SVI. The ip pim-sparse vsx virtual-neighbor command should not be configured
on this P2P SVI.

Figure 14 and Figure 15 show a border VTEP with ROP/P2P SVIs connected to an L3 switch with receivers
on the external (non overlay) network. The example in Figure 16 shows multicast data and joins sent to
different VSX switches, and the example in Figure 15 shows multicast data and joins sent to the same VSX
switch.

Figure 4. A. VSX Border VTEP with ROP/P2P SVIs to receivers on external (non-overlay)
network

Figure 5. B. VSX Border VTEP with ROP/P2P SVIs to receivers on external (non-overlay)
network

Public

VSX Border VTEP with L3 connectivity to external (... 999

The following actions are taken:

1.  PIM hello packets are synced between VSX switches via L3 over ISL.

2.  PIM joins from ROP/P2P will reach one of the VSX switches.

3.  Only one VSX switch sends the PIM join towards the source-connected VTEP.

4.  The source-connected VTEP only adds the L3 VNI as an OIL and forward multicast data to the L3 VNI.

5.  Any VSX switch that receives data from the L3 VNI adds the dedicated L3 Interface (as an OIL for all

data coming from L3 VNI) and pending joined port as egress interface.

6.  The other VSX switch receives data over the dedicated L3 Interface. That VSX switch adds any pending
ROP/P2P joins as egress interface. No SVI is added as OIL as the VSX switch which received the data
from the L3VNI would have already added the SVI to OIL.

Public

VSX Border VTEP with L3 connectivity to external (... 1000

Dedicated interface details for the default forwarder

•  One interface is created per VRF, this functions as a default interface for forwarding packets if the

incoming interface is L3 VNI.

•  Note that packets are forwarded to this interface irrespective whether the OIL is ROP/P2P SVI or not.

•  The receiver side VSX device also adds the OIL on every interface that has pending joins.

•  Note that when the source is extended via ROP/P2P SVI, it is recommended to create an alternate L3

P2P interface between the VSX peers. This solution uses those interfaces only.

RP placement and election

Rendezvous Point (RP) placement is very important in a multicast network. The RP should to be located
close to the multicast source.

The recommendations for RP are:

•

•

If there is an existing multicast network with sources and receivers, the new VXLAN network VTEPs
should learn about the existing RPs (on non VXLAN network) via bootstrap router (BSR) or point to the
existing RPs statically.

If the new VXLAN network does not require multicast connectivity with the external network, redundant
VTEPs as new BSR/RPs should be deployed (this could be 2 x standalone VTEPs or 1 x VSX logical
VTEP that utilizes unique loopback IPs as BSR/RP with the primary VSX switch typically set to preferred.
If the primary VSX switch fails, all VTEPs utilize the secondary VSX switch unique loopback IP as
the remaining BSR/RP (refer to Use case 1: Campus network with centralized L3 gateway sample
configurations for a better understanding).

•  Anycast RP is supported in single-fabric, multi-fabric, and shared border setups.

Subtopics

Anycast RP support for VXLANs

Anycast RP support for VXLANs

Anycast RP functionality is supported in the overlay and can be achieved with the help of MSDP. Anycast
RP allows receivers and sources to connect with the closest RP, improving RP convergence. This benefits the
overlay fabric by reducing the PIM control plane traffic in the tunnel, as the PIM registrations or joins do not

Public

RP placement and election 1001

need to traverse the tunnel if the anycast RP is configured in the local leaf. For an active source, the PIM
registration occurs on the first hop leaf without entering the tunnel. Receivers requesting a join are served
by the first hop RP and can switch directly to SPT, reducing the number of joins sent over the tunnel.

Another advantage is in the event of an RP failure, the next nearest RP is selected due to the anycast
nature, avoiding single point of failures. This is particularly useful in the case of multi-fabric sites. Similar to
traditional network, MSDP peering must be configured between all anycast RP routers in the overlay. It is
important to note that MSDP rules in traditional networks apply to the overlay as well. For example, MSDP
peering must be done across all anycast peers in a full mesh peering, and a unique MSDP mesh group name
must be configured between them. The connect-source loopback used for peering must be configured with
unicast ip address.

Refer to the Anycast RP Support section of the Campus network with centralized L3 gatewayuse cases for a
better understanding.

NOTE

The PIM boundary feature is not supported in the overlay.

Example Topology

In the following example, the primary and secondary border routers are configured with anycast RP, and
MSDP peering is enabled between them. The anycast IP 10.0.0.1 is configured on both the primary and
secondary routers. A unique MSDP mesh-group name AnyRpMesh is configured between them.

Figure 1. Example of Anycast RP

Public

Anycast RP support for VXLANs 1002

MSDP configuration on Leaf1 VSX primary

!RP Interface Anycast IP
interface loopback 0

    vrf attach green

    ip address 10.0.0.1/24

    ip pim-sparse enable

!Connect source Interface Unicast IP

interface loopback 1

    vrf attach green

    ip address 10.1.1.1/24

    ip pim-sparse enable

!Enable PIM in overlay VRF

router pim vrf green

    enable

Public

Anycast RP support for VXLANs 1003

rp-candidate source-ip-interface loopback0

    bsr-candidate source-ip-interface loopback1

router msdp vrf green

   enable

   ip msdp peer 10.1.1.2

      connect-source loopback1

      mesh-group AnyRpMesh

      enable

MSDP configuration on Leaf1 VSX secondary

!RP Interface Anycast IP

interface loopback 0

    vrf attach green

    ip address 10.0.0.1/24

    ip pim-sparse enable

!Connect source Interface Unicast IP

interface loopback 1

    vrf attach green

    ip address 10.1.1.2/24

    ip pim-sparse enable

!Enable PIM in overlay VRF

router pim vrf green

    enable

    rp-candidate source-ip-interface loopback0

    bsr-candidate source-ip-interface loopback1

router msdp vrf green

   enable

   ip msdp peer 10.1.1.1

      connect-source loopback1

      mesh-group AnyRpMesh

      enable
Refer to Use case 1: Campus network with centralized L3 gateway for a better understanding of anycast RP.

Important points to note

•  There must be a peering session (full mesh peering) between all anycast RP routers in the network.

•  For small fabrics (less VTEP scale), it is possible to configure all leafs as anycast RP and configure mesh
group between all of them. However, note that with each peering session more router CPU resource will
be utilized.

•

It is possible to have anycast RP peering from an RP with in the fabric to an RP outside the fabric.

Public

Anycast RP support for VXLANs 1004

•  For multi-fabric setups, it is recommended to configure the border router as the RP and enable mesh
group between various border routers from different fabric. Peering between regular leaf across the
sites/fabrics are not recommended.

•  BSR can be used to advertise the RP to group mapping in the fabric, and the Candidate BSR must be

configured with unicast IP.

•  MSDP SA-Filter and MSDP SA-Limit features are supported in the overlay to filter SA messages.

EVPN VXLAN distributed anycast gateway

The distributed anycast gateway feature for EVPN VXLAN is a default gateway-addressing mechanism that
enables the use of the same gateway IP addresses across all the VTEP switches that are part of a VXLAN
network.

NOTE

For PIMv6, both the IPv6 link-local and the active-gateway address of the
anycast VLAN interface are configured with the anycast link-local address.

NOTE

In the anycast scenario, all three address (primary, secondary, and active
gateway) are the same.

Example of a VSX Primary Configuration for IPv4

interface vlan 10

   description multicast_source_connected_anycast_vlan

   vrf attach red

   ip address 100.100.1.2/24

   active-gateway ip mac 00:00:00:00:00:aa
   active-gateway ip 100.100.1.2

   ip pim-sparse enable
Example of a VSX Primary Configuration for IPv6

interface vlan 10

   description multicast_source_connected_anycast_vlan

   vrf attach red

   ipv6 address link-local fe80::2/64
   ipv6 address 100:100::2/64

   active-gateway ipv6 mac 00:00:00:00:00:aa

   active-gateway ipv6 fe80::2

Public

EVPN VXLAN distributed anycast gateway 1005

active-gateway ipv6 100:100::2/64

   ipv6 pim6-sparse enable
Example of a VSX Secondary Configuration for IPv4

interface vlan 10

   description multicast_source_connected_anycast_vlan

   vrf attach red

   ip address 100.100.1.2/24

   active-gateway ip mac 00:00:00:00:00:aa

   active-gateway ip 100.100.1.2

   ip pim-sparse enable
Example of a VSX Secondary Configuration for IPv6

interface vlan 10

   description multicast_source_connected_anycast_vlan

   vrf attach red

   ipv6 address link-local fe80::2/64

   ipv6 address 100:100::2/64

   active-gateway ipv6 mac 00:00:00:00:00:aa

   active-gateway ipv6 fe80::2

active-gateway ipv6 100:100::2/64

   ipv6 pim6-sparse enable

Subtopics

Designated router priorities

Designated router priorities

In this anycast gateway solution, the VXLAN source IP address is used as the designated router (DR) priority
and it overrides the default DR priority.

The  show ip pim interface <vlan-id>  command shows the operational DR priority and also
the DR priority of the elected DR. When distributed anycast gateway is configured, all the PIM neighbors
share the same anycast IP. The DR election is possible here by internally assigning a unique DR priority on
the anycast interfaces and is managed by PIM. All neighbors in the anycast segment get a unique DR priority
that can be viewed in the Operational DR Priority field. The Elected DR in the segment can be identified by
the Elected DR Priority field.

The show ip pim neighbor all-vrfs command also displays whether an anycast neighbor is present.

Subtopics

Recommendations
Limitation

Public

Designated router priorities 1006

Recommendations

•  For seamless operation, it is recommended not to manually configure DR priorities when anycast

gateway is configured.

•

If the DR priorities are configured, they should be unique in each of the leaf nodes and need to have the
same value on the VSX peers.

Limitation

Anycast VLANs cannot be configured as a candidate-RP or candidate-BSR. But they can learn the elected
RP set through BSR.

Supported platforms and standards

IPv4 and IPv6 multicast VXLAN is supported by the HPE Aruba Networking 6300, 6400, 8100, 8325, 8360,
8400, and 9300, and 10000 Switch Series.

Supported RFCs and standards

Public

Supported RFCs and standards 1010

AOS-CX supports two operating modes in VXLAN fabric, CX native mode using IGMP/MLD/PIM messages
in the fabric and standard based multicast with IGMP-MLD proxy. The CX native mode implementation is
currently proprietary and is not able to inter-operate with other vendors. However, the border VTEP that
connects to the external non-VXLAN network uses standards-based protocols such as PIM-SM (RFC 4601)
+ IGMP (RFC 3376 and RFC 2236). Standard based multicast with IGMP-MLD proxy mode is based on RFC
9251 and is inter-operable with other vendors.

Considerations and best practices
The following considerations and best practices are recommended for Multicast VXLAN:
•  IGMP or MLD snooping should be enabled on all VTEPs; multicast traffic flooding will happen without
IGMP or MLD snooping.
•  redistribute local-svi or redistribute local-mac are required on all VTEPs for proper querier
information propagation.
|     | Public |     | Considerations and best practices | 1018 |
| --- | ------ | --- | --------------------------------- | ---- |

•

In a VSX VxLAN deployment, ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor needs to be enabled on all PIM
enabled SVI interfaces with VSX-LAGs and L2 VNI mapped SVI interfaces. This is needed for both L2
and L3 extensions.

•

Issue the command vsx-sync mgmd-global on access switches to avoid duplicate packets if an ISL
event occurs on the core switches.

•  A unique virtual-mac should be configured on all VTEPs for a distributed L3 gateway use case; a VSX

VTEP pair should be configured with the same virtual-mac.

•  RP can be placed in the overlay network or in the external non-overlay network. Any existing RP in the
external non-overlay network can also be used. It is recommended for RPs to be placed near multicast
sources.

•  Underlay links, which is used to establish overlay connectivity, should not be enabled with multicast

(PIM/IGMP/MLD) configuration.

•  Overlay RPs or BSRs on VSX VTEPs are only supported on the HPE Aruba Networking 6400, 8100,

8325, 8360, 9300 and 8400 Switch Series.

•

It is not recommended to enable ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor on P2P SVI or ROP extensions.
Enabling the command can lead to traffic loss.

•  Customers who need to route Multicast Traffic across VRFs, need to configure the VRF merging router

as the RP/BSR in the overlay networks.

VTEP Use Cases

These are the supported VSX VTEP use cases:

•  VSX switches (logical VTEPs) for IGMP or MLD snooping and PIM-SM

•  VSX border VTEP with VSX LAG, L2 extension to sources/receivers in external/non overlay network

•  VSX border VTEP with VSX LAG, SVI + PIM-SM L3 extension to sources/receivers in external/non

overlay network

•  VSX border VTEP with ROP/P2P SVIs + PIM-SM L3 extension to sources and receivers in external/non

overlay network

Consider the following when deploying ROP and P2P SVI as uplinks on VSX border VTEPs to non-VXLAN
networks:

•  An additional L3 peering per tenant VRF between VSX primary and secondary on overlay is required to

handle failover scenarios, this link will normally be P2P SVIs over ISL.

Public

Considerations and best practices 1019

◦

ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include command is required to be configured on the L3 peering,
when receivers are connected behind ROP or P2P SVI uplinks.

NOTE

For example:

hostname BorderVTEP1A

interface vlan 1500

    description transit VLAN

    vrf attach VRF1

! IPv4

    ip address 1.1.2.1/30

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip pim-sparse enable

    ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include

•  When using a P2P SVI L3 extension, do not allow upstream VLANs in ISL; it will make the link non P2P.

•

ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor command is not recommended to be enabled on the additional L3
peering or P2P SVI link per tenant VRF between VSX peers.

The following restrictions are applicable for IPv4 Multicast VXLAN:

•  MSDP, mDNS, mtrace, Inter VRF Route Leaking (IVRL), and PIM-DM over VXLAN are not supported.

•  GShut is not supported on VXLAN based overlay networks.

Use cases

Select a use case from the list in the left navigation menu.

The use cases provide examples of networks using multicast VXLAN configuration.

NOTE
IPv6 is supported. The use case examples in this section show IPv4
configurations.

Use case 1: Campus network with centralized L3 gateway

This use case provides the following details:

•  Sample configurations

•  Relevant verification commands for a campus network with centralized L3 gateway

•  Overlay BSRs/RPs on VSX switches

•  Anycast RP configured between core VSX VTEPs

•  Receivers in overlay

•  Sources in external non-VXLAN network and external network that learn about overlay BSRs/RPs

On the HPE Aruba Networking 8360 VSX centralized L3 gateway, the additional 802.1Q link is required
as ROP is used towards the non-VXLAN network (as mentioned in the Considerations and best practices
section).

Figure 1. Use case 1 – Campus network with centralized L3 gateway topology

        neighbor 172.16.1.2 send-community extended

router pim

    enable

    rp-candidate source-ip-interface loopback3 group-prefix 224.0.0.0/4

    rp-candidate priority 200
    bsr-candidate source-ip-interface loopback3

    bsr-candidate priority 100

https-server vrf mgmt

Core-vsx-secondary

Configuration

!export-password: default

hostname Core-vsx-secondary

clock timezone us/pacific

no ip icmp redirect

profile Aggregation-Leaf

!

!

!

!

!

ssh server vrf mgmt

vlan 1,101

vlan 1001

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 1002

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 1003

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 1004

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 1005

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 1006

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 1007

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 1008

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 1009

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 1010

Public

Use case 1: Campus network with centralized L3 gat... 1046

ip igmp snooping enable

evpn

    redistribute local-svi

    vlan 1001

        rd 172.1.1.5:1001

        route-target export 1001:1001

        route-target import 1001:1001

        redistribute host-route

    vlan 1002

        rd 172.1.1.5:1002

        route-target export 1002:1002

        route-target import 1002:1002

        redistribute host-route

    vlan 1003

        rd 172.1.1.5:1003

        route-target export 1003:1003

        route-target import 1003:1003

        redistribute host-route

    vlan 1004

        rd 172.1.1.5:1004

        route-target import 1004:1004

        redistribute host-route

    vlan 1005

        rd 172.1.1.5:1005

        route-target import 1005:1005

        redistribute host-route

    vlan 1006

        rd 172.1.1.5:1006

        route-target import 1006:1006
        redistribute host-route

        route-target import 1007:1007

        redistribute host-route

Use case 1: Campus network with centralized L3 gat... 1047

vlan 1009

        rd 172.1.1.5:1009

        route-target export 1009:1009

        route-target import 1009:1009

        redistribute host-route

    vlan 1010

        rd 172.1.1.5:1010

        route-target export 1010:1010

        route-target import 1010:1010

        redistribute host-route

interface mgmt

    no shutdown

    ip dhcp

interface lag 100

    no shutdown

    description ISL

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1 tag

    vlan trunk allowed all

    lacp mode active

interface 1/1/1

    no shutdown

    lag 100

interface 1/1/2

    no shutdown

    description Additional L3 link

    ip address 80.1.1.1/30

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip ospf network point-to-point

interface 1/1/3

    no shutdown

interface 1/1/4
    no shutdown

interface 1/1/5

    no shutdown

    description KA

    ip address 192.168.100.2/30

interface 1/1/7

    no shutdown

interface 1/1/28

    no shutdown

interface 1/1/47

    no shutdown

    router-id 30.2.1.6

    distance 210

    redistribute connected

    area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 65535

    bgp router-id 172.16.1.4

    no bgp fast-external-fallover

    neighbor 172.16.1.1 remote-as 65535

    neighbor 172.16.1.1 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 172.16.1.2 remote-as 65535

    neighbor 172.16.1.2 update-source loopback 1
    address-family ipv4 unicast

        network 41.1.1.1/32

    exit-address-family

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 172.16.1.1 activate

        neighbor 172.16.1.1 send-community extended

        neighbor 172.16.1.2 activate

        neighbor 172.16.1.2 send-community extended

    exit-address-family

!

router pim

!

!

!

router ospf 1

    router-id 11.20.1.1

    area 0.0.0.1

router bgp 65535

    bgp router-id 172.16.1.1

    no bgp fast-external-fallover

    neighbor 172.16.1.2 remote-as 65535

    neighbor 172.16.1.2 update-source 172.16.1.1

    neighbor 172.16.1.4 remote-as 65535

    neighbor 172.16.1.4 update-source 172.16.1.1

    neighbor 172.16.1.5 remote-as 65535

    neighbor 172.16.1.5 update-source 172.16.1.1

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 172.16.1.2 activate

        neighbor 172.16.1.2 send-community extended

        neighbor 172.16.1.4 activate

        neighbor 172.16.1.4 send-community extended

        neighbor 172.16.1.5 activate

        neighbor 172.16.1.5 send-community extended
    exit-address-family

!

https-server vrf default

https-server vrf mgmt

router ospf 1

    router-id 11.20.2.1

    area 0.0.0.2

router bgp 65535

    bgp router-id 172.16.1.2

    no bgp fast-external-fallover

    neighbor 172.16.1.1 remote-as 65535

    neighbor 172.16.1.1 update-source 172.16.1.2

    neighbor 172.16.1.4 remote-as 65535

    neighbor 172.16.1.4 update-source 172.16.1.2

    neighbor 172.16.1.5 remote-as 65535

    neighbor 172.16.1.5 update-source 172.16.1.2

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 172.16.1.1 activate

        neighbor 172.16.1.1 send-community extended

        neighbor 172.16.1.4 activate

        neighbor 172.16.1.4 send-community extended

        neighbor 172.16.1.5 activate
        neighbor 172.16.1.5 send-community extended

    exit-address-family

!

https-server vrf default

https-server vrf mgmt

Anycast RP support

To configure anycast RP between core VSX VTEPs, use the following configurations:

NOTE

MSDP peering must be enabled in the tenant VRF, and in this use case, it is the
default VRF.

Configuration

Core-vsx-primary

  interface loopback 10

    ip address 11.0.0.1/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip pim-sparse enable

interface loopback 11

    ip address 11.0.0.12/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip pim-sparse enable

router pim

    enable

    rp-candidate source-ip-interface loopback10 group-prefix 224.0.0.0/4

    bsr-candidate source-ip-interface loopback11

router msdp

    enable

    ip msdp peer 11.0.0.13

        connect-source loopback11

        enable

        mesh-group mesh1

Core-vsx-secondary

    interface loopback 10

    ip address 11.0.0.1/32

Public

Use case 1: Campus network with centralized L3 gat... 1097

ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip pim-sparse enable

interface loopback 11

    ip address 11.0.0.13/32

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip pim-sparse enable

router pim

    enable

    rp-candidate source-ip-interface loopback10 group-prefix 224.0.0.0/4

    bsr-candidate source-ip-interface loopback11

router msdp

    enable

    ip msdp peer 11.0.0.12

        connect-source loopback11

        enable

        mesh-group mesh1

Verify MSDP peer information

Verify whether the peer state is up. For that the peer must be enabled and there must be a unicast path to
peer IP.

core-vsx-primary# show ip msdp peer all-vrfs

VRF: default

MSDP Peer: 11.0.0.13

Connection status

State: up  Resets: 0  Connection Source: loopback11(11.0.0.12)

Uptime(Downtime): 32m 54s   SA Messages sent: 19

SA's learned from this peer: 1

Peer Keepalive interval: 60

Peer Hold time: 75

Peer Connection Retry interval: 30

Mesh group: mesh1

Verify MSDP peer summary

The show command can be used to verify MSDP peer summary on the core VSX secondary.

core-vsx-primary# show ip msdp summary all-vrfs

VRF: default

MSDP Peer Status Summary

Peer address    State    Uptime(Downtime)    Reset Count    SA Count

11.0.0.13       up       32m 59s             0              1

Verify MSDP peer count

This show command can be used to verify MSDP peer count on the core VSX secondary.

Public

Use case 1: Campus network with centralized L3 gat... 1098

core-vsx-primary# show ip msdp count all-vrfs

VRF: default

SA state per Peer counters

<Peer>:<#SA learned>

11.0.0.13: 2

Total entries: 2

Verify MSDP peer SA-Cache information

This show command is used to verify sa-cache to check if the flow is listed. With the exception of the RP
near to the multicast source, all other RP learn the source information via SA message exchange and stored
in the SA-Cache.

Core-vsx-primary# show ip msdp sa-cache all-vrfs

VRF: default

Total entries: 2

(60.1.1.2, 227.1.1.1)  RP: 11.0.0.1  Peer: 11.0.0.13

(60.1.1.2, 228.1.1.1)  RP: 11.0.0.1  Peer: 11.0.0.13

Verify the active multicast flows that are registered by source DR to this RP. The anycast RP
that is near to the multicast source will have this entry updated and the remaining RP will
have the SA-Cache updated.

switch# show ip pim rp-registered-sources all-vrfs

Multicast flows registered with this RP

VRF : default

Total number of entries : 2

Source Address           Group Address           RP Address

--------------------     --------------------    --------------------

60.1.1.2                 227.1.1.1               11.0.0.1

60.1.1.2                 228.1.1.1               11.0.0.1

Use case 2: DC network with distributed L3 gateway

This use case shows sample configurations and relevant verification commands for a DC network with
distributed L3 gateway, external BSR/RPs on a non-VXLAN network, sources in DC VXLAN network, and
receivers in a non-VXLAN network.

Figure 1. DC network with distributed L3 gateway topology

Public

Use case 2: DC network with distributed L3 gateway 1099

Configuration and verification details for the devices in this use case are provided in the following sections:

vsx

    system-mac 00:00:00:0a:0a:0a

    area 0.0.0.0

    neighbor 5.5.5.5 remote-as 1

    neighbor 5.5.5.5 update-source loopback 0

        neighbor 5.5.5.5 send-community extended
    exit-address-family

        no bgp fast-external-fallover

            redistribute connected

            redistribute static

    neighbor 4.4.4.4 remote-as 1

    neighbor 5.5.5.5 remote-as 1

    neighbor 5.5.5.5 update-source loopback 0

        neighbor 5.5.5.5 activate

        neighbor 5.5.5.5 send-community extended

    exit-address-family

hostname Leaf02

profile Leaf

vrf DC

    route-target export 65501:1 evpn

    route-target import 65501:1 evpn

cli-session

    timeout 0

!

!

!

!

!

vlan 1

router ospf 1

    router-id 6.6.6.6

    area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 1

    bgp router-id 6.6.6.6

    neighbor 4.4.4.4 remote-as 1
    neighbor 4.4.4.4 update-source loopback 0

    neighbor 5.5.5.5 remote-as 1

    neighbor 5.5.5.5 update-source loopback 0

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 activate

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 send-community extended

        neighbor 5.5.5.5 activate

        neighbor 5.5.5.5 send-community extended

    exit-address-family

!

    vrf DC

            redistribute connected

            redistribute static

router pim vrf DC

8325-border-prim

Public

Use case 2: DC network with distributed L3 gateway 1154

Configuration

!export-password: default

hostname 8325-border-prim

no ip icmp redirect

profile Leaf

vrf DC

    rd 192.1.0.7:1

    route-target export 65501:1 evpn

    route-target import 65501:1 evpn

cli-session

    timeout 0

!

!

!

!

!

ssh server vrf mgmt

vlan 1

vlan 101

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 102

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 103

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 104

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 105

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 106

    ip igmp snooping enable
vlan 107

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 108

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 109

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 110

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 500

virtual-mac 00:00:00:0c:0c:0c

evpn

    redistribute local-svi

Use case 2: DC network with distributed L3 gateway 1161

area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 1

    bgp router-id 109.0.0.1

    neighbor 4.4.4.4 remote-as 1

    neighbor 4.4.4.4 update-source loopback 0

    neighbor 5.5.5.5 remote-as 1

    neighbor 5.5.5.5 update-source loopback 0

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 activate

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 send-community extended

        neighbor 5.5.5.5 activate

        neighbor 5.5.5.5 send-community extended

    exit-address-family

!

    vrf DC

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

            redistribute ospf

        exit-address-family

!

router pim vrf DC

    enable

https-server vrf mgmt

8325-border-sec

Configuration

!export-password: default

hostname 8325-border-sec

no ip icmp redirect

profile Leaf

vrf DC

    rd 192.1.0.7:1

    route-target export 65501:1 evpn

    route-target import 65501:1 evpn

ntp server 10.100.0.12 minpoll 4 maxpoll 4 iburst

cli-session

    timeout 0

!

!

!

!

!

ssh server vrf mgmt

vlan 1

vlan 101

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 102

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 103

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 104

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 105

    ip igmp snooping enable

Public

Use case 2: DC network with distributed L3 gateway 1173

vlan 106

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 107

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 108

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 109

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 110

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 500

virtual-mac 00:00:00:0c:0c:0c

evpn

    redistribute local-svi

    vlan 101

        rd auto

        route-target export auto

        route-target import auto

        redistribute host-route

    vlan 102

        rd auto

        route-target export auto

        route-target import auto

        redistribute host-route

    vlan 103

        rd auto

        route-target export auto

    vlan 108

    vlan 109

    vlan 110

        route-target import auto

        redistribute host-route

interface mgmt

    no shutdown

    ip dhcp

system interface-group 1 speed 10g

    !interface group 1 contains ports 1/1/1-1/1/12

system interface-group 2 speed 10g

    !interface group 2 contains ports 1/1/13-1/1/24

system interface-group 3 speed 10g

    !interface group 3 contains ports 1/1/25-1/1/36

system interface-group 4 speed 10g

    !interface group 4 contains ports 1/1/37-1/1/48
interface lag 1

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1 tag

    vlan trunk allowed all

    lacp mode active

interface lag 2 multi-chassis

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 500

Public

Use case 2: DC network with distributed L3 gateway 1179

distance 210

    redistribute bgp

    redistribute connected

    area 0.0.0.0

router ospf 1

    router-id 99.99.99.99

    area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 1

    bgp router-id 110.0.0.1

    neighbor 4.4.4.4 remote-as 1

    neighbor 4.4.4.4 update-source loopback 0

    neighbor 5.5.5.5 remote-as 1

    neighbor 5.5.5.5 update-source loopback 0

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 activate

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 send-community extended

        neighbor 5.5.5.5 activate

        neighbor 5.5.5.5 send-community extended

    exit-address-family

!

    vrf DC

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

            redistribute ospf

        exit-address-family

!

router pim vrf DC

    enable

https-server vrf mgmt.

!export-password: default

hostname Edge-primary

no ip icmp redirect

profile Aggregation-Leaf

ntp server 10.100.0.12 minpoll 4 maxpoll 4 iburst

cli-session

    timeout 0

!

!

!

!

ssh server vrf mgmt

vlan 1

vlan 301

    ip igmp snooping enable

    ip igmp snooping enable

    ip igmp snooping enable

    ip igmp snooping enable

    ip igmp snooping enable

    ip igmp snooping enable

    ip igmp snooping enable

    ip igmp snooping enable

    ip igmp snooping enable

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 500

interface mgmt

    no shutdown

    ip dhcp

system interface-group 1 speed 10g

    !interface group 1 contains ports 1/1/1-1/1/4

interface lag 1

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1 tag

    vlan trunk allowed all

    lacp mode active
interface lag 2 multi-chassis

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 500

    lacp mode active

interface lag 3 multi-chassis

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 1,301-310

Use Case 3: IPv6 VXLAN tunnel source with dual stack IPv4/IPv6
multicast overlay

PIM supports both IPv4 and IPv6 as underlay VTEP IP addresses. In deployments with an IPv6 underlay, PIM
forms an auto-generated link-local address to exchange control packets and forms a PIM neighborship over
an L3VNI interface with other VTEPs. The auto generated IP will be a link local IP in case of overlay IPv6.

The following command introduced in AOS-CX 10.12.1000 to configure an IP address for L3VNI
neighborship.

ipv6 multicast pim6 l3vni-source-ip  X:X::X:X
This configuration provides greater network manageability by overriding the auto-generated IPv6 address
with a user-specified IPv6 address for the PIM L3VNI interface. This configuration is optional and only

Public

applicable for deployments using an IPv6 underlay type. The IPv6 address configured with this command
will be used only for PIM control packet exchanges and will not reside in the router's forwarding tables.

!Version ArubaOS-CX LL.10.12.1000L

!export-password: default

hostname 8360-Stub-Std-Sec

vrf vrf_emp

    rd 172.19.105.8:10010

    route-target export 65001:10010 evpn

    route-target import 65001:10010 evpn

vrf vrf_guest

    rd 172.19.105.8:10020

    route-target export 65001:10020 evpn

    route-target import 65001:10020 evpn

vrf vrf_loc

    rd 172.19.105.8:10030

    route-target export 65001:10030 evpn

    route-target import 65001:10030 evpn

vrf vrf_server

    rd 172.19.105.8:10040

    route-target export 65001:10040 evpn

    route-target import 65001:10040 evpn

ntp server 10.5.6.10 minpoll 4 maxpoll 4 iburst

ntp enable

cli-session

    timeout 0

!

!

    name vlan_30_vrf_emp

    ip igmp snooping enable

    ipv6 mld snooping enable

    ip igmp snooping enable
    ipv6 mld snooping enable

vlan 50

    name vlan_50_vrf_emp

    ip igmp snooping enable

    ipv6 mld snooping enable

vlan 60

    ip igmp snooping enable

    ipv6 mld snooping enable

vlan 3500-3640,3810-3949

virtual-mac 02:00:a8:00:00:01

evpn

    area 0.0.0.0

    bgp router-id 172.19.105.2

    no bgp fast-external-fallover

    neighbor ntlcampus update-source loopback 0

Public

Configuration example for the 6300-VSF VTEP switch... 1255

area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 65050

    bgp router-id 172.19.105.10

    no bgp fast-external-fallover

    neighbor ntlcampus peer-group

    neighbor ntlcampus remote-as 65050

    neighbor ntlcampus fall-over

    neighbor ntlcampus update-source loopback 0

    neighbor 172:19:105::5 peer-group ntlcampus

    neighbor 172:19:105::6 peer-group ntlcampus

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 172:19:105::5 send-community extended

        neighbor 172:19:105::5 activate

        neighbor 172:19:105::6 send-community extended

        neighbor 172:19:105::6 activate

    exit-address-family

!

    vrf vrf_emp

        no bgp fast-external-fallover

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

        address-family ipv6 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

!

    vrf vrf_guest

        no bgp fast-external-fallover

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

        address-family ipv6 unicast
            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

!

    vrf vrf_loc

        no bgp fast-external-fallover

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

        address-family ipv6 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

Public

Configuration example for the 6300-VSF VTEP switch... 1256

!

    vrf vrf_server

        no bgp fast-external-fallover

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

        address-family ipv6 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

!

router pim vrf vrf_emp

    enable

    rp-candidate source-ip-interface vlan850 group-prefix 224.0.0.0/4

    bsr-candidate source-ip-interface vlan850

router pim vrf vrf_guest

    enable

router pim vrf vrf_loc

    enable

router pim vrf vrf_server

    enable

router pim6 vrf vrf_emp

    enable

router pim6 vrf vrf_guest

    enable

router pim6 vrf vrf_loc

    enable

router pim6 vrf vrf_server

    enable

    neighbor ntlcampus fall-over

    neighbor ntlcampus update-source loopback 0

    neighbor 172:19:105::5 peer-group ntlcampus

    neighbor 172:19:105::6 peer-group ntlcampus

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 172:19:105::5 send-community extended

        neighbor 172:19:105::5 activate

        neighbor 172:19:105::6 send-community extended

        neighbor 172:19:105::6 activate

    exit-address-family

!

    vrf vrf_emp

        no bgp fast-external-fallover

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

        address-family ipv6 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

!

    vrf vrf_guest

        no bgp fast-external-fallover

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

        address-family ipv6 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

!

    vrf vrf_loc
        no bgp fast-external-fallover

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

        address-family ipv6 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

!

    vrf vrf_server

        no bgp fast-external-fallover

        address-family ipv4 unicast

Public

Configuration example for the 6400 access switch: 1266

redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

        address-family ipv6 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

!

router pim vrf vrf_emp

    enable

router pim vrf vrf_guest

    enable

router pim vrf vrf_loc

    enable

router pim vrf vrf_server

    enable

router pim6

    enable

router pim6 vrf vrf_emp

    enable

router pim6 vrf vrf_guest

    enable

router pim6 vrf vrf_loc

    enable

router pim6 vrf vrf_server

    enable

https-server vrf default

https-server vrf mgmt

Verifying the configuration

About this task

Use the following procedure to verify the correct configuration of a IPv6 VXLAN tunnel source with dual
stack IPv4/IPv6 multicast overlay, as shown in Use Case#3.

Procedure

1.  Verify the Access VTEP configuration.

a.  Verify ospfv3 output on the 6300 VSF VTEP switch using the show ipv6 ospfv3 neighbors

command.

Public

Verifying the configuration 1267

b.  Verify BGP neighborship on the 6300 VSF VTEP switch using the command show bgp all summary.

2.  Verify IPv6 VXLAN tunnels are operational.

a.  On the 6400 access switch, verify L2VNI tunnels and L3VNI tunnels are UP using the show interface

vxlan vteps command.

Public

Verifying the configuration 1268

b.  On the 6400 access switch, verify PIM6 neighbors are UP over vxlan for each VRF using the show

ipv6 pim6 neighbor brief vrf <vrf> command.

Public

Verifying the configuration 1269

3.  Verify MLD joins are acting expected for all VLANs using the show ipv6 mld snooping vlan <vlan>and

show ipv6 mld interface <interface> commands on the 6400 access switch.

4.  Verify pim6 mroute using the show ipv6 mld mrout vrf <vrf> command on the 6400 access switch.

Public

Verifying the configuration 1270

5.  Verify RP-set info is learned on all VTEPs.

a.  Issue the show ipv6 pim6 rp-set vrf <vrf> command on the 6300 standalone VTEP switch.

b.  Issue the show ipv6 pim6 rp-set vrf all-vrfs command on the 6300 VTEP VSF switch.

Public

Verifying the configuration 1271

Example Configuration Outputs

The following image displays example output of the show run interface VXLAN command on the 6400
access switch.

Public

Verifying the configuration 1272

The following image displays example output of the show run interface <vlan> command on the 6400
access switch.

The following image displays example output of the show run pim6 command on the 8360 standalone
switch.

Public

Verifying the configuration 1273

Scale-IPv6 Underlay
Platform
|          | 6300 | 6400 | 8360 | 8100 |
| -------- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- |
| VTEPs    | 32   | 32   | 32   | 32   |
| MROUTE   | 2000 | 2000 | 2000 | 2000 |
| MLD join | 2000 | 2000 | 2000 | 2000 |
| L3VNI    | 16   | 16   | 16   | 8    |
| L2VNI    | 128  | 128  | 128  | 64   |

Sample Multicast over VXLAN Config of a VSX Leaf i...
Sample Multicast over VXLAN Config of a VSX Leaf in Symmetric
IRB topology
NOTE
In cases of IPv6 overlay, use the IPv6 version of the commands. Examples in this
section show the IPv4 version of commands.
Public Sample Multicast over VXLAN Config of a VSX Leaf i... 1274

Leaf-1A configuration:

hostname Leaf-1A

vrf Test1

    rd 100.0.0.1:15000

    route-target export 1234567:15000 evpn

    route-target import 1234567:15000 evpn

vlan 1

vlan 101

#Enable IGMP snooping

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 200

virtual-mac 00:00:00:0a:0a:0a

#evpn configuration

evpn

    redistribute local-svi

    vlan 101

        rd 100.0.0.1:101

        route-target export 101:101

        route-target import 101:101

#L2vni svi configuration

Enable IGMP and PIM on the L2 VNI mapped SVI

interface vlan 101

    vrf attach Test1

    ip address 101.1.1.2/24

    active-gateway ip mac 00:00:20:00:10:01

    active-gateway ip 101.1.1.1

    ip igmp enable

    ip pim-sparse enable

    ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

#MC-LAG-AF SVI Connection to device Outside Fabric

interface vlan 200

    vrf attach Test1

    vsx active-forwarding

    ip address 101.2.1.2/24

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip pim-sparse enable

    ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

#vxlan configuration

interface vxlan 1

    source ip 100.0.0.1

    vxlan-counters aggregate

    no shutdown

    vni 101

        vlan 101

Public

Sample Multicast over VXLAN Config of a VSX Leaf i... 1275

vni 15001

        vrf Test1

        routing

vsx

    inter-switch-link lag 1

    role primary

    keepalive peer 192.168.1.2 source 192.168.1.1

router ospf 1

    router-id 11.11.11.11

    area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 1

    bgp router-id 1.1.1.1

    neighbor 3.3.3.3 remote-as 1

    neighbor 3.3.3.3 update-source loopback 0

    neighbor 4.4.4.4 remote-as 1

    neighbor 4.4.4.4 update-source loopback 0

    neighbor 5.5.5.5 remote-as 1

    neighbor 5.5.5.5 update-source loopback 0

    address-family ipv4 unicast

        redistribute connected

    exit-address-family

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 activate

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 send-community extended

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 activate

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 send-community extended

        neighbor 5.5.5.5 activate

        neighbor 5.5.5.5 send-community extended

    exit-address-family

!

    vrf Test1

        address-family ipv4 unicast
            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

!

#Enable PIM

router pim vrf Test1

    enable

hostname Leaf-1B

vrf Test1

    rd 100.0.0.1:15000

    route-target import 1234567:15000 evpn

   #Enable IGMP snooping

vlan 200

#evpn configuration

evpn

    redistribute local-svi

    vlan 101

        rd 100.0.0.1:101

#L2vni svi configuration. Configure IGMP and PIM on the L2 VNI mapped SVI

#MC-LAG-AF SVI Connection to device Outside Fabric

        vrf Test1

        routing

vsx

Public

Sample Multicast over VXLAN Config of a VSX Leaf i... 1277

inter-switch-link lag 1

    role secondary

    keepalive peer 192.168.1.1 source 192.168.1.2

router ospf 1

    router-id 1.1.2.1

    area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 1

    bgp router-id 1.1.2.1

    neighbor 3.3.3.3 remote-as 1

    neighbor 3.3.3.3 update-source loopback 0

    neighbor 4.4.4.4 remote-as 1

    neighbor 4.4.4.4 update-source loopback 0

    neighbor 5.5.5.5 remote-as 1

    neighbor 5.5.5.5 update-source loopback 0

        redistribute connected

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 activate

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 send-community extended

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 activate

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 send-community extended

        neighbor 5.5.5.5 activate

        neighbor 5.5.5.5 send-community extended
