!

    vrf Test1

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

!

#Enable PIM
router pim vrf Test1

    enable

RP configuration on PIM router outside fabric:

# SVI Connection to VxLAN VSX Leaf-1

interface vlan 200

    ip address 101.2.1.3/24
    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip pim-sparse enable

router ospf 1

    router-id 7.7.7.7

Public

Sample Multicast over VXLAN Config of a VSX Leaf i... 1278

    area 0.0.0.0
router pim
    enable
    rp-candidate source-ip-interface loopback0
    rp-candidate group-prefix 224.0.0.0/4
    bsr-candidate source-ip-interface loopback0

Multicast support over static VXLAN tunnels
In heterogeneous networks with static and dynamic VXAN tunnels, decisions on forwarding multicast control
packets are determined by the configuration of tunnel origin and inter-vxlan-bridging-mode.
NOTE
5420 Switch series support static VXLAN tunnels only.
Table 1. When the inter‐vxlan‐bridging mode is static‐evpn
| static‐evpn |     | Static |     | EVPN  |     |
| ----------- | --- | ------ | --- | ----- | --- |
| Static      |     | Block  |     | Allow |     |
| EVPN        |     | Allow  |     | Block |     |
Table 2. When the inter‐vxlan‐bridging mode is static‐all
static‐all
|        |     | Static |     | EVPN  |     |
| ------ | --- | ------ | --- | ----- | --- |
| Static |     | Allow  |     | Allow |     |
| EVPN   |     | Allow  |     | Block |     |
Deployment scenarios
In the following topology, static-evpn is required for IGMP/MLD control packets to flow between Extended
Edge and Edge-2. Extended Edge is a 6200 Switch Series which supports only static VXLAN configuration.
A dynamic tunnel will be formed between Edge-1 and Edge-2. For data and control packets to flow between
extended Edge-1 and Edge-2, inter-vxlan-briding-mode should be configured as static-evpn on Edge-1.
With this configuration, the traffic will flow between static and dynamic tunnels.
|     | Public |     | Multicast support over static VXLAN tunnels |     | 1279 |
| --- | ------ | --- | ------------------------------------------- | --- | ---- |

In the following topology, static-all is required for IGMP/MLD control packets to flow between Extended
Edges, and between Extended Edges and Edge-2. Extended edges are 6200 which supports only static
VXLAN configuration. A dynamic tunnel will be formed between Edge-1 and Edge-2. For data and control
packets to flow between extended Edge-1 and Edge-2 as well as between two extended edges, inter-vxlan-
briding-mode should be configured as static-all on Edge-1. With this configuration, the traffic will flow from
static tunnels to static as well as dynamic tunnels.

Public

Multicast support over static VXLAN tunnels 1280

NOTE

In the typical deployment, the Edge act as the layer-3 gateway for the static
VXLAN clients (extended edges). The static VXLAN will be terminated on the
edge and PIM routing handles routing. Depending on requirements, the VLAN
can be stretched from Extended Edge to Edge, to the core VTEP, and across
different fabrics as well.

Limitations

•  PIM over static VXLAN is not supported, hence it is not recommended to enable PIM on Extended Edge.

•  The chain topology with multiple 6200 switches are connected one after another as extended edges is

not supported.

Troubleshooting

To check whether the querier is learned correctly on the extended edge, use the following show command
on Extended Edges:

switch# show ip igmp snooping

VLAN ID : 10

Public

Multicast support over static VXLAN tunnels 1281

VLAN Name : VLAN10

IGMP Configured Version : 3

IGMP Operating Version : 3

IGMP preprogram-starg-flow is not operational

Querier Address : 10.10.10.1

Querier Port : vxlan1(1.1.1.1)

Querier UpTime :0m 25s

Querier Expiration Time :0m 12s
In the output, Querier Port : vxlan1(1.1.1.1) ensures that the querier is learned through the static tunnel of
Edge.

To make sure joins are learned correctly from the VXLAN port, use the following show command on Edge-1:

EDGE-1# show ip igmp snooping vlan 100 detail

IGMP Snooping Protocol Info

Total VLANs with IGMP enabled             : 1

Current count of multicast groups joined  : 3

IGMP Drop Unknown Multicast               : Global

VLAN ID : 100

VLAN Name : VLAN100

IGMP Configured Version : 3

IGMP Operating Version : 3

IGMP preprogram-starg-flow is not operational

Querier Address [this switch] : 10.10.10.1

Querier Port :

Querier UpTime :59m 21s

Querier Expiration Time :3m 57s

Router Detected Port(s) :

Active Group Address   Tracking  Vers Mode Uptime    Expires    Ports/Vteps

--------------------- ---------- ---- ---- --------- ----------

------------------------------

225.1.1.1              Filter    3    EXC  51m 54s   4m 8s      2.2.2.2

                                                                1/3/8
225.1.1.2              Filter    3    EXC  51m 54s   4m 8s      2.2.2.2

                                                                1/3/8

225.1.1.3              Filter    3    EXC  51m 54s   4m 8s      2.2.2.2

                                                                1/3/8
In the output, Ports/Vteps ensures that querier is learnt through the static tunnel of Edge.

VSX deployment scenario

The following is the example of typical VSX deployment scenario:

Public

Multicast support over static VXLAN tunnels 1282

VSX is supported on the static VXLAN feature. Configuration and verification details for the devices in this
use case are provided in the following sections:

Subtopics

RR
Edge-1 (VSX primary)
Edge-1 (VSX secondary)
EDGE-2
Extended Edge

RR

Configuration

!export-password: default

Public

RR 1283

hostname SPINE

!

!

ssh server vrf mgmt

vlan 1

interface mgmt

    no shutdown

    ip static 192.168.1.1/24

interface 1/1/34

    no shutdown

    ip address 11.1.1.1/24

    ip ospf 10 area 0.0.0.0

interface 1/1/35

    no shutdown

    ip address 12.1.1.1/24

    ip ospf 10 area 0.0.0.0

interface 1/1/48

    no shutdown

    ip address 13.1.1.1/24

    ip ospf 10 area 0.0.0.0

interface loopback 1

    ip address 1.1.1.1/32

    ip ospf 10 area 0.0.0.0

!

!

!

!

!

router ospf 10

    router-id 1.1.1.1

    redistribute connected

    area 0.0.0.0
router bgp 65001

    bgp router-id 5.5.5.5

    neighbor 4.4.4.4 remote-as 65001

    neighbor 4.4.4.4 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 5.5.5.5 remote-as 65001

    neighbor 5.5.5.5 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 6.6.6.6 remote-as 65001

    neighbor 6.6.6.6 update-source loopback 1

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 route-reflector-client

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 send-community extended

        neighbor 6.6.6.6 send-community extended

    neighbor 1.1.1.1 update-source loopback 1

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 1.1.1.1 send-community extended

        neighbor 1.1.1.1 activate

    exit-address-family

!

clear ip mroute

Syntax

clear ip mroute

   all-vrfs

   group-prefix <group-prefix>

Public

Multicast VXLAN commands 1294

<group-address>[<source-address>]

   <port>

   vrf <vrf-name>

Description

Clears the multicast routing information for a specified VRF or all VRFs. If you do not include VRF
information in these commands, the clear ip mroute commands clear the default VRF.

Parameter

all‐vrfs

<group‐address>

   <source‐address>

group‐prefix <group‐prefix>

<port>

Description

Clears multicast routing for all VRFs.

Clears the multicast routing information for the group address i
n the specified VRF or all VRFs.

If the command does not include VRF information, it clears routi
ng information for the group address in the default VRF.

Clears multicast routing information for the group and source a
ddresses in the specified VRF or all VRFs.

If the command does not include VRF information, it clears routi
ng information for the group and source addresses in the defau
lt VRF.

Clears the multicast routing information for the group prefix in t
he specified VRF or all VRFs. The group prefix must be in the fo
rmat A.B.C.D/length.

If the command does not include VRF information, it clears routi
ng information for the group address in the default VRF.

Clears the multicast routing information for the port in the spec
ified VRF or all VRFs.

If the command does not include VRF information, it clears routi
ng information for the port in the default VRF.

vrf <VRF‐NAME>

Clears multicast routing information for a specific VRF.

Examples

Clears multicast routing information for the default VRF.

switch# clear ip mroute
Clears multicast routing information the group address 225.1.1.1 for the VRF Lab2.

switch# clear ip mroute 225.1.1.1 vrf Lab2
Clears multicast routing information the group address 225.1.1.1 and source address 192.0.2.6 for all VRFs

Public

clear ip mroute 1295

switch# clear ip mroute 225.1.1.1 192.0.2.6 all-vrfs
Clears multicast routing information for the port VLAN10 on the Default VRF.

clear ip mroute vlan10
Clears multicast routing information for the port VLAN10 for the group address 225.1.1.1 and source
address 192.0.2.6 for the VRF Lab3.

switch# clear ip mroute vlan20 225.1.1.1 192.0.2.6 Lab3

ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include

Syntax

ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include

no ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include

Description

Enables the router to forward multicast data received on the VXLAN L3VNI fabric to this interface,
regardless of whether a multicast join was received on this interface or not. This allows the interface to be in
the same multicast data path state on both the VSX peers.

This command must be enabled on the VSX VXLAN routers to support multicast receivers over ROP or P2P
SVI extensions. This command can be enabled on a transit L3 peering between VSX peers. This command is
optional when the uplink is an MCLAG SVI.

The no form of the command disables forwarding of multicast data on the interface.

NOTE

•  An IP address must be configured on the interface and  pim-sparse

must be enabled.

•  This command must be enabled only on one interface per VRF.

PIM enabled VXLAN VTEPs can be extended to other routers that can be connected to sources or clients.
The following types of L3 or L2 extensions are supported:

•  L2 VSX LAG: Upstream or downstream routers are connected using L2 VSX LAG links.

•  L3 VSX LAG: Upstream or downstream routers are connected using L3 VSX LAG links.

•  ROP extension: L3 extension for sources or clients using ROPs is supported.

•  Point-to-point SVI extension: L3 extension for sources or clients using point-to-point SVIs is supported.

If the source is connected using ROP or P2P SVIs, it is recommended to have an additional L3 link per VRF
between the VSX devices for upstream redundancy. If the L3 link is an SVI, it is recommended to not add an
ISL port in that VLAN.

Parameter

Description

datapath‐auto‐include

Includes the interface for multicast data forwarding.

Examples

Configuring interface 40.0.0.4/24 of the router to forward multicast data:

Public

ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include 1297

switch(config)# interface vlan40

switch(config-if-vlan)# ip address 40.0.0.4/24

switch(config-if-vlan)# ip pim-sparse enable

switch(config-if-vlan)# ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include

ts for this command.

ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

Syntax

ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

no ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

Public

ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor 1298

Description

Once configured, the router processes IGMP/MLD and PIM joins received on this interface regardless of its
DR or Prime Neighbor role. The command must be enabled for VSX VXLAN leaf switches for both L2 and L3
extensions. This allows for the interface to be in the same multicast data path state on both the VSX peers.

The no form of the command disables the vsx-virtual-neighbor on the interface.

NOTE

This command is applicable for normal SVI interfaces and L2 VNI mapped SVI
interfaces. It is valid for VXLAN-enabled VLANs only and has no effect on
non-VXLAN-enabled VLANs.

Examples

switch(config)# interface vlan40

switch(config-if-vlan)# ip address 40.0.0.4/24

switch(config-if-vlan)# ip pim-sparse enable

switch(config-if-vlan)# ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

 ipv6 pim6-sparse datapath-auto-include

Syntax

ipv6 pim6-sparse datapath-auto-include

no ipv6 pim6-sparse datapath-auto-include

Description

Enables the router to forward multicast data received on the VXLAN L3VNI fabric to this interface,
regardless of whether a multicast join was received on this interface or not. This allows the interface to be in
the same multicast data path state on both the VSX peers.

This command must be enabled on the VSX VXLAN routers to support multicast receivers over ROP or P2P
SVI extensions. This command can be enabled on a transit L3 peering between VSX peers. This command
should not be enabled when the uplink is an MCLAG SVI.

The no form of the command disables forwarding of multicast data on the interface.

NOTE

•  An IP address must be configured on the interface and  pim-sparse

must be enabled.

•  This command must be enabled only on one interface per VRF.

PIM enabled VXLAN VTEPs can be extended to other routers that can be connected to sources or clients.
The following types of L3 or L2 extensions are supported:

•  L2 VSX LAG: Upstream or downstream routers are connected using L2 VSX LAG links.

•  L3 VSX LAG: Upstream or downstream routers are connected using L3 VSX LAG links.

•  ROP extension: L3 extension for sources or clients using ROPs is supported.

•  Point-to-point SVI extension: L3 extension for sources or clients using point-to-point SVIs is supported.

Public

ipv6 pim6-sparse datapath-auto-include 1300

If the source is connected using ROP or P2P SVIs, it is recommended to have an additional L3 link per VRF
between the VSX devices for upstream redundancy. If the L3 link is an SVI, it is recommended to not add an
ISL port in that VLAN.

Parameter

Description

datapath‐auto‐include

Includes the interface for multicast data forwarding.

Examples

Configuring interface 40.0.0.4/24 of the router to forward multicast data:

switch(config)# interface vlan40

switch(config-if-vlan)# ipv6 address 40:40::4/64

switch(config-if-vlan)# ipv6 pim6-sparse enable

switch(config-if-vlan)# ipv6 pim6-sparse datapath-auto-include

no ipv6 pim6-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

Description

Once configured, the router processes IGMP/MLD and PIM joins received on this interface regardless of its
DR or Prime Neighbor role. The command must be enabled for VSX VXLAN leaf switches for both L2 and L3
extensions. This allows for the interface to be in the same multicast data path state on both the VSX peers.

The no form of the command disables the vsx-virtual-neighbor on the interface.

NOTE

This command is applicable for normal SVI interfaces and L2 VNI mapped SVI
interfaces. It is valid for VXLAN-enabled VLANs only and has no effect on
non-VXLAN-enabled VLANs.

Examples

switch(config)# interface vlan40

switch(config-if-vlan)# ipv6 address 40:40::4/64

switch(config-if-vlan)# ipv6 pim6-sparse enable

switch(config-if-vlan)# ipv6 pim6-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

multicast-replication-mode oism

Syntax

multicast-replication-mode oism

no multicast-replication-mode oism

Description

Allows inter-subnet IP multicast traffic to be routed among L2 VNIs. EVPN Optimized Inter-Subnet Multicast
(OISM) is a standard defined in RFC9625. In deployments using PIM over VXLAN, multicast routing is
achieved by exchanging PIM control packets over VXLAN tunnels.

Some of the problems with this deployment includes:

•

It can result in traffic hairpinning. Multicast traffic egressing from a PE router may return to the same
router on another VNI, due to routing being performed by the designated router (DR).

•

Interoperability with other vendors is not supported.

•  As the network scales, the number of PIM packets exchanged across the fabric increases significantly.

OISM eliminates these problems and provides optimal routing for inter-subnet multicast traffic. It does not
introduce any new route types and uses the RT6/IGMP-MLD proxy feature to exchange IGMP/MLD group
membership information.

Public

multicast-replication-mode oism 1303

Each Tenant Domain must be associated with a Supplementary Broadcast Domain (SBD), which behaves as a
control-plane BD without local ports. All PE routers in the VRF must have SBD configured, and IMET routes
are exchanged over SBD to support routed multicast traffic between PE routers.

The no form of this command disables the EVPN Optimized Inter-Subnet Multicast (OISM) functionality.

Examples

Enabling the EVPN Optimized Inter-Subnet Multicast (OISM) mode:

switch(config-evpn)# multicast-replication-mode oism
Disabling the EVPN Optimized Inter-Subnet Multicast (OISM) mode:

switch(config-evpn)# no multicast-replication-mode oism

Added support for 10040 Switch platforms.

register-source

Syntax

register-source <INTERFACE-NAME>

no register-source <INTERFACE-NAME>

Description

Specifies the source interface to be used for PIM registration in the case of VXLAN anycast interfaces. When
the PIM enabled anycast VLAN is directly connected to a multicast source, register-source is used to send
registration messages to the RP and this interface receives the register-stop messages from the RP.

The no form of this command removes the register source configuration.

Parameter

Description

Specifies the name of the interface to use.

<INTERFACE‐NAME>

Usage

•  This is a global configuration under router-pim configuration and is required in Symmetric IRB with

anycast IP address configuration.

•  This configuration is required in the source connected switch only when the PIM-DR and RP are in two

different switches.

•  Without this configuration, there will be traffic loss as the registration sequence will not be successful. It
is mandatory to have this source interface configured with a non-anycast IP address which is unique to
the VTEP, and with PIM enabled.

Examples

Configuring the source interface for PIM registrations:

switch# config
switch(config)# router pim vrf vrf1
switch(config-pim)# register-source loopback1

Removing the register-source configuration:

Public

register-source 1305

switch(config-pim)# no register-source loopback1

NOTE

For more information on features that use this command, refer to the Multicast
Guide for your switch model.

show evpn oism

Syntax

show evpn oism [all-vrfs | <VRF-NAME>]

Description

Displays the status and configuration details of OISM in the EVPN network.

Parameter

<VRF-NAME>

[all-vrfs]

Examples

Description

Specifies the VRF.

Specifies all VRFs.

Displays the status and configuration details of OISM for all VRFs:

switch# show evpn oism all-vrfs

OISM Global Status : Enabled

VRF                : blue

SBD                : 100

Gateway BD         : vlan10

VRF                : green

SBD                : 200

Gateway BD         : vlan20
Displays the status and configuration details of OISM for vrf red:

switch# show evpn oism vrf red

OISM Global Status   : Disabled

VRF                  : red

SBD                  : Not configured
Gateway BD           : Not configured

NOTE

For more information on features that use this command, refer to the VXLAN
Guide for your switch model.

show ip mroute

Syntax

show ip mroute [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer]

Description

Shows multicast routing information. Optionally, you can show specific information by VRF. If no options are
specified, it shows information for the default VRF.

Public

show ip mroute 1308

Parameter

all‐vrfs

Description

Shows all PIM neighbors information.

vrf <VRF‐NAME>

Shows PIM neighbor information for a specific VRF.

vsx‐peer

Examples

Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do
not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f
rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai
lable on switches that support VSX.

Multicast route with L3VNI in Incoming Interface List:

switch# show ip mroute all-vrfs

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : red

Total number of entries : 1

Group Address           : 225.1.1.1

Source Address          : 80.1.1.11

Neighbor                : 1.1.1.1

Incoming interface      : vni2

Outgoing Interface List :

Interface       State

-----------     ----------

vlan10          forwarding

switch# show ip mroute 225.1.1.1 80.1.1.11 all-vrfs

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : red

Group Address                 : 225.1.1.1

Source Address                : 80.1.1.11
Neighbor                      : 1.1.1.1

Incoming interface            : vni2

Multicast Routing Protocol    : PIM-SM

Unicast Routing Protocol      : BGP

Metric                        : 0

Metric  Pref                  : 200

Uptime (HH:MM:SS)             : 00:07:23

Downstream Interface

Interface       State

-----------     ----------

Public

show ip mroute 1309

vni2            forwarding

vni2            forwarding
Multicast route with L3VNI in Outgoing Interface List:

switch# show ip mroute all-vrfs

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : red

Total number of entries : 1

Group Address           : 225.1.1.1

Source Address          : 80.1.1.11

Neighbor                :

Incoming interface      : vlan20

Outgoing Interface List :

Interface       State

-----------     ----------

vni2               forwarding

switch# show ip mroute 225.1.1.1 80.1.1.11 vrf red

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : red

Total number of entries : 1

Group Address                 : 225.1.1.1

Source Address                : 80.1.1.11

Neighbor                      :

Incoming interface            : vlan20

Multicast Routing Protocol    : PIM-SM

Metric                        : 0

Downstream Interface

Interface       State

-----------     ----------

vni2               forwarding
Detailed multicast route that displays the individual VTEPs to which the packet is forwarded:

switch# show ip mroute 239.2.2.2 100.2.1.4 vrf red detail

VRF : red

Group Address           : 239.2.2.2

show ip pim neighbor

Public

show ip pim neighbor 1311

Syntax

show ip pim neighbor [<IP-ADDR>] [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>]

show ip pim neighbor [<BRIEF>] [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>]

Description

Displays the information about PIM interfaces currently configured in the router for the given VRF. If VRF is
not given, it displays for default VRF.

Parameter

Description

Shows PIM neighbor information.

<IP‐ADDR>

<BRIEF>

all‐vrfs

Shows brief PIM neighbor information.

Shows all PIM neighbors information

vrf <VRF‐NAME>

Shows PIM neighbor information for a specific VRF.

Examples

Show information for all VRFs:

switch# show ip pim neighbor all-vrfs

PIM Neighbor

VRF                       : Test_1

Total number of neighbors : 2

IP Address                : 100.1.1.252

Interface                 : vlan100

Up Time (HH:MM:SS)        : 00:44:38
Expire Time (HH:MM:SS)    : 00:01:32

DR Priority               : 1

Hold Time (HH:MM:SS)      : 00:01:45

IP Address                : 172.1.1.1

Interface                 : vni1000

Up Time (HH:MM:SS)        : 00:44:35

Expire Time (HH:MM:SS)    : 00:03:25

DR Priority               : 1

Hold Time (HH:MM:SS)      : 00:03:30
PIM supports both IPv4 and IPv6 as underlay VTEP IP addresses. In deployments with an IPv6 underlay, PIM
forms an auto-generated link-local address to exchange control packets and forms a PIM neighborship over
an L3VNI interface with other VTEPs. The auto generated IP will be a link local IP in case of overlay IPv6.

Public

show ip pim neighbor 1312

The following example displays L3VNI neighbors for an IPv6 underlay tunnel with an IPv4 overlay.

switch# show ip pim neighbor all-vrfs

PIM Neighbor

VRF                       : Test_1

Total number of neighbors : 1

IP Address                : 169.254.125.33

Interface                 : vni1000

Up Time (HH:MM:SS)        : 00:44:35

Expire Time (HH:MM:SS)    : 00:03:25

DR Priority               : 1

Hold Time (HH:MM:SS)      : 00:03:30
In deployments with an IPv4 overlay, multicast route entries with an incoming L3VNI interface will have a
neighbor ip address that is an auto-generated IPv4 address derived from the underlay V6 tunnel address,

switch# show ip mroute 230.1.1.1 vrf Test_1 detail

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : Test_1

Total number of entries : 1

Group Address                 : 230.1.1.1

Source Address                : 40.40.1.100

Neighbor                      : 169.254.125.33

Incoming interface            : vni1000

Unicast Routing Protocol      : BGP

Metric                        : 0

Metric  Pref                  : 200

Uptime (HH:MM:SS)             : 00:01:45

Downstream Interface

Interface       State

-----------     ----------

vlan20          forwarding
Show brief information for PIM neighbor:

switch# show ip pim neighbor brief

----------------------------------------------------------------------------

---------------

VRF: default                 Total number of neighbor : 2

----------------------------------------------------------------------------

---------------

Interface     Neighbor    Uptime       Expires       DR       Hold Time

Secondary Address

      (IPV4)     (HH:MM:SS)   (HH:MM:SS)   Priority

(HH:MM:SS)    (IPV4)

show ipv6 mroute

Public

show ipv6 mroute 1314

Syntax

show ipv6 mroute [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer]

Description

Shows multicast routing information. Optionally, you can show specific information by VRF. If no options are
specified, it shows information for the default VRF.

Parameter

all‐vrfs

Description

Shows all PIM neighbors information.

vrf <VRF‐NAME>

Shows PIM neighbor information for a specific VRF.

vsx‐peer

Examples

Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do
not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f
rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai
lable on switches that support VSX.

Showing multicast route with L3VNI in Incoming Interface List:

switch# show ipv6 mroute all-vrfs

show ipv6 pim6 interface vlan

Syntax

show ipv6 pim6 interface vlan <vlan name>

Description

Displays the IPV6 information about PIM6 interfaces currently configured in the router for the given VLAN.
If the VLAN name is not specified, the output of this command displays information for the default VLAN.

Parameter

vlan <vlan name>

Description

Specifies the VLAN.

show ipv6 pim6 neighbor

Syntax

show ipv6 pim6 neighbor [<IP-ADDR>] [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>]

Description

Displays the information about PIM interfaces currently configured in the router for the given VRF. If VRF is
not given, it displays for default VRF.

NOTE

The overlay IPv6 address of the LRVNI is an autogenerated link local IP that is
derived by PIM using the VXLAN source IP.

Parameter

Description

Shows PIM neighbor information.

<IP‐ADDR>

all‐vrfs

Shows all PIM neighbors information

vrf <VRF‐NAME>

Shows PIM neighbor information for a specific VRF.

Examples

Show information for all VRFs:

switch# show ipv6 pim6 neighbor all-vrfs

PIM Neighbor

VRF                       : red

Total number of neighbors : 2

IPv6 Address              : fe80::5:5:5:5

Interface                 : vni10000

Up Time (HH:MM:SS)        : 06:57:307

Expire Time (HH:MM:SS)    : 00:03:26

DR Priority               : 1

Hold Time (HH:MM:SS)      : 00:03:30

IPv6 Address              : fe80::3281:c780:a5c:18c0

Interface                 : vlan10

Public

Expire Time (HH:MM:SS)    : 00:01:29

DR Priority               : 1

Hold Time (HH:MM:SS)      : 00:01:45

Secondary IP Addresses    : 100:100::3
PIM supports both IPv4 and IPv6 as underlay VTEP IP addresses. If the outgoing interface is a L3VNI, the
forwarded VTEP IP address will be displayed as the actual IPv6 underlay tunnel source IP address.

switch# show ipv6 mroute 230.1.1.1 vrf Test_1 detail

displays multicast routes for an IPv6 underlay tunnel with an IPV6 overlay.

switch# show ipv6 mroute ff55::1 vrf Test_1 detail

supplementary-broadcast-domain

Syntax

supplementary-broadcast-domain

no supplementary-broadcast-domain

Description

Configures a given VLAN as a Supplementary Broadcast Domain (SBD). In a VXLAN-EVPN network, only
one VLAN per VRF can be configured as an SBD. EVPN Route Type-6 joins on the client VLAN are
translated to the SDB VLAN, and the remote VTEP learns the join on the SDB VLAN.

The no form of this command removes VLAN from its role as the Supplementary Broadcast Domain (SBD).

NOTE
Only one SBD can be configured per VRF.

Public

supplementary-broadcast-domain 1323

Usage

•  A dedicated BD should be configured as the OISM SBD, and it must be configured on all PE routers in

the EVPN domain.

•  The OISM SBD should not include any local ports. However, in VSX deployments, the ISL port should be

included to synchronize multicast flows.

•  or centralized routing use cases where access switches do not have SVIs, OISM is not required. PIM
should be enabled on the router to support routing. IGMP/MLD snooping and proxy are needed to
propagate joins via route type 6.

•  When OISM is enabled, there is no need to configure PIM on individual interfaces or use VxLAN

PIM-related commands such as vsx-virtual-neighbor or datapath-auto-include.

•  PIM must still be enabled globally per VRF to support multicast source address resolution and mroute

formation.

Examples

Configuring VLAN 10 as a Supplementary Broadcast Domain (SBD):

switch(config-evpn)# vlan 10

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# supplementary-broadcast-domain
Removing the VLAN as the Supplementary Broadcast Domain (SBD):

switch(config-evpn-vlan-10)# no supplementary-broadcast-domain

IGMP/MLD

In a VXLAN deployment, enabling IGMP/MLD snooping prevents flooding of unknown multicast packets to
all the VTEPs in the L2VNI. AHQ from the querier is sent to all the VTEPs in the L2VNI along with the
regular ports in the associated VLAN. Additionally, IGMP/MLD Membership Reports from local hosts are
forwarded to all the VTEPs in the L2VNI.

In a situation where a VSX pair is acting like a VTEP, IGMP/MLD Membership Reports from remote VTEPS
are synchronized among the VSX peers. Both the VSX peers learn the join on the same VTEP. In order to
achieve this, redistribute local-svi or redistribute local-mac commands must be enabled under the EVPN
context. When running VSX on a pair of switches, only one device gets the packet on the VTEP. IGMP/MLD
snooping should be enabled on all devices in the fabric, otherwise all multicast traffic is flooded.

NOTE

If the VTEP does not have any IGMP/MLD configurations, VXLAN encapsulated
packets are copied to the CPU even though there is no IGMP/MLD configuration;
the packets are forwarded by the software to the other ports.

In a VXLAN-enabled topology where there are tunnel endpoints configured on
different platforms, the scale supported are the lowest value among the different
platforms. This is because the joins are synced across the Tunnel Endpoints.

PIM-SM

In a VXLAN deployment, when PIM is configured on the tenant VRF, PIM is automatically enabled on the
L3VNI interface on that VRF. This interface is used to route multicast traffic across tunnels in a symmetric

Public

IGMP/MLD 1325

IRB network. PIM neighbors are learned on this interface and the remote VTEP neighbors are listed under
this interface. Multicast traffic is forwarded through the same L3VNI interface to reach other VTEP clients.
PIM neighbors formed across L3VNI can be seen using show ip pim neighbor or show ipv6 pim6 neighbor.
For a L3 VNI neighbor, neighbor IP will be the underlay VTEP IP in the default VRF and this will be same
for all the tenant VRFs. The output of show ip mroute or show ipv6 mroute displays the multicast routes
ingressing and egressing on L3VNI interfaces.

PIM uses the VXLAN source IP as the DR priority and it overrides the default DR priority. It is recommended
not to configure DR priorities. However, if you must configure the priority, the DR priority should be unique
in each of the leaf nodes. For a VSX VTEP, the VSX peers should have the same DR priority.

NOTE

•  PIM-DM with VXLAN is not currently supported.

•  PIM-SM VxLAN with the MSDP is not supported.

•  Non-Anycast RP can be configured on extended L3-Router or on VxLAN

VTEPs including VSX devices.

•  MTrace in a PIM-SM VxLAN deployment is not currently supported.

•  PIM active-active is not applicable for Multicast over VXLAN.

For IPv6, PIM auto-generates overlay IPv6 address from the underlay IPv4 tunnel source IP address. This
Auto-generated IP is used to form neighborship between L3VNI interfaces. Similarly, show commands for
mroute displays the multicast routes ingressing/egressing on L3VNI interfaces. The auto generated IP is
a link local IP. For example, if the tunnel source IP address is configured as 10.122.189.11, then the
auto-generated overlay IPv6 address would be fe80::10:122:189:11.

PIM-SSM

PIM-SSM over VXLAN supports the groups within SSM range addresses.The SSM range addresses can be a
default SSM group address range or can be defined using SSM range ACL.

PIM-SSM supports the legacy IGMPv1/IGMPv2/MLD1 clients using SSM-Map. PIM-SSM over VxLAN is
supported for IPv4 group address ranges.

PIM-SSM with Multifabric is supported for IPv4 address ranges. PIM-SSM with Multifabric supports the
legacy clients (i.e IGMPv1/IGMPv2/MLDv1) using SSM-Map. Any changes in PIM-SSM configurations clear
all internal states and Mroutes associated with L3VNI interface. This results in a short period of traffic loss
until PIM rebuilds the states.

Refer to Protocol Independent Multicast - Source‑Specific Multicast for more information and related
commands.

Public

PIM-SSM 1326

Recommendations for PIM-SSM over VXLAN

Enable the PIM-SSM on the PIM-DR devices and client connected devices.

Configure PIM-SSM Map on PIM-DR, client connected VTEPs, or all devices where L2 VNI is extended.

Limitations

PIM-SSM is not supported with IPv6 underlay for PIM-SSM over VXLAN.

PIM-SSM is not supported with IPv6 mutlicast address ranges on Overlay.

Mroute output for SSM over VXLAN

All multicast route entries that carry PIM-SSM in the Multicast Routing Protocol field rerpresent an SSM
mroute.

This example shows an SSM mroute with incoming interface as L3VNI:

leaf1# show ip mroute 232.0.0.1 100.1.1.10 vrf red

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : red

Group Address                 : 232.0.0.1

Source Address                : 100.1.1.10

Neighbor                      : 3.3.3.3

Incoming interface            : vni10000

Multicast Routing Protocol    : PIM-SSM

Unicast Routing Protocol      : BGP

Metric                        : 0

Metric  Pref                  : 200

Uptime (HH:MM:SS)             : 00:00:11

Downstream Interface

Interface       State

-----------     ----------

vlan20          forwarding
This example shows an SSM mroute with incoming interface as L2VNI:

leaf1# show ip mroute 232.1.2.1 11.10.11.2 vrf red

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : red

Group Address                 : 232.1.2.1

Source Address                : 11.10.11.2

Neighbor                      : 11.4.11.1

Incoming interface            : vlan21

Multicast Routing Protocol    : PIM-SSM

Unicast Routing Protocol      : BGP

Metric                        : 0

Metric  Pref                  : 200

Uptime (HH:MM:SS)             : 01:23:26

Public

PIM-SSM 1327

Downstream Interface

Interface       State

-----------     ----------

vlan11          forwarding

PIM-SSM Scenario

Configuration and verification details for a PIM-SSM scenario with configurations for PIM-SSM over VXLAN
with Symmetric IRB routing in a single fabric are provided in the following sections:

Core VSX primary

vlan 21

ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 23

ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 26

ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 28

ip igmp snooping enable

virtual-mac 00:11:11:00:00:22

Public

VTEP 2 1354

evpn

redistribute local-svi

vlan 21

rd 192.168.0.102:16021

route-target export 65535:16021

route-target import 65535:16021

Debugging and troubleshooting

Debugging and troubleshooting

The recommended multicast VXLAN troubleshooting flow for overlay IPv4 and IPv6 is provided in Figure 1.
Additional tips and guidance for each step are provided below.

NOTE

In cases of IPv6 overlay, use the IPv6 version of the commands. Examples in this
section show the IPv4 version of commands.

Figure 1. Recommended multicast VXLAN troubleshooting flow

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1358

The recommended IPv4 multicast VXLAN troubleshooting procedure includes the following sequence of
steps:

Step 1: Check underlay network reachability and VXLAN tunnels between VTEPs.

1.  Ensure tunnel source/destination loopback IPs are correctly advertised in the underlay network.

2.  Use extended pings on VTEPs to confirm network connectivity between tunnel source/destination

loopback IPs.

3.  Fix underlay connectivity issues if discovered.

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1359

4.

If there are no underlay network issues, validate VXLAN tunnels, ensure they are operational.
VTEP1# sh int vx vtep

Source        Destination      Origin   Status          VNI

Routing   VLAN  VRF

------------- ---------------- -------- --------------- ---------

--------- ----- ---

192.168.2.6   192.168.2.5      evpn     operational     100001

enabled   --     VRF1

192.168.2.6   192.168.2.5      evpn     operational     100111

disabled  111    --

192.168.2.6   192.168.11.3     evpn     operational     100001

enabled   --     VRF1

192.168.2.6   192.168.11.3     evpn     operational     100002

enabled   --     VRF2

5.

If EVPN tunnel is down, ensure correct EVPN configs are used.

Step 2: Validate multicast specific configurations.

1.

In a distributed L3 gateway deployment,  virtual-mac  configuration is needed on all the VTEPs.
Make sure  virtual-mac  is configured and it is unique per VTEP, a VSX VTEP peer would share 1
virtual-mac:
VTEP1# show run | i virtual-mac

virtual-mac 00:00:22:00:00:21

2.  On VSX VTEPs,  ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor  is required on all SVIs where PIM

is enabled.
interface vlan40

 no shutdown

 vrf attach red

 ip address 40.40.40.1/24

 ip pim-sparse enable

 ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

3.

If IGMP or MLD is enabled on any VLAN, the  redistribute local-svi  or  redistribut
e local-mac  command must be enabled under the evpn context. This is needed for proper querier
information propagation.

4.  Validate unicast reachability to the multicast sources/DR/RP/BSR addresses across all the VTEPs. Use  s

how ip route  command to validate the routes. For example:

VTEP1# show ip route 80.1.1.1 vrf red
VRF: red

   Prefix           : 80.1.1.0/24                 VRF(egress)        : -

   Nexthop          : 11.1.1.1                    Interface          : -

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1360

Origin           : bgp                         Type               :

bgp_evpn

   Distance         : 200                         Metric             : 0

   Age              : 02h:08m:19s                 Tag                : 0

   Encap Type       : vxlan                       Encap Details      :

l3vni 2

Step 3: Check PIM neighbors.

1.  Ensure that PIM neighbors are formed between L3 VNI interfaces. For a L3 VNI neighbor, neighbor IP

will be the underlay VTEP IP in the default VRF and this will be same for all the tenant VRFs.

2.

If the same VLAN is extended across VTEPs, ensure that the PIM neighbors are formed on the extended
VLANs across the VTEPs.
VTEP1# show ip pim neighbor all-vrfs

PIM Neighbor

VRF : red

Total number of neighbors : 2

IP Address : 1.1.1.1

Interface : vni2

Up Time (HH:MM:SS) : 00:33:30

Expire Time (HH:MM:SS) : 00:03:01

DR Priority : 1

Hold Time (HH:MM:SS) : 00:03:30

IP Address : 100.100.1.3

Interface : vlan10

Up Time (HH:MM:SS) : 00:33:29

Expire Time (HH:MM:SS) : 00:01:17

DR Priority : 16843009

Hold Time (HH:MM:SS) : 00:01:45

3.

4.

If PIM neighbors are not formed, validate the configurations and ensure  ip pim-sparse  is enabled
on the interface and  router pim  is enabled on the corresponding VRF.

If the configurations are correct, validate PIM Hello packets are exchanged between the VTEPs. See Step
8: Check multicast packet exchanges between VTEPs for instructions.

5.  Validate PIM-SSM configurations are correct on LHR, PIM DR of client VLAN, transit routers, and FHR.

Step 4: Check PIM interface states and DR role.

1.  Validate PIM Interface configurations using the  show ip pim interface  command. Ensure

that the DR election is successful and updated in the show command.

2.

If the VLAN is extended across VTEPs. ensure only one VTEP is elected as DR on the VLAN. If the VTEP
is a pair of VSX switches, both VSX peers will be elected as DR. Only one VSX peer will route the data.

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1361

VTEP1# show ip pim interface vlan 10

PIM Interfaces

VRF: red

Interface : vlan10

Neighbor count : 1

IP Address : 100.100.1.3/24

Mode : sparse

Designated Router : 100.100.1.4

Proxy DR : false

Hello Interval (sec) : 30

Hello Delay (sec) : 5

Override Interval (msec) : 2500 Lan Prune Delay : Yes

Propagation Delay (msec) : 500 Configured DR Priority : 1

Operational DR Priority : 16843009

Neighbor Timeout : 93

Step 5: Check PIM BSR and RP-SET information.

NOTE

This step is not required for PIM-SSM groups when PIM-SSM is enabled.

1.

If BSR is configured, ensure that E-BSR is learnt on all the VTEPs.
VTEP1# show ip pim bsr

Status and Counters- PIM-SM Bootstrap Router Information

VRF                       : default

E-BSR Address             : 172.1.1.1

E-BSR Priority            : 0

E-BSR Hash Mask Length    : 30

E-BSR Up Time             : 49 secs

Next Bootstrap Message    : 21 secs

C-BSR Admin Status        : This system is a Candidate-BSR

C-BSR Address             : 172.1.1.1

C-BSR Priority            : 0

C-BSR Hash Mask Length    : 30

C-BSR Message Interval    : 60

C-BSR Source IP Interface : loopback1

C-RP Admin Status         : This system is a Candidate-RP

C-RP Address              : 172.1.1.1

C-RP Hold Time            : 150

C-RP Advertise Period     : 60

C-RP Priority             : 192

C-RP Source IP Interface  : loopback1

Group Address    Group Mask

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1362

---------------  ---------------

224.0.0.0        240.0.0.0

2.  Ensure RP-set is learned on all the VTEPs. If the RP is a static-RP, make sure all the VTEPs have the

static RP configured.
VTEP1# show ip pim rp-set all-vrfs

VRF: red

Status and Counters - PIM-SM Learned RP-Set Information

Group Address    Group Mask       RP Address       Hold Time  Expire Time

---------------  ---------------  ---------------  ---------  -----------

224.0.0.0        240.0.0.0        172.1.1.1        150        94

3.

If the BSR or RP-set is not learned, make sure of the unicast reachability to BSR/RP IP address. The
show ip route command should display unicast routes to reach BSR/RP address.

4.  Ensure that PIM is enabled on the next-hop pointed by the unicast route. If PIM is not enabled,

E-BSR/RP-Set information will not be updated correctly.

5.

If the unicast routes are present and PIM is enabled on the nexthop interface, check packet captures to
validate that BSR bootstrap packets and candidate RP advertisement messages are exchanged across
VTEPs.

Step 6: Check IGMP or MLD joins across VTEPs.

1.  Ensure IGMP or MLD joins are present on the DR in the client VLAN.

VTEP1# show ip igmp interface vlan 10

VRF Name   : red

Interface  : vlan10

IGMP Configured Version  : 3

IGMP Operating Version   : 3

Querier State              : Querier

Querier IP [this switch]   : 100.100.1.2

Querier Uptime             : 2m 29s

Querier Expiration Time    : 0m 41s
IGMP Snoop Enabled on VLAN : False

Active Group Address   Vers Mode Uptime    Expires

---------------------- ---- ---- --------- ---------

239.1.1.1              2         0m 17s    4m 3s

2.

If IGMP or MLD snooping is enabled, ensure that the joins are learned on the correct VTEP/port.
VTEP1# show ip igmp snooping vlan 10 group 239.1.1.1

IGMP ports and group information for group 239.1.1.1
VLAN ID   : 10

VLAN Name : VLAN10

Group Address : 239.1.1.1

Last Reporter : 200.200.1.1

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1363

Group Type    : Filter

                                                       V1

V2        Sources   Sources

Port                     Vers Mode Uptime    Expires   Timer

Timer     Forwarded Blocked

------------------------ ---- ---- --------- --------- ---------

--------- --------- --------

vxlan1(1.1.1.1)          2    EXC  5h 24m    3m 38s              3m

38s    0         0

3.

If joins are not shown, make sure that the querier is elected successfully in the interface and the periodic
AHQ packet reaches the clients.

4.  Ensure joins are refreshed periodically and are not removed. Check uptime in show ip igmp or show

ipv6 mld and show ip igmp snooping and show ipv6 mld snooping outputs to ensure joins are stable.

5.  On a VSX VTEP, IGMP or MLD joins are synchronized amongst the VSX switches. If IGMP or MLD

snooping is enabled, make sure that the joined VTEP is the same in both VSX peers. Similarly querier
port should be the same across both VSX peers. If they are different, ensure that the redistribute
local-svi or redistribute local-mac command is configured on all VTEPS.

6.  Make sure there are no COPP drops for IGMP or MLD class.

VTEP1# show copp-policy statistics class igmp

Statistics for CoPP policy 'default':

Class: igmp

Description: Internet Group Management Protocol.

priority : 4

rate (pps) : 1600

burst size (pkts) : 450

packets passed : 294267766 packets dropped : 0

Step 6a: Check IGMP joins across VTEPs with PIM-SSM

1.  Ensure IGMP joins are present on the DR in the client VLAN.

VTEP1# show ip igmp interface vlan 10

VRF Name : VRF_1

Interface : vlan11

IGMP Configured Version : 3

IGMP Operating Version : 3

Querier State : Querier

Querier IP [this switch] : 100.100.1.2
Querier Uptime : 2m 29s

Querier Expiration Time : 0m 41s

IGMP Snoop Enabled on VLAN : False

Active Group Address   Vers  Mode  Uptime    Expires

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1364

---------------------- ----  ----  --------- --------

232.1.1.1              3           0m 17s    4m 3s

2.  Ensure IGMP joins are present on the DR in the client VLAN.

VTEP1# show ip igmp snooping vlan 141 group 232.15.0.1

IGMP ports and group information for group 232.15.0.1

VLAN ID   : 141

VLAN Name : VLAN141

Group Address : 232.15.0.1

Last Reporter : 11.1.41.50

Group Type    : Filter

V1        V2        Sources   Sources

Port            Vers Mode Uptime   Expires  Timer    Timer Forwarded

Blocked

------------------------ --------  -------- -------- ----- ---------

-------

vxlan1(4.4.4.4) 3    INC  53m 25s  3m 34s   1        0

Group Address  : 232.15.0.1

Source Address : 60.1.25.100

Source Type    : Filter

Port                     Mode Uptime    Expires   Configured Mode

------------------------ ---- --------- --------- ----------------

vxlan1(4.4.4.4)          INC  53m 25s   3m 34s    Auto

3.

4.

If joins are not shown, make sure that the querier is elected successfully in the interface and the periodic
AHQ packet reaches the clients.

In order for PIM traffic to correctly flow down the multicast tree for SSM groups, IGMPv3 joins should be
seen on LHR and PIM DR for the client vlan. Verify that source sent in IGMPv3 joins matches with the
actual source IP.

5.  Ensure IGMP PIM-SSM Map ACL is applied on interface for multicast groups where joins are sent by

legacy IGMPv1/IGMPv2 clients. Ensure configuration are applied on all VTEPs where L2VNI is extended.
Ensure IGMP SSM Map ACL range does not include multicast groups with IGMPv3 joins.

6.  Ensure joins are refreshed periodically and are not removed. Check uptime in show ip igmp or show ip

igmp snooping outputs to ensure joins are stable.

7.  On a VSX VTEP, IGMP joins are synchronized amongst the VSX switches. If IGMP snooping is enabled,
make sure that the joined VTEP is the same in both VSX peers. Similarly querier port should be the
same across both VSX peers. If they are different, ensure that the redistribute local-SVI or redistribute
local-mac command is configured on all VTEPS.

8.  Ensure there are no COPP drops for IGMP class.

VTEP1# show copp-policy statistics class igmp

Statistics for CoPP policy 'default':

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1365

Class: igmp

Description: Internet Group Management Protocol.

priority : 4

rate (pps) : 1600

burst size (pkts) : 450

packets passed : 294267766 packets dropped : 0

Step 7: Check multicast routes across VTEPs.

1.  Ensure multicast routes are formed correctly across VTEPS. Make sure L3 VNI interface is added

correctly in the incoming/outgoing interface list in a distributed L3 gateway use case.

•  Mroutes on Source DR (routing to L3 VNI Interface):

VTEP1# show ip mroute all-vrfs

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : red

Total number of entries : 1

Group Address           : 225.1.1.1

Source Address          : 73.1.1.11

Neighbor                : 100.100.1.3

Incoming interface      : vlan10

Outgoing Interface List :

Interface       State

-----------     ----------

vni2               forwarding

•  Mroutes on last hop router (routing from L3 VNI interface to receiver SVI):

VTEP2# show ip mroute all-vrfs

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : red

Total number of entries : 1

Group Address           : 225.1.1.1
Source Address          : 73.1.1.11

Neighbor                : 5.5.5.5

Incoming interface      : vni2

Outgoing Interface List :

Interface       State

-----------     ----------

vlan20          forwarding

2.

In the centralized L3 gateway use case, ensure multicast routes are formed correctly on the routers. In
this case the multicast routes will have SVI/ROP/L3 LAG as incoming/outgoing interfaces.

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1366

3.

If the outgoing interface is SVI and it contains VTEPs, multicast data traffic will be encapsulated and
transmitted to the VTEP.
L3VTEP# show ip mroute all-vrfs

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : red

Total number of entries : 1

Group Address           : 239.1.1.1

Source Address          : 80.1.1.11

Neighbor                :

Incoming interface      : vlan20

Outgoing Interface List :

Interface       State

-----------     ----------

vlan30               forwarding

4.

If the multicast routes are not formed as expected, there will be traffic loss. If the interface is missing in
multicast routes or the mroute is in bridge state (no outgoing interfaces):

•  On the client VLAN, ensure IGMP or MLD joins are present on the DR.

•  Validate unicast reachability to RP and multicast source on the last hop router (LHR).

•  Validate IGMP or MLD joins are processed by PIM using the show ip pim pendingor show ipv6
pim6 pending command. If the multicast data is seen by the router, there are not any pending
entries.

•

•

If the joins are seen, check the packet captures between LHR to ensure PIM *,G joins are sent
towards RP.

If RP has received the *,G join, check the packet captures between RP and Source DR to ensure (S,G)
joins are sent towards the source.

•  Once LHR receives the data via RPT, ensure that the LHR is sending (S,G) joins towards source via

packet captures.

5.

If the multicast routes is not present in show ip mroute in the source DR:

•  Ensure that the flow is active.

•

If the flow is active, but mroutes are not shown, it indicates an issue in the PIM registration path.

•  Make sure Source DR can reach RP and RP can reach back to the source DR.

•  Check that the virtual-mac is configured correctly, this is needed for Register/Register-Stop packets

to be processed correctly.

6.

If the clients are seeing duplicate traffic:

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1367

•  Ensure the vsx-virtual-neighbor command is enabled on all the VSX routers.

•

In a VSX setup, only one of the routers is responsible for routing the traffic. Ensure this by
monitoring interface statistics/packet captures.

Step 7a: Check multicast routes across VTEPs with PIM-SSM.

1.  Ensure multicast routes are formed correctly across VTEPS. Make sure L3 VNI interface is added

correctly in the incoming/outgoing interface list in a distributed L3 gateway use case. Mroutes on Source
DR (routing to L3 VNI Interface):
VTEP2# show ip mroute all-vrfs

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : VRF_1

Total number of entries : 1

Group Address           : 232.1.1.1

Source Address          : 192.168.171.10

SSM Mroute              : True

Neighbor                : 192.168.161.3

Uptime                  : 01:10:19

State                   : route

Incoming interface      : vlan4001

Outgoing Interface List :

Interface       State

-----------     ----------

vni10001        forwarding

Mroutes on last hop router (routing from L3 VNI interface to receiver SVI):

leaf1# show ip mroute 232.6.0.1 all-vrfs

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : VRF_1

Total number of entries : 1

Group Address           : 232.1.1.1

Source Address          : 192.168.171.10

SSM Mroute              : True

Neighbor                : 192.168.0.110

Uptime                  : 01:21:35

State                   : route

Incoming interface      : vni10001

Outgoing Interface List :

Interface       State

-----------     ----------

vlan21          forwarding
1.

In the centralized L3 gateway use case, ensure multicast routes are formed correctly on the routers. In
this case the multicast routes have SVI/ROP/L3 LAG as incoming/outgoing interfaces.

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1368

2.

If the outgoing interface is SVI and it contains VTEPs, multicast data traffic is encapsulated and
transmitted to the VTEP.
L3VTEP# show ip mroute all-vrfs

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : VRF_1

Total number of entries : 1

Group Address           : 232.2.3.5

Source Address          : 192.168.171.10

SSM Mroute              : True

Neighbor                : 192.168.161.3

Uptime                  : 00:16:30

State                   : route

Incoming interface      : vlan4001

Outgoing Interface List :

Interface       State

-----------     ----------

vlan51          forwarding

3.

If the multicast routes are not formed as expected, there is traffic loss. If the interface is missing in
multicast routes or the mroute is in bridge state (no outgoing interfaces):

•  On the client VLAN, ensure IGMP joins are present on the DR.

•  Validate unicast reachability to multicast source on the last hop router (LHR).

•  Validate IGMP joins are processed by PIM using the  show ip pim pending  command. If the

multicast data is seen by the router, there are not any pending entries.

•

If the joins are seen, check the packet captures between LHR and upstream device to ensure PIM S,G
joins are sent towards source.

•  Check the packet captures to ensure (S,G) joins are received on source.

4.

If the multicast routes is not present in show ip mroute in the source DR:

•  Ensure that the flow is active.

•  Make sure Source DR can reach client DR.

5.

If the clients are seeing duplicate traffic:

•  Ensure the vsx-virtual-neighbor command is enabled on all the VSX routers.

•

In a VSX setup, only one of the routers is responsible for routing the traffic. Ensure this by
monitoring interface statistics/packet captures.

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1369

Step 8: Check multicast packet exchanges between VTEPs.

•  All the multicast packets (IGMP/MLD/PIM/PIM6 control packets, multicast data frames) exchanged

across the VTEPS are encapsulated with VXLAN.

•  The packets exchanged over VTEPs can be inspected by configuring a mirror session on non-VTEP
core/spine switches. The mirror destination should be a port where a packet capture is running.

l2:/home/admin# tcpdump -i 1

tcpdump: verbose output suppressed, use -v or -vv for full protocol decode

listening on MirrorRxNet, link-type EN10MB (Ethernet), capture size 262144

bytes

08:58:51.562444 IP 1.1.1.1.35569 > 3.3.3.3.4789: VXLAN, flags [I] (0x08),

vni 30 --> Encapsulated PIM Hello packet

IP 1.1.1.1 > 224.0.0.13: PIMv2, Hello, length 34

Subtopics

Debugging and troubleshooting of anycast RP

Debugging and troubleshooting of anycast RP

The recommended anycast RP troubleshooting procedure includes the following sequence of steps:

Step 1: Verify whether the peer state is up. For that the peer must be enabled and there must be a
unicast path to peer IP.

switch# show ip msdp peer all-vrfs

VRF: default

MSDP Peer: 11.0.0.13

Connection status

State: up  Resets: 0  Connection Source: 1/1/1(10.1.1.2)
Uptime(Downtime): 0m 25s    SA Messages sent: 0

SA's learned from this peer: 0

Peer Keepalive interval: 70

Peer Hold time: 90

Peer Connection Retry interval: 40

SA Filtering

Input (S,G) filter: msdp_sa_filter1  (S,G) entries dropped: 0

Output (S,G) filter: msdp_sa_filter2  (S,G) entries dropped: 30

Mesh group: AnyRpMesh

SA Limit: 1000

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting of anycast RP 1370

Step 2: Verify SA-Cache to check if the flow is listed, Except for the RP near to the multicast source, all
other RP learn the source information via SA message exchange and stored in the SA-Cache.

switch# show ip msdp sa-cache all-vrfs

VRF: default

Total entries: 2

(60.1.1.2, 227.1.1.1)  RP: 11.0.0.1  Peer: 11.0.0.13

(60.1.1.2, 228.1.1.1)  RP: 11.0.0.1  Peer: 11.0.0.13

Step 3: Verify the active multicast flows that are registered by source DR to this RP. The anycast RP
that is near to the multicast source will have this entry updated and the remaining RP will have the
SA-Cache updated.

switch# show ip pim rp-registered-sources all-vrfs

Multicast flows registered with this RP

VRF : default

Total number of entries : 2

Source Address           Group Address           RP Address

--------------------     --------------------    --------------------

60.1.1.2                 227.1.1.1               11.0.0.1

60.1.1.2                 228.1.1.1               11.0.0.1

FAQ

1. Can I enable multicast on both the overlay (leaf/access VTEP) and underlay (core/spine non-VTEP)?

No, it should not be done and is not recommended. The solution will not work if PIM or IGMP is enabled on
the core/spine switches connected to VTEPs.

2. Does the solution require EVPN control plane?

Yes, the solution requires EVPN based control plane both for L2 and L3 multicast. Remote VTEPs are
learned via EVPN type 3. In addition, remote hosts and routes are learned via EVPN type 2 and EVPN type
5. IGMP and PIM protocols rely on this information to forward packets.

3. Is PIM-SM used in underlay for BUM traffic replication?

No, the solution does not use PIM-SM at underlay to replicate BUM traffic. The solution uses Head
End Replication to send multicast traffic to remote VTEPs, this means that each broadcast/multicast is
encapsulated and replicated to all VTEPs from the source VTEP. The list of VTEPs to be replicated can be
learned via EVPN type 3 or through multicast protocols running in the overlay.

NOTE
PIM-SM is not supported, but PIM-BDIR is supported.

Public

FAQ 1371

4. Are there are any platform differences to be aware of?

•  For the VSX VTEPs on the HPE Aruba Networking 8325, 8400, 9300, and 10000 Switch Series, when
a packet is routed from a SVI/L3VNI to a L3VNI/L2VNI, only the primary VSX VTEP forwards. This
prevents duplication of packets.

•  For the VSX VTEPs on the HPE Aruba Networking 6400, 8100, and 8360 Switch Series , whichever
device gets data directly (not via ISL) will forward. This is done because these platforms have a
predicate rule programmed that any packet over ISL will not be sent over tunnel. This means there will
never be a case when packets go over the VSX ISL and into a VXLAN tunnel.

5. Is the Active-Active command required on VSX VTEPs?

The Active-Active command is optional for VSX VTEPs. This is because VSX peers act as logical VTEPs and
work in Active-Active mode natively. There is no need to explicitly enable Active-Active on VSX VTEPS.

6. Why is the VSX virtual neighbor command required?

•  The VSX virtual neighbor command needs to be enabled on all PIM enabled SVI interfaces with
VSX-LAGs and L2 VNI mapped interfaces, the command once enabled does multiple things.

•

•

If the VLAN is the first hop connected router, then both the VSX VTEPs will become DR and will forward
PIM joins for every IGMP join that received.

If the VLAN is the not the first-hop connected router, then both the VSX VTEPs will ignore the prime
neighbor check and will forward PIM joins upstream.

7. Does PIM need to be enabled on the L3 VNI?

No, PIM-SM does not need to be enabled on the L3 VNI. It will be automatically enabled when PIM-SM is
enabled on the tenant VRF. A VTEP forms PIM neighbors with the other remote VTEPs over L3VNI. AOS-CX
forms automatic PIM-SM neighbors with peer VTEPs over L3VNI as soon as PIM-SM is enabled on the
tenant VRF.

8. Does the solution work for all types of underlay interfaces?

The solution work with all types of underlay interfaces that are currently supported in AOS-CX.

9. If I change the DR priority of my DR on an SVI with multicast VXLAN, will it work?

In a logical VSX VTEP, DR priority is automatically derived from the VTEP address. This ensures that the
VSX VTEPs have identical priorities with both of them functioning as DRs or as non DRs. In case DR priority
is changed, one of the VSX peer will start behaving differently from the other. To conclude, do not change DR
priority on SVIs of VSX VTEPs.

10. In a VSX VTEP, are VSX keepalive links mandatory for multicast VXLAN?

The VSX keepalive is not mandatory as private IP is used between VSX peers is used to sync joins received
over L3 VNIs

Public

FAQ 1372

11. Why do I require an additional L3 link between VSX border VTEPs when there is ROP/P2P SVI
extension?

The additional connectivity is required from the VSX border VTEP to provide an additional link in case the
main uplink connectivity from the primary VSX peer goes down. In general the rule that is programmed is
that only VSX primary VTEP will forward. If uplink connectivity from the primary VTEP goes down then PIM
joins from primary are sent to the secondary and the traffic is pulled from secondary towards the primary.

12. How do I identify an IPv6 PIM neighbor learned on a L3 VNI interface?

PIM auto-generates an overlay IPv6 address from the underlay IPv4 tunnel source IP address. This Auto-
generated IP is used to form neighborship between L3VNI interfaces. The auto generated IP is a link local IP.
For instance, if the VXLAN tunnel source IP is configured as 10.122.189.11, then the auto-generatedIPv6
overlay address is fe80::10:122:189:11. In other words, fe80::10:122:189:11 represents a VTEP neighbor
with a VXLAN tunnel source IP of 10.122.189.11.

EVPN-VXLAN Standard based Multicast

NOTE

The HPE Aruba Networking 5420. 6200, 8100, 8320, and 8400 Switch Series do
not support VXLAN.

IPv4 and IPv6 multicast forwarding for both L2 and L3 are supported with AOS-CX VXLAN/EVPN
deployments. Refer to the VXLAN Guide for more info on VXLAN/EVPN and overlays/underlays.

Protocol and feature details

AOS-CX supports efficient forwarding of multicast traffic in the VXLAN fabric. In VXLAN deployments,
multicast receivers and sources can be distributed across multiple VTEPs that are extending the VLAN
domain. RFC9251 outlines requirements for IGMP/MLD proxy along with support for new EVPN route types
to optimize the multicast control traffic over VxLAN tunnels. AOS-CX supports the following modes in the
overlay:

•  CX native mode using IGMP/MLD/PIM messages in the fabric.

•  Standard-based multicast with IGMP-MLD proxy.

This chapter focuses on standard-based multicast forwarding using the IGMP-MLD proxy feature. With this
support, multicast traffic is excluded from regular BUM flows and is instead forwarded based on receiver
membership reports learned via the EVPN control plane.

Subtopics

L2 multicast over VXLAN
L2 multicast over VXLAN enabled with IGMP-MLD proxy and IGMP or MLD snooping
IGMP-MLD proxy
Distributed querier
PIM routing

L2 multicast over VXLAN

By default, L2 multicast data streams are sent by the source-connected VTEP to all other remote VTEPs
interested in the same VNI, even if there are devices that are non-receivers (devices that are not interested
in that multicast stream).

Figure 1. L2 multicast over VXLAN with non-receiver

Public

Protocol and feature details 1374

L2 multicast over VXLAN enabled with IGMP-MLD proxy and IGMP
or MLD snooping

IGMP or MLD snooping can be configured at the VLAN level with L2 multicast over VXLAN tunnels, this
prevents the source connected VTEP from sending the multicast stream to VTEPs without any interested
receivers (non-receivers).

Figure 1. L2 multicast over VXLAN enabled with IGMP snooping

Public

L2 multicast over VXLAN enabled with IGMP-MLD prox... 1375

IGMP-MLD proxy

IGMP-MLD Proxy feature enables optimized Layer 2 multicast by using SMET routes (EVPN Route Type
6). When enabled alongside IGMP or MLD snooping, SMET routes are advertised instead of IGMP or MLD
messages, enhancing multicast efficiency. Enabling this feature switches the Layer 2 multicast operating
mode from CX's native mode (using IGMP/MLD messages) to standard-based SMET routes within the fabric.
IGMP/MLD snooping must be configured on all participating VTEPs in the multicast network. This feature
also automatically starts the multicast Querier functionality on all VNI-mapped VLANs in the VTEP’s, using
the SVI IP, if configured, or the globally configured Querier IP (ip igmp snooping querier-address) if no
SVI is present. Additionally, this feature triggers an IMET route update for all VNIs with the multicast flags
extended community, setting the IGMP and MLD proxy capabilities. This feature is enabled using EVPN
configuration of igmp-mld-proxy. Refer to the EVPN chapter in the VXLAN Guide for more information
about this configuration.

Distributed querier

With the IGMP-MLD Proxy feature, each VTEP initiates the querier functionality, forming a distributed
querier for all attached hosts within its respective network. This also enables the suppression of IGMP
Membership Reports and Membership Queries over the fabric. If the access VTEP hosts an SVI, the

Public

IGMP-MLD proxy 1376

IGMP querier functionality starts automatically when the IGMP-MLD Proxy is enabled. In the absence
of an SVI, such as in centralized routing scenarios, the configured querier address (ip igmp snooping
querier-address) is used.

PIM routing

PIM routing is supported with the IGMP-Proxy feature, and routing can be configured on the border leaf.
Border routers can be set up to form a Centralized Gateway Router (CGW). Additionally, Distributed Active
Gateway (AGW) is also supported with PIM routing on the Border VTEP. If the border leaf does not support
PIM routing, an external PIM router can be configured.

Configuration task list

The following are prerequisites for a campus topology with centralized gateway:

•  Single fabric VXLAN topology is up with ingress replication mode

•  Underlay OSPF

•  Overlay IBGP

•

Interface VLANs configured only on the core-VSX VTEP

•  Access VTEPs configured only with VLANs (no SVIs)

•  Enable IGMP/MLD snooping on all multicast enabled VLANs

•  PIM enabled only on core-VSX VTEP

•  L2VNIs extended on VTEps having multicast clients

•  All L2VNI tunnels are operational

Enable igmp-mld proxy under EVPN on all VTEPs.

evpn

igmp-mld-proxy

On core VTEP(CGW) create interface VLANs and enable IGMP/MLD and PIM.

vlan30

ip igmp snooping enable
ipv6 mld snooping enable

interface vlan 30

vrf attach vrf_emp

ip mtu 9198

Public

PIM routing 1377

ip address 10.101.128.1/24

active-gateway ip mac 00:00:00:00:00:30

active-gateway ip 10.101.128.1

ipv6 address 2000:101:80::1/64

active-gateway ipv6 mac 00:00:00:00:00:30

active-gateway ipv6 fe80::80

ip igmp enable

ipv6 mld enable

ip pim-sparse enable

ipv6 pim6-sparse enable

ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

ipv6 pim6-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor

Configure IGMP and MLD snooping and snooping querier address on access VTEPs. On access VTEPs, when
no SVIs are configured, it is required to configure a snooping querier address. In the case of distributed
anycast gateway deployments, the snooping querier address is optional and ip igmp can be enabled on the
SVI.

switch(config)# ip igmp snooping querier-address 1.1.1.9

switch(config)# ipv6 mld snooping querier-address fe80::786

Vlan 30

ip igmp snooping enable

ipv6 mld snooping enable

8360 L2 Switch 1403

Debugging and troubleshooting

The general procedure for troubleshooting IGMP-MLD proxy includes the following sequence of steps:

Step 1: Verify IGMP-MLD proxy configuration under EVPN.

Ensure that the igmp-mld proxy feature is configured under the EVPN configuration on all relevant VTEPs.
This is crucial for enabling proper multicast proxying functionality across the network.

switch# show run | in proxy

igmp-mld-proxy

Step 2: Verify multicast flags and IGMP-MLD proxy configuration.

Ensure that every VTEP participating in multicast advertises the Multicast Flags Extended Community in
the IMET route. Verify that the IGMP-MLD proxy bits are set under the extended community. Confirm that
the igmp-mld-proxy feature is enabled under the EVPN configuration on all participating VTEPS, ensuring
proper multicast handling across the fabric. Ensure that all other leaf and spine routers in the fabric are
running software versions that support EVPN Route Type 6 and can correctly interpret the Multicast Flags
Extended Community.

switch# show bgp l2vpn evpn extcommunity

Status codes: s suppressed, d damped, h history, * valid, > best, =

multipath, i                  internal, e external, S stale, R removed, a

additional-paths

Origin codes: i - IGP, e - EGP, ? - incomplete

EVPN Route-Type 1 prefix: [1]:[ESI]:[EthTag]

EVPN Route-Type 2 prefix: [2]:[ESI]:[EthTag]:[MAC]:[OrigIP]

EVPN Route-Type 3 prefix: [3]:[EthTag]:[OrigIP]

EVPN Route-Type 4 prefix: [4]:[ESI]:[OrigIP]

EVPN Route-Type 5 prefix: [5]:[ESI]:[EthTag]:[IPAddrLen]:[IPAddr]

EVPN Route-Type 6 prefix: [6]:[EthTag]:[McastSrcIP]:[McastGrpIP]:[OrigIP]

VRF : default

Local Router-ID 172.19.105.3

Network                                   Nexthop         Ext-Community
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

-------

Route Distinguisher: 172.19.106.1:30 (L2VNI 30)

*>i [2]:[0]:[0]:[00:15:01:00:00:01]:[]    172.19.106.1    100:30

* i [2]:[0]:[0]:[00:15:01:00:00:01]:[]    172.19.106.1    100:30

*>i [2]:[0]:[0]:[b8:d4:e7:dc:b7:00]:[]    172.19.106.1    100:30

* i [2]:[0]:[0]:[b8:d4:e7:dc:b7:00]:[]    172.19.106.1    100:30

*>i [2]:[0]:[0]:[b8:d4:e7:dc:f0:00]:[]    172.19.106.1    100:30

* i [2]:[0]:[0]:[b8:d4:e7:dc:f0:00]:[]    172.19.106.1    100:30

* > i [3]:[0]:[172.19.106.1]                172.19.106.1    100:30,IGMP-MLD

Proxy

            * i [3]:[0]:[172.19.106.1]                172.19.106.1

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1404

100:30,IGMP-MLD Proxy

Route Distinguisher: 172.19.106.3:30 (L2VNI 30)

*>  [2]:[0]:[0]:[00:16:01:00:00:01]:[]    172.19.106.3    100:30

*>  [2]:[0]:[0]:[64:e8:81:bc:fb:c0]:[]    172.19.106.3    100:30

* >   [3]:[0]:[172.19.106.3]                172.19.106.3    100:30,IGMP-MLD

Proxy

Step 3: Verify IGMP/MLD Membership report at the receiver VTEP.

1.  Verify membership report: Ensure that the receiver VTEP has learned the membership report from the

multicast receiver.

2.  Check the receiver port: confirm that the receiver port correctly refleccts the interface connected to the

receiver.

3.  Verify IGMP-MLD proxy configuration: when using IGMP-MLD proxy, ensure that each VTEP acts as a

Querier. Verify that the Querier Address field is updated with the VTEPs own IP address.

switch# show ip igmp snooping detail

IGMP Snooping Protocol Info

Total VLANs with IGMP enabled             : 10

Current count of multicast groups joined  : 10

IGMP Drop Unknown Multicast               : Global

Configured IGMP Proxy Querier address     : 1.1.1.2

VLAN ID : 30

VLAN Name : VLAN30

IGMP Configured Version : 3

IGMP Operating Version : 3

Querier Address (this switch) : 1.1.1.2

Querier Port :

Querier UpTime: 29m 47s

Router Detected Ports (VTEPs) : 172.19.106.6

Active Group Address     Tracking   Vers  Mode  Uptime   Expires  Ports/

VTEPs

---------------------    --------   ----  ----  -------  -------

-----------

228.8.8.8                Filter     2           14m 38s  0m 54s   4/1/27

Step 4: Check querier configuration in centralized routing (optional for SVIs).

In centralized routing scenarios, ensure that access VTEPs without L3 SVIs have a valid querier-address
configured to enable querier functionality.

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1405

switch# show run | in querier

ip igmp snooping querier-address 1.1.1.2

ipv6 mld snooping querier-address fe80::781

Step 5: Verify IGMP joins are exported to BGP.

On the receiver-connected switch, check that IGMP joins are reflected under the BGP local routes (without
i/e flags). This confirms that IGMP joins are being exported to BGP, ensuring propagation to other BGP
neighbors.

switch# show bgp l2vpn evpn route-type 6

Status codes: s suppressed, d damped, h history, * valid, > best, =

multipath, i                  internal, e external, S stale, R removed, a

additional-paths

Origin codes: i - IGP, e - EGP, ? - incomplete

EVPN Route-Type 1 prefix: [1]:[ESI]:[EthTag]

EVPN Route-Type 2 prefix: [2]:[ESI]:[EthTag]:[MAC]:[OrigIP]

EVPN Route-Type 3 prefix: [3]:[EthTag]:[OrigIP]

EVPN Route-Type 4 prefix: [4]:[ESI]:[OrigIP]

EVPN Route-Type 5 prefix: [5]:[ESI]:[EthTag]:[IPAddrLen]:[IPAddr]

EVPN Route-Type 6 prefix: [6]:[EthTag]:[McastSrcIP]:[McastGrpIP]:[OrigIP]

VRF : default

Local Router-ID 172.19.105.3

Network                                            Nexthop        Metric

LocPrf      Weight    Path

----------------------------------------------------------------------------

-------

Route Distinguisher: 172.19.106.3:30 (L2VNI 0)

* >  [6]:[0]:[0.0.0.0]:[228.8.8.8]:[172.19.106.3]   172.19.106.3

0        100

   0         ?

Step 6: Verify remote SMET routes in BGP.

On the centralized router or source-connected VTEP (in a bridging scenario), ensure that remote SMET
routes (with "I" flag) are learned in BGP. This confirms that the routes are properly imported into the BGP
tables.

switch# show bgp l2vpn evpn route-type 6

Status codes: s suppressed, d damped, h history, * valid, > best, =

multipath, i                  internal, e external, S stale, R removed, a
additional-paths
Origin codes: i - IGP, e - EGP, ? - incomplete

EVPN Route-Type 1 prefix: [1]:[ESI]:[EthTag]

EVPN Route-Type 2 prefix: [2]:[ESI]:[EthTag]:[MAC]:[OrigIP]

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1406

EVPN Route-Type 3 prefix: [3]:[EthTag]:[OrigIP]

EVPN Route-Type 4 prefix: [4]:[ESI]:[OrigIP]

EVPN Route-Type 5 prefix: [5]:[ESI]:[EthTag]:[IPAddrLen]:[IPAddr]

EVPN Route-Type 6 prefix: [6]:[EthTag]:[McastSrcIP]:[McastGrpIP]:[OrigIP]

VRF : default

Local Router-ID 172.19.105.6

Network                                             Nexthop        Metric

LocPrf     Weight    Path

----------------------------------------------------------------------------

-------

Route Distinguisher: 172.19.106.3:30 (L2VNI 0)

* > | [6]:[0]:[0.0.0.0]:[228.8.8.8]:[172.19.106.3]   172.19.106.3

0        100

   0         ?

Step 7: Check BGP routes in the EVPN table.

On the centralized router or source-connected VTEP (in a bridging scenario), verify that the previously
mentioned BGP routes are present in the EVPN table.

switch# show evpn evi igmp snooping

IGMP-MLD Proxy Status                : Enabled

Forward to Non-Proxy Status          : Disabled

EVI    Source       Group          Next-hop                 Mode     Version

----------------------------------------------------------------------------

-------

30     0.0.0.0      228.8.8.8      vxlan1(172.19.106.3)     -        2

Step 8: Verify BGP EVPN Route are reflected as IGMP/MLD joins.

On the centralized router or source-connected VTEP (in a bridging scenario), ensure that the previously
verified BGP/EVPN routes are imported into IGMP/MLD reports. Check the Port/VTEP column to confirm
that the receiver VTEP is listed. This indicates the presence of a multicast receiver for the given group on
that VTEP. This confirms that the IGMP joins are synchronized across the fabric. For sources and receivers
on the same VLAN, but distributed across the fabric (bridging scenario), the source stream should now be
forwarded to the receiver VTEP.

switch# show ip igmp snooping vlan 30 detail

IGMP Snooping Protocol Info

Total VLANs with IGMP enabled            : 12

Current count of multicast groups joined : 1

IGMP Drop Unknown Multicast              : Global
Configured IGMP Proxy Querier address    : 1.1.1.9

VLAN ID                                  : 30

VLAN Name                                : VLAN30

IGMP Configured Version                  : 3

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1407

IGMP Operating Version                   : 3

Querier Address                          : 10.101.128.1

Querier Port                             : lag100

Querier UpTime                           : 1h 38m

Querier Expiration Time                  : 2m 19s

Router Detected Ports (VTEPs)            : lag100

Active Group Address   Tracking   Vers    Mode    Uptime    Expires   Ports/

VTEPs

--------------------   --------   ----    ----    --------  -------

------------

228.8.8.8              Filter     2               10m 38s   0m 0s

172.19.106.3

Step 9: Verify Default SMET in Routing Scenarios.

In routing scenarios, ensure that the centralized router correctly routes traffic from the source VLAN to
the destination VLAN. Verify that the Default SMET is learned by the source-connected VTEP. The Default
SMET is a special SMET route (represented by 0.0.0.0, 0.0.0.0) genrated by PIM-enabled VTEPs and
sent to all other VTEPs. Confirm that the Default SMET is present on the access VTEPs, ensuring proper
multicast source forwarding from access VTEPs to centralized router.

switch# show evpn evi igmp snooping

IGMP-MLD Proxy Status                    : Enabled

Forward to Non Proxy Status              : Disabled

EVI      Source      Group      Next-hop                Mode     Version

--------------------------------------------------------------------------

31       0.0.0.0     0.0.0.0    vxlan1(172.19.106.6)    -        -

Step 10: Verify Router Detection for Default SMET.

Ensure that the Default SMET route from the PIM-enabled VTEP is reflected as a Router Detected VTEP on
the access VTEP. Check the Router Detected VTEP section to confirm that the PIM-enabled VTEP is listed,
verifying proper multicast router detection across the fabric.

switch# show ip igmp snooping detail

4/1/27

Step 11: Verify mroute for Source-Group pair at centralized router.

At the centralized router, verify that there is an mroute for the source-group pair. If the source and receiver
are on the same VLAN, the mroute does not have any downstream interface listed. If the mroute is missing,
ensure that the Default SMET is installed on the source-attached access VTEP.

switch# show ip mroute 228.8.8.8 all-vrfs
