vlan31           forwarding    -

Supported platforms and standards

Standard-based multicast VXLAN is supported by the HPE Aruba Networking 6300, 6400, 8100, 8325,
8360, and 9300, and 10000 Switch Series. This feature follows the specifications outlined in RFC 9251,
ensuring compatibility with standard requirements. However, support for multihoming is excluded from this
implementation.

Subtopics

Scale

Public

Supported platforms and standards 1409

Scale

| VLANs/L2 | 128  | 128  | TBD  | 128  | 128  | 128  | 128   |
VNIs (Multi
cast suppor
t)
| PIM interfac | 64  | 64  | TBD | 64  | 64  | 64  | 64  |

es (SVIs)
| IGMP group | 2000 | 2000 | TBD | 2000 | 2000 | 2000 | 2000 |

| ------- | ---- | ---- | --- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- |
| VTEPs   | 32   | 32   | TBD | 32   | 32   | 32   | 32   |

Considerations and best practices
•  If the border router lacks PIM routing, an external router can be configured. In such cases, SVI/VLANs
must be stretched towards the external router.
•  When PIM routing is configured on the VSX Border router, VSX-specific PIM commands are applicable.
•  The querier should not be configured on any access switches attached to the Leaf nodes. For querier
redundancy, use VSX as Leaf nodes.
•  Ensure that spine routers are running software versions that support EVPN Route Type 6 and can
interpret Multicast Flags Extended Community. This is essential for correct processing of EVPN-based
multicast signaling and for interoperability in VXLAN EVPN delpoyments.
Subtopics
Supported configurations
Limitations for standard based IGMP-MLD proxy mode

|     | Public |     |     |     |     |     | Scale 1410 |
| --- | ------ | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ---------- |

Supported configurations

•  Distributed Active Gateway (AGW) and Centralized Gateway Routing (CGW) with PIM routing at the

Border VTEP.

•  Multicast routing within the fabric (Border Leaf only) outside of the fabric (External PIM router), or both

simultaneously.

•  Both IPv4 and IPv6 tunnels.

•

Interconnection of the leaf node to the Extended Edge via static VXLAN.

Limitations for standard based IGMP-MLD proxy mode

•  Multihoming (using Route Type 7 and 8) is currently not supported. Use VSX as an alternative.

•  Multifabric and shared border configurations are not supported.

OISM support

EVPN Optimized Inter-Subnet Multicast (OISM) enables inter-subnet IP multicast traffic routing across Layer
2 Virtual Network Identifiers (L2VNIs), as defined in RFC 9625. In deployments using PIM over VXLAN,
multicast routing is achieved by exchanging PIM control packets over VXLAN tunnels. Some of the problems
with this deployment include:

•

It can result in traffic hairpinning. Multicast traffic egresses from a PE router and returns to the same
router on another VNI because routing is performed by the designated router (DR).

•

Interoperability with other vendors is not supported.

•  As the scale increases, the number of PIM packets exchanged across the fabric increases.

OISM eliminates these issues and provides optimal routing for inter-subnet multicast traffic. When OISM
is enabled, there is no need to configure PIM on individual interfaces or use PIM-related commands such
as vsx-virtual-neighbor or datapath-auto-include. However, PIM must still be enabled globally per VRF.
Additional commands, such as gateway-bd and accept-remote-source, are required. Local VLANs that do not
have a mapped L2VNI need to have PIM enabled at the interface level.

A dedicated Broadcast Domain (BD) should be configured as OISM Shared BD (SBD)and it should be
configured on all PE routers in the EVPN domain. The OISM SBD should not include any local ports.
However, in VSX deployments, the ISL port should be included to synchronize multicast flows.

For centralized routing use cases where access switches do not have SVIs, OISM is not required. Instead, PIM
should be enabled on the centralized router to support multicast routing.

Public

Supported configurations 1411

OISM is supported on the following switch series: 10000, 6300, 6400, 8100, 8325, 8325H, 8325P, 8360,
9300, 9300S and 10040 Switch Series.

Supported Features

•  OISM with Head-End Replication

•  Extension: Gateway BD

•  Symmetric/asymmetric IRB and distributed anycast gateway

Limitations

•  Underlay multicast replication is not supported.

•  Multi-fabric deployments are not suppoted.

•  PIM External Gateway extension is not supported.

•

Interoperability with other vendors is not supported. All leafs in the VXLAN fabric must be OISM-
capable.

For OISM-related configurations, please refer to the EVPN VXLAN Guide.

Configuration task list

Prerequisites

The following are prerequisites for a Standard based Multicast in VXLAN with OISM:

•  Single fabric VXLAN topology is up with Ingress Replication mode

•  Underlay – OSPF

•  Overlay – iBGP

•  SBD extended on VTEPs

•  L2VNIs configured on required VTEPs

•  L3VNIs configured on all VTEPs

•  All L2VNI tunnels are operational

•  All L3VNI tunnels are operational

•

Interface VLANs configured on all VTEPs

•  Enable IGMP/MLD snooping on all multicast-enabled VLANs

Public

Configuration task list 1412

•  Underlay Extension – Layer 2 Extension

Procedure

1.  Enable igmp-mld proxy under EVPN on all VTEPs.

evpn

igmp-mld-proxy

2.  Enable Multicast Replication mode to OISM on all VTEPs.

multicast-replication-mode oism

3.  Enable SBD VLAN per VRF under EVPN.

vlan 1000

rd auto

route-target export 100:1000

route-target import 100:1000

supplementary-broadcast-domain

4.  Create interface VLANs for SBD VLAN on all VTEPs.

interface vlan 1000

    vrf attach vrf_emp

    ip address 100.100.100.100/24

    ipv6 address 100::103/64

    ip igmp enable

    ipv6 mld enable

5.  Create interface VLANs and enable IGMP/MLD for L2VNI mapped VLANs.

Vlan 30

Ip igmp snooping enable

Ipv6 mld snooping enable

interface vlan 30
    vrf attach vrf_emp

    ip address 10.101.128.1/24

    active-gateway ip mac 00:00:00:00:00:30

    active-gateway ip 10.101.128.1

    ipv6 address 2000:101:80::9/64

    active-gateway ipv6 mac 00:00:00:00:00:30

    active-gateway ipv6 fe80::80

    ip igmp enable

    ipv6 mld enable

6.  Create interface VLANs and enable IGMP/MLD and PIM for L3VNI mapped VLANs on VTEP (VLAN 850

is local to single VTEP)

Public

Configuration task list 1413

interface vlan 850

    vrf attach vrf_emp

    ip address 30.1.0.1/16

    active-gateway ip mac 00:00:00:00:00:30

    ipv6 address 2003:103:1::5/64

    active-gateway ipv6 mac 00:00:00:00:00:30

    active-gateway ipv6 2003:103:1::5

    active-gateway ipv6 fe80::1

    ip igmp enable

    ipv6 mld enable

    ip pim-sparse enable

    ipv6 pim6-sparse enable

Interop with External PIM router – Layer3-PIM-Extension via
Gateway BD

router pim vrf <name>

gateway-bd vlan70

This command enables multicast interconnectivity between EVPN PE routers and PIM routers in EVPN
VXLAN networks by using a Gateway Bridge Domain (Gateway BD). EVPN provides multicast forwarding
within the EVPN domain. To extend multicast communication to external PIM-based domains, configure
a Gateway BD. It is recommended that only one Gateway BD be configured per EVPN tenant to prevent
multicast loops.

Sources connected to external routers: When a receiver within the network sends an IGMP/MLD Join, the
request is propagated as an SBD RT-6 Join toward the Gateway BD enabled PE router. Upon receiving this,
the PE router will translate the join into an IGMP/MLD message on the gateway (BD), ensuring that the
external PIM router processes the request. The PIM router then sends a PIM Join upstream to pull multicast
traffic from the source. Once received, the Gateway BD forwards the multicast stream using OISM to bridge
or route it toward the clients.

Clients connected to external routers: When a client is connected to an external PIM router, multicast
traffic from within the EVPN Tenant Domain must be forwarded to the external network. To achieve this,
each OISM PE attached to the Gateway BD must generate an SMET route on all L2VNIs, ensuring interest in
all multicast flows. The Gateway BD is automatically included in the outgoing interface list, allowing multicast
traffic to reach the external PIM router. For VSX deployments, it is recommended that the Gateway BD must
be configured on both VSX primary and secondary devices, even if the client is connected through ROP from
only one VSX peer.

This configuration is required only when there is a Layer 3 extension outside the VXLAN fabric to an
external PIM router, where multicast sources or clients may exist. In a Gateway BD setup, the multicast
source must reside in a VLAN that is stretched across all VTEPs. If the multicast source is placed in a VLAN

that is local-only and not extended to other VTEPs, multicast traffic will not be forwarded, and the flow will
fail.

NOTE

The extension should be an SVI extension, and the gateway BD must be
configured on this extended SVI. In the example below, VLAN 70 is the extended
SVI over an MCLAG.

 interface vlan 40

 ip pim-sparse accept-remote-source

 ipv6 pim6-sparse accept-remote-source
This command enables the router to accept multicast traffic from remote sources. When enabled, the router
accepts multicast traffic from sources that are not directly connected and acts as the First Hop Router
(FHR), performing registration to the Rendezvous Point (RP). In scenarios where a Gateway BD is configured
in EVPN OISMnetworks, this should be applied to the external router interface that connects to the Gateway
BD.

Gateway BD Configurations

router pim vrf vrf_emp

    enable

    gateway-bd vlan70

router pim6 vrf vrf_emp

    enable

    gateway-bd vlan70

interface vlan 70

    vrf attach vrf_emp

    vsx active-forwarding

    ip address 70.1.1.1/24

    ipv6 address 70::1/64

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ipv6 ospfv3 1 area 0.0.0.0
    ip pim-sparse enable

    ipv6 pim6-sparse enable

interface lag 70 multi-chassis

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    vlan trunk allowed 70

    lacp mode active

PIM Extension Router

Public

Interop with External PIM router – Layer3-PIM-Exte... 1415

interface vlan 70

    vrf attach vrf_emp

    ip address 70.1.1.3/24

    ipv6 address 70::3/64

    ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ipv6 ospfv3 1 area 0.0.0.0

    ip igmp enable

    ipv6 mld enable

    ip pim-sparse enable

    ipv6 pim6-sparse enable

    ip pim-sparse accept-remote-source

    ipv6 pim6-sparse accept-remote-source

    neighbor ntlcampus update-source loopback 0

Use Case 1: OISM mixed symmetric IRB 1440

routing

!

router ospf 1

    router-id 9.9.9.14

    graceful-restart ignore-lost-interface

    area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 65050

    bgp router-id 172.19.105.4

    no bgp fast-external-fallover

    bgp graceful-restart restart-time 300

    neighbor ntlcampus peer-group

    neighbor ntlcampus remote-as 65050

    neighbor ntlcampus fall-over

    neighbor ntlcampus update-source loopback 0

    neighbor 172.19.105.5 peer-group ntlcampus

    neighbor 172.19.105.6 peer-group ntlcampus

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 172.19.105.5 send-community extended

        neighbor 172.19.105.5 activate

        neighbor 172.19.105.6 send-community extended

        neighbor 172.19.105.6 activate

    exit-address-family

!

    vrf vrf_emp

        no bgp fast-external-fallover

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

        address-family ipv6 unicast

            redistribute connected

        exit-address-family

!
router pim vrf vrf_emp

    enable

router pim6 vrf vrf_emp

    enable

https-server vrf default

https-server vrf mgmt

Debugging and troubleshooting

About this task

The general procedure for troubleshooting OISM-related configuration includes the following sequence of
steps:

Step 1: Verify OISM configuration under EVPN.

switch#show evpn oism all-vrfs

OISM Global Status            : Enabled

VRF                           : vrf_emp

SBD                           : 1000

Gateway BD (IPv4)             : vlan70

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1470

Gateway BD (IPv6)             : vlan70

switch#show run | in oism

    multicast-replication-mode oism
Step 2: Verify SBD and OISM flags

switch# show bgp l2vpn evpn extcommunity

Status codes: s suppressed, d damped, h history, * valid, > best, =

multipath,

              i internal, e external S Stale, R Removed, a additional-paths

Origin codes: i - IGP, e - EGP, ? - incomplete

EVPN Route-Type 1 prefix: [1]:[ESI]:[EthTag]

EVPN Route-Type 2 prefix: [2]:[ESI]:[EthTag]:[MAC]:[OrigIP]

EVPN Route-Type 3 prefix: [3]:[EthTag]:[OrigIP]

EVPN Route-Type 4 prefix: [4]:[ESI]:[OrigIP]

EVPN Route-Type 5 prefix: [5]:[ESI]:[EthTag]:[IPAddrLen]:[IPAddr]

EVPN Route-Type 6 prefix: [6]:[EthTag]:[McastSrcIP]:[McastGrpIP]:[OrigIP]

VRF : default

Local Router-ID 172.19.105.5

*>i [3]:[0]:[172.19.106.1]            172.19.106.1

100:30,IGMP-MLD Proxy,OISM-supported

* i [3]:[0]:[172.19.106.1]            172.19.106.1

 100:30,IGMP-MLD Proxy,OISM-supported

172.19.106.4

 100:1000

*>i [3]:[0]:[172.19.106.4]             172.19.106.4

100:1000,IGMP-MLD Proxy,OISM-supported,OISM-SBD
Step 3: Ensure that RT6 joins are learned on SBD VLANs across all remote VTEPs.

Local join on VLAN 30 on the 6300 VSF VTEP.

switch#show ip igmp snooping vlan 30

IGMP Snooping Protocol Info

Total VLANs with IGMP enabled             : 26

Current count of multicast groups joined  : 1

IGMP Drop Unknown Multicast               : Global

Configured IGMP Proxy Querier address     : 1.1.1.2

VLAN ID : 30

VLAN Name : VLAN30

IGMP Configured Version : 3

IGMP Operating Version : 3

Public

Querier Expiration Time : 0m 50s

--------------------- ---------- ---- ---- --------- ----------

228.8.8.8              Filter    2         13m 23s   3m 12s

228.8.8.8              Filter    2         13m 51s   0m 0s
Step 4: Verify the PIM Mroute bridging scenario on the source VTEP.

vsf-switch# show ip mroute 224.4.4.4 10.101.128.30 vrf vrf_emp detail
Group Address                 : 224.4.4.4

Source Address                : 10.101.128.30

Neighbor                      :

Incoming interface            : vlan30

Upstream Forwarded VTEP(s)    : 172.19.106.1

Unicast Routing Protocol      : connected

Metric                        : 0

Public

Debugging and troubleshooting 1472

Metric  Pref                  : 0

Uptime (HH:MM:SS)             : 00:01:41

NOTE

When the source VLAN is shared between the source and client VTEPs, traffic
is bridged on the source VLAN (VLAN 30) without any TTL decrement. The
upstream-forwarded VTEPs list includes all VTEPs to which the traffic is bridged.

Step 5: Verify the PIM Mroute routing scenario

In the example below, source VLAN 850 is not shared between the source VTEP and client VTEP
172.19.106.1. As a result, traffic is routed from the source VLAN to the SBD VLAN for VTEP 172.19.106.1.

On the source VTEP (Source VLAN to SBD routing)

Interface       State          Vteps

-----------     ----------     -----

vlan1000        forwarding     172.19.106.1

Step 6: PIM Mroute validation routing and bridging scenario

On the source VTEP (source VLAN to SBD routing, and bridging on the source VLAN)

In the example below, source VLAN 850 is shared between the source VTEP and client VTEP 172.19.106.3.
Hence, traffic is bridged on the source VLAN. However, source VLAN 850 is not shared between the source
VTEP and client VTEP 172.19.106.1. As a result, traffic is routed from the source VLAN to the SBD VLAN
for VTEP 172.19.106.1.

Multi-fabric multicast VXLAN

The Multicast VXLAN for multi-fabric solution allows the total number of VTEPs to scale beyond single-
fabric capabilities. Refer to Single fabric Multicast VXLAN for more information on single-fabric capabilities.
This solution can also be used if there are multiple sites which require multicast VXLAN connectivity. The
multi-fabric solution focuses on interconnecting multiple campuses.

Public

Multi-fabric multicast VXLAN 1474

Figure 1 shows an example of multi-fabric EVPN VXLAN.

Figure 1. Multi-fabric EVPN VXLAN

Figure 2 shows an example of one fabric per site, where the border VTEP has intra-fabric VXLAN tunnels to
all VTEPs within a fabric and inter-fabric VXLAN tunnels towards other fabric border VTEPs.

Figure 2. One Fabric per site

Public

Multi-fabric multicast VXLAN 1475

In some deployments, splitting a site into multiple fabrics to support the required VTEP scale is necessary.

Figure 3 shows an example of multiple fabrics per site, where one fabric per site is configured to a border
leader VTEP. This helps to minimize full mesh eBGP peering to other sites. Border VTEPs in other fabrics
within a site only eBGP peer to the border leader VTEP.

Figure 3. Multiple fabrics per site

Public

Multi-fabric multicast VXLAN 1476

With eBGP EVPN peering established, full multi-fabric EVPN VXLAN tunnels are dynamically formed, as
shown in Figure 4.

Figure 4. Dynamically formed full miulti-fabric EVPN VXLAN tunnels

Public

Multi-fabric multicast VXLAN 1477

Public

Multi-fabric multicast VXLAN 1478

Protocol and feature details

A campus/ DC network can be divided into multiple fabrics for the following scenarios:

•  Campuses are geographically distributed

•  VTEPs may scale poorly after a certain limit

Refer to Protocol and feature details for single fabric multicast VXLAN for more information for protocol and
feature details for Multi-fabric Multicast VXLAN.

Subtopics

Multi-fabric Shared Border

Multi-fabric Shared Border

Campus deployments typically have multiple branch offices (fabrics) within their sites, and there may be
multiple similar sites, too. With shared border support the core router can aggregate different branch offices
(more than one fabric) and act as the gateway to other sites.

In Figure 1, Border Router BR1 at SiteA acts as the Shared Border, functioning as the gateway that serves
both fabric 1 and fabric 2.

Figure 1. Shared border multi-fabric

Public

Protocol and feature details 1479

Unlike in the regular leaf, the Shared Border routers are attached to multiple fabrics. Each individual fabric
forms a set of VTEPS. Split horizon rules are enabled on VTEPs and then disabled between the individual
fabrics to allow them to communicate across fabrics. In regular border router, the tunnels are classified based
on EVPN peering: iBgp or eBgp. PIM differentiate the iBgp and eBgp neighbors and split horizon rules are
applied. The same rules are applicable to individual fabrics attached within the Shared Border. The same
rules apply to the shared border as with individual fabrics. For example, Figure 1 shows packets unable to
reforward back to the originating VTEPs when routing one fabric to another in the shared border router. As
shown above, the source attaches to R1 and because BR1 acts as the RP, it receives both R2 and R3. When
R1 traffic reaches BR1, it cannot forward the traffic back to Fabric 1 due to R1 directly serving R2 via direct
mesh. Traffic can only be forwarded to Fabric 2. In the absence R2 will see duplicate traffic.

Multicast routing on a Shared Border requires a unique local-broadcast-group ID route-map configuration
applied for each fabric. This configuration is mandatory within Multicast for both L3VNI and L2VNI across
fabrics, unlike in the case of unicast where it is only required for L2VNI subnet stretch.

Routemap Configuration with local Broadcast Group ID

A unique, local-broadcast-group ID route-map configuration is required for multicast routing on shared
borders for both L3VNI and L2VNI fabrics. The following example shows the required configuration at a
shared broder with two fabrics. In this example, broadcast group ID 2 and 4 represent each fabric.

route-map rmap1 permit seq 10

set local-broadcast-group 2

route-map rmap2 permit seq 10

set local-broadcast-group 4

Public

Multi-fabric Shared Border 1480

...

router bgp 5000

    ...

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 1.1.1.1 route-map rmap1 in

        neighbor 3.3.3.3 route-map rmap2 in

   exit-address-family

Subtopics

Multi-fabric Border

Multi-fabric Border

ip multicast multi-fabric border
This command is used to enable PIM's border router functionality. This configuration must be enabled on the
border router when iBGP-eBGP is not used. This configuration is global and is applicable to all tenant's VRF's
configured in that router.

Subtopics

Multi-fabric Identifier

Multi-fabric Identifier

ip multicast multi-fabric identifier-value <IDENTIFIER VALUE> <cr>
With MFID configuration, multicast protocol can override the default LEAF to Fabric association which is
based on the BGP AS number with MFID values. When the individual fabrics are placed on different AS, the
default setting suffices. The default setting allows multicast to use BGP's AS number to differentiate the
fabric a leaf router belongs to. Occasionally, when two or more fabrics share the same AS number, a manual
overriding of MFID is required.

Supported platforms and standards

The EVPN VXLAN multi-fabric solution is supported only with the 8325 and 10000 Switch Series as a
border-leader VTEP. Border VTEPs (with the exception of non-border-leaders) that support this solution
include the 8325, 8360, 6400 and 10000 Switch Series. Non-border VTEPs that support this solution

Public

Multi-fabric Border 1481

include the 6300, 8100, 6400, 8325, 8360 and 10000 Switch Series. Shared border is supported by the
8325 and 8360 Switch Series.

NOTE

For the 10000 Switch series, a firewall policy is not supported when the switch is
acting as a border-VTEP or border-leader.

L3VNI and L2VNI are supported between the fabrics. L3VNI is supported between the fabrics in Shared
Border only. Centralized L3 gateway is not supported between fabrics.

The multicast multi-fabric EVPN VXLAN implementation is currently proprietary to AOS-CX.

Border VTEP extensions

Public

Border VTEP extensions 1486

Border VTEPs are capable of terminating the fabric and extending to other L2/L3 routers. PIM enabled
VXLAN VTEPs can be extended to other routers which can be connected to sources/clients. The following
types of L3/L2 extensions are supported:

•  L2 VSX LAG: Upstream or Downstream routers are connected via L2 VSX LAG links.

•  L3 VSX LAG: Upstream or Downstream routers are connected via L3 VSX LAG links.

•  ROP extension: L3 extension for Sources/Clients via Route Only Ports is supported.

•  Point to Point SVI extension: L3 extension for Sources/Clients via Point to Point SVIs is supported.

If the source is connected via Route only Port or via Point to Point SVI, it is recommended to have an
additional L3 Link per VRF between the VSX devices for upstream redundancy. If the L3 link is a SVI, it is
recommended to not add ISL port in that VLAN.

Subtopics

Packet forwarding rules at the border router

Packet forwarding rules at the border router

PIM Hellos are periodically sent to both intra-fabric and inter-fabric VTEP tunnels when the Hello timer
expires on the L3VNI and L2VNI interfaces. Because the border routers are connected to both inter and intra
tunnels, Hellos from the local VTEPs as well from the remote border VTEPs are received. The border VTEP
learns both intra-fabric VTEP and the remote border leaf VTEP under the L3VNI and L2VNI interfaces.

NOTE

The border VTEP does not re-forward the PIM hellos from one fabric to another.

.

.

Multicast Routing Across Fabrics

The typical deployment for multi-fabric will have receiver and source distributed at different sites (see
Figure 1).

In Figure 1, a receiver request from site B for multicast group is processed by the VTEP B router where
the receiver is present. RP is configured on the border router at site A. VTEP B determines that the Border
VTEP is the Nexthop to reach the RP. Site A's border VTEP, which is the RP, processes the PIM join and then
initiates S, G PIM join towards Site A's VTEP router, where the source is connected. Traffic then flows from
Site A's border VTEP to Site A's border VTEP, then from there to Site B's Border VTEP, then finally towards
Site B's border VTEP to the client.

Public

Packet forwarding rules at the border router 1487

All four VTEPs in this path install the Mroutes. The border VTEPs on both sites route traffic from one tunnel
to another. The VTEP A border VTEPs from the source interface to the tunnel and the VTEP B border
VTEPs from the tunnel to the receiver's interface.

At the Border VTEP, the list of outgoing VTEPs are updated in the Intra-Inter Forwarded VTEP(s) section
of the Mroute table. This field contains the combined set of inter and intra tunnels where the packet needs
to be forwarded. If the packet is ingressing from the intra-fabric tunnel, it is forwarded only to the list of
inter-fabric tunnels listed in this field. If the packet is ingressing from the inter-fabric tunnel, it is forwarded
only to the list of intra-fabric tunnels listed in this field.

Figure 1. Receiver and source at different sites

PIM-SSM

PIM-SSM over multi-fabric is supported and inherits the functionality of PIM-SM over multi-fabric. The
PIM-SSM over multi-fabric supports only IPv4 address ranges. PIM-SSM with Multifabric supports the legacy
clients (IGMPv1/IGMPv2) using SSM-Map.

Distributed Anycast Gateway

Distributed anycast gateway functionality is supported across the fabrics and within the fabric. It is possible
to enable anycast gateway within the same fabric and serve the attached multicast source and clients.

When multicast sources are attached to these anycast interfaces, the register-source configuration must be
provisioned.

Public

PIM-SSM 1488

Caveats

The following are the caveats of multicast vxlan for multi-fabric:

•  Multicast Boundary feature (ip multicast boundary) is not supported for single and multifabric.

•  PIM Dense Mode is not supported for single and multifabric.

•  PVLAN is not supported for single and multifabric.

•  Centralized L3 gateway is not supported across fabrics.

•  Shared border functionality is not supported on the 6300, 6400, 8100, 8325H, 8400, and 10000

platforms.

•  Mtrace is not supported for single and multifabric.

•

Interop with fabrics running any solution other than CX Overlay is not supported.

•  Static multicast route entries are not supported for single and multifabric.

•  Multifabric with underlay IP type IPv6 is not supported.

•  PIM-SSM over multifabric is not supported for IPv6 overlay.

•  Shared border L2VNI subnet stretch is not supported.

Considerations and Best Practices

Refer to Considerations and best practices for Single fabric Multicast VXLAN for more information for
protocol and feature details.

•  AOS-CX supports MCLAG, ROP, and P2P SVI extensions between the Border VTEP and External router

(non-VXLAN). The MCLAG extension is preferred.

•  Check configuration of the following: BGP, VPN, and VXLAN interface.

•  Check the status of the Multicast configuration (IGMP, IGMP Snooping, and PIM).RP and Source must be

reachable from other fabrics.

Subtopics

RP Replacement
Multi-fabric L3VNI and L2VNI Stretch
Shared Border

Public

Caveats 1489

RP Replacement

Refer to the following considerations and best practices specific to RP Replacement:

•  RPs do not need to be present at every site. In multi-fabric topology, the RP can be placed at one site

centrally or can be configured at multiple sites to mitigate site failures.

•  A Border router or Leaf router can be configured as RP. Both dynamic and static RPs are supported.

•  The general recommendation is to place the RP at the site where the majority of multicast sources are

present.

•  An RP located outside the fabric is also supported, provided that the unicast and multicast reachability is

there to the external RP.

•  Anycast RP is supported in single-fabric, multi-fabric and shared border setups. For more information,

see Anycast RP support for VXLANs.

Multi-fabric L3VNI and L2VNI Stretch

Refer to the following considerations and best practices specific to Multi-fabric L3VNI and L2VNI stretch:

•  Preferred Border Router should be C-RP, C-BSR.

•  Command dyn-vxlan-tunnel-bridging-mode ibgp-ebgp under evpn must be set on the Border Router.

Shared Border

Refer to the following considerations and best practices specific to Shared Border:

•  Preferred Shared Border Router should be C-RP, C-BSR.

•  Command ip multicast multi-fabric border must be set on the Shared Border to determine it is a

Multicast shared Border Router.

•  By default, if there is no need to configure the MFID, it will automatically bet set to the local AS Number.

•  Ensure that the Shared Border peering in Route Reflector is set to "next-hop-unchanged".

•  Remove the inter-vxlan-briging mode before; it is not allowed to create a new route map for the

broadcast group.

•  Confirm the correct Broadcast group is assigned to the appropriate BGP peer within the EVPN address

Family.

Public

RP Replacement 1490

•  Broadcast Groups must be configured even if L2 Unicast extension is not required between fabrics.

Use Cases

Select a use case from the list in the left navigation menu.

The use cases provide examples of multi-fabric multicast VXLAN.

NOTE

Anycast RP with MSDP is also supported on multi-fabric and shared borders.
Sample configurations are provided in this section.

Subtopics

Use case 1: one fabric per site (PIM-SM)
Use case 2: multiple fabrics per site (PIM-SM)
Use case 3: one fabric per site (PIM-SSM)
Use case 4: multiple fabrics per site (PIM-SSM)
Use case 5: Shared Border (PIM-SM)

Use case 1: one fabric per site (PIM-SM)

NOTE

L2VNI subnet stretch across fabrics is supported on 10.13 onward.

Figure 1. Use case 1: one fabric per site (PIM-SM)

no shutdown

vlan 101

vni 9002

vrf vrf2

routing

routing

!

inter-switch-link lag 120

set tag 110

route-map tag_ospf_bgp deny seq 10

match tag 110

route-map tag_ospf_bgp deny seq 20

match tag 120

route-map tag_ospf_bgp permit seq 30

router ospf 1

route-map test_1 permit seq 10

match ip address prefix-list test_1

distance 250

Site-2-Access-vtep-2 1839

redistribute local loopback

    area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 65536

    bgp router-id 112.15.1.3

    neighbor 192.168.1.3 remote-as 65536

    neighbor 192.168.1.3 update-source loopback 1

    neighbor 192.168.1.4 remote-as 65536

    neighbor 192.168.1.4 update-source loopback 1

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 192.168.1.3 send-community extended

        neighbor 192.168.1.3 activate

        neighbor 192.168.1.4 send-community extended

        neighbor 192.168.1.4 activate

    exit-address-family

!

    vrf vrf1

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

            redistribute local loopback

            redistribute ospf

        exit-address-family

!

    vrf vrf2

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

            redistribute local loopback

            redistribute ospf

        exit-address-family

!

router pim vrf vrf1

    enable

    pim-ssm
router pim vrf vrf2

    enable

    pim-ssm

    pim-ssm range-access-list pim-ssm-range-acl-vrf2

Site-2-Border-vtep-Primary

hostname Site-2-Border-vtep-Primary

no ip icmp redirect

route-redistribute active-routes-only

vrf vrf1

    rd 1:12

    route-target export 1:100 evpn

    route-target import 1:100 evpn

vrf vrf2

    rd 2:12

    route-target export 2:100 evpn

    route-target import 2:100 evpn

cli-session

    timeout 0

!

!

ssh server vrf mgmt

access-list ip pim-ssm-range-acl-vrf2
    vsx-sync

    !

    10 permit any any 225.1.0.0/255.255.0.0

    20 permit any any 225.2.1.0/255.255.255.0

    30 permit any any 225.2.2.0/255.255.255.0

    40 permit any any 232.0.0.0/255.0.0.0

vlan 1

vlan 501

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 502

    ip igmp snooping enable

Public

Site-2-Border-vtep-Primary 1844

vlan 701

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 702

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 752,1001-1002

vlan 1301

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 1302

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 3001-3002

virtual-mac 00:11:00:00:00:11

evpn

    redistribute local-mac

    redistribute local-svi

!! this command is specific to evpn multi-site on Border-vtep

    dyn-vxlan-tunnel-bridging-mode ibgp-ebgp

    vlan 501

        rd auto

        route-target export 501:141

        route-target import 501:141

        redistribute host-route

    vlan 502

        rd auto

        route-target export 502:142

        route-target import 502:142

        redistribute host-route

    vlan 701

        rd auto

        route-target export 701:141

        route-target import 701:141

        redistribute host-route

    vlan 702
        rd auto

        route-target export 702:142

        route-target import 702:142

        redistribute host-route

interface mgmt

    no shutdown

    ip dhcp

interface lag 120

    no shutdown

    no routing

    vlan trunk native 1

    ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include

interface vlan 3002

    ip address 61.10.2.1/30

    source ip 80.80.80.80

    no shutdown

    vni 2100

        vlan 501

    vni 2101

        vlan 502

        vlan 701

    vni 2301

        vlan 702

    vni 9002

        vrf vrf2

        routing

    vni 16777214
        vrf vrf1

        routing

vsx

    system-mac 00:11:00:00:00:11

    inter-switch-link lag 120

    role primary

    keepalive peer 45.1.1.2 source 45.1.1.1

ip prefix-list test_1 seq 10 deny 192.170.1.255/32

ip prefix-list test_1 seq 20 deny 193.170.1.255/32

ip prefix-list test_1 seq 40 deny 197.170.1.255/32

ip prefix-list test_1 seq 50 deny 198.170.1.255/32

Public

Site-2-Border-vtep-Primary 1849

ip prefix-list test_1 seq 60 permit 4.4.4.50/32

ip prefix-list test_1 seq 70 permit 8.8.8.8/32

!

!

!

!

route-map tag_bgp_ospf permit seq 10

     set tag 210

route-map tag_ospf_bgp deny seq 10

     match tag 210

route-map tag_ospf_bgp deny seq 20

     match tag 220

route-map tag_ospf_bgp permit seq 30

route-map test_1 permit seq 10

     match ip address prefix-list test_1

!

router ospf 1

    router-id 112.12.1.3

    distance 250

    redistribute connected

    redistribute local loopback

    redistribute bgp route-map test_1

    area 0.0.0.0

router ospf 1 vrf vrf1

    router-id 112.12.1.5

    distance 250

    redistribute connected

    redistribute bgp route-map tag_bgp_ospf

    area 0.0.0.0

router ospf 1 vrf vrf2

    router-id 112.12.2.5

    distance 250
    redistribute connected

    redistribute bgp route-map tag_bgp_ospf

    area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 65536

    bgp router-id 112.12.1.3

    neighbor 4.4.4.4 remote-as 65536

    neighbor 4.4.4.4 update-source loopback 114

    neighbor 40.1.1.1 remote-as 65535

    neighbor 40.1.3.1 remote-as 65535

    neighbor 45.1.1.2 remote-as 65536

    neighbor 85.85.85.85 remote-as 65536

Public

Site-2-Border-vtep-Primary 1850

neighbor 85.85.85.85 update-source loopback 114

    neighbor 193.170.1.255 remote-as 65535

    neighbor 193.170.1.255 ebgp-multihop 5

    neighbor 193.170.1.255 update-source loopback 113

    neighbor 194.170.1.255 remote-as 65535

    neighbor 194.170.1.255 ebgp-multihop 5

    neighbor 194.170.1.255 update-source loopback 113

    neighbor 197.170.1.255 remote-as 65537

    neighbor 197.170.1.255 ebgp-multihop 5

    neighbor 197.170.1.255 update-source loopback 113

    neighbor 198.170.1.255 remote-as 65537

    neighbor 198.170.1.255 ebgp-multihop 5

    neighbor 198.170.1.255 update-source loopback 113

    address-family ipv4 unicast

        neighbor 40.1.1.1 activate

        neighbor 40.1.3.1 activate

        neighbor 45.1.1.2 activate

        redistribute connected

        redistribute local loopback

    exit-address-family

    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 next-hop-self

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 route-reflector-client

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 send-community extended

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 activate

        neighbor 85.85.85.85 next-hop-self

        neighbor 85.85.85.85 route-reflector-client

        neighbor 85.85.85.85 send-community extended

                neighbor 85.85.85.85 activate

        neighbor 193.170.1.255 send-community extended

        neighbor 193.170.1.255 activate

        neighbor 194.170.1.255 send-community extended
        neighbor 194.170.1.255 activate

        neighbor 197.170.1.255 send-community extended

        neighbor 197.170.1.255 activate

        neighbor 198.170.1.255 send-community extended

        neighbor 198.170.1.255 activate

    exit-address-family

!

    vrf vrf1

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

            redistribute local loopback

Public

Site-2-Border-vtep-Primary 1851

redistribute ospf route-map tag_ospf_bgp

        exit-address-family

!

hostname site-2-Border-vtep-Secondary

no ip icmp redirect

route-redistribute active-routes-only

vrf vrf2

    rd 2:12

    route-target export 2:100 evpn

    route-target import 2:100 evpn

cli-session

    timeout 0

!

!

    vsx-sync

    !

    10 permit any any 225.1.0.0/255.255.0.0

    20 permit any any 225.2.1.0/255.255.255.0

    30 permit any any 225.2.2.0/255.255.255.0

    40 permit any any 232.0.0.0/255.0.0.0

vlan 1

    ip igmp snooping enable

    ip igmp snooping enable

    ip igmp snooping enable

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 1402

    ip igmp snooping enable

vlan 752,2001-2002,3001-3002

virtual-mac 00:11:00:00:00:11

evpn

    redistribute local-mac

    redistribute local-svi

        rd auto

        route-target export 501:141

        redistribute host-route

    vni 2100

        vlan 501
    vni 2101

Public

Site-2-Border-vtep-Secondary 1862

routing

vsx

    system-mac 00:11:00:00:00:11

    inter-switch-link lag 120

    role secondary

    keepalive peer 45.1.1.1 source 45.1.1.2

ip prefix-list test_1 seq 10 deny 192.170.1.255/32

ip prefix-list test_1 seq 20 deny 193.170.1.255/32

ip prefix-list test_1 seq 40 deny 197.170.1.255/32

ip prefix-list test_1 seq 50 deny 198.170.1.255/32

ip prefix-list test_1 seq 60 permit 4.4.4.50/32

ip prefix-list test_1 seq 70 permit 8.8.8.8/32

!

!

!

!

route-map tag_bgp_ospf permit seq 10

     set tag 220

route-map tag_ospf_bgp deny seq 10

     match tag 210

route-map tag_ospf_bgp deny seq 20

     match tag 220

route-map tag_ospf_bgp permit seq 30

route-map test_1 permit seq 10

     match ip address prefix-list test_1

!

router ospf 1

    router-id 112.13.1.3

    distance 250

    redistribute connected

    redistribute local loopback

    redistribute bgp route-map test_1
    area 0.0.0.0

router ospf 1 vrf vrf1

    router-id 112.13.1.5

    distance 250

    redistribute connected

    redistribute local loopback

    redistribute bgp route-map tag_bgp_ospf

    area 0.0.0.0

router ospf 1 vrf vrf2

    router-id 112.13.1.5

    distance 250

Public

Site-2-Border-vtep-Secondary 1863

redistribute connected

    redistribute local loopback

    redistribute bgp route-map tag_bgp_ospf

    area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 65536

    bgp router-id 112.13.1.3

    neighbor 4.4.4.4 remote-as 65536

    neighbor 4.4.4.4 update-source loopback 114

    neighbor 40.1.2.1 remote-as 65535

    neighbor 40.1.4.1 remote-as 65535

    neighbor 45.1.1.1 remote-as 65536

    neighbor 85.85.85.85 remote-as 65536

    neighbor 85.85.85.85 update-source loopback 114

    neighbor 193.170.1.255 remote-as 65535

    neighbor 193.170.1.255 ebgp-multihop 5

    neighbor 193.170.1.255 update-source loopback 113

    neighbor 194.170.1.255 remote-as 65535

    neighbor 194.170.1.255 ebgp-multihop 5

    neighbor 194.170.1.255 update-source loopback 113

    neighbor 197.170.1.255 remote-as 65537

    neighbor 197.170.1.255 ebgp-multihop 5

    neighbor 197.170.1.255 update-source loopback 113

    neighbor 198.170.1.255 remote-as 65537

    neighbor 198.170.1.255 ebgp-multihop 5

    neighbor 198.170.1.255 update-source loopback 113

    address-family ipv4 unicast

        neighbor 40.1.2.1 activate

        neighbor 40.1.4.1 activate

        neighbor 45.1.1.1 activate

        redistribute connected

        redistribute local loopback

    exit-address-family
    address-family l2vpn evpn

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 next-hop-self

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 route-reflector-client

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 send-community extended

        neighbor 4.4.4.4 activate

        neighbor 85.85.85.85 next-hop-self

        neighbor 85.85.85.85 route-reflector-client

        neighbor 85.85.85.85 send-community extended

        neighbor 85.85.85.85 activate

        neighbor 193.170.1.255 send-community extended

        neighbor 193.170.1.255 activate

Public

Site-2-Border-vtep-Secondary 1864

neighbor 194.170.1.255 send-community extended

        neighbor 194.170.1.255 activate

        neighbor 197.170.1.255 send-community extended

        neighbor 197.170.1.255 activate

        neighbor 198.170.1.255 send-community extended

        neighbor 198.170.1.255 activate

    exit-address-family

!

    vrf vrf1

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

            redistribute local loopback

            redistribute ospf route-map tag_ospf_bgp

        exit-address-family

!

    vrf vrf2

        address-family ipv4 unicast

            redistribute connected

            redistribute local loopback

            redistribute ospf route-map tag_ospf_bgp

        exit-address-family

!

router pim vrf vrf1

    enable

    pim-ssm

router pim vrf vrf2

    enable

    pim-ssm

    pim-ssm range-access-list pim-ssm-range-acl-vrf2

https-server vrf mgmt

Site2-fabric1-BorderRR-primary# show run

hostname Site2-fabric1-BorderRR-primary

power consumption-average-period 600

profile aggregation-leaf

cli-session

timeout 0

!

!

!

!
!

!

ssh server vrf mgmt

vlan 1

virtual-mac 00:00:10:21:00:03

interface mgmt

no shutdown

ip dhcp

interface lag 100

no shutdown

no routing

vlan trunk native 1 tag

Public

Site-2-Fabric1-BorderRR-primary 1989

vlan trunk allowed all

lacp mode active

interface 1/1/29

description port_Site2-fabric1-Access-vtep-1

no shutdown

ip address 70.1.1.1/30

ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface 1/1/33

description port_Site2-fabric1-BorderRR-secondary

no shutdown

lag 100

interface 1/1/34

description port_Site2-fabric1-Access-vtep-2

no shutdown

ip address 70.1.2.1/30

ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface 1/1/35

description port_Site2-Shared-border-vsxsec

no shutdown

ip address 60.1.2.2/30

interface 1/1/36

description port_Site2-Shared-border-vsxpri

no shutdown

ip address 60.1.1.2/30

interface 1/1/37

description port_Site2-fabric1-BorderRR-secondary

no shutdown

ip address 192.168.100.1/30

interface loopback 0

ip address 12.0.1.1/32

ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface loopback 1

ip address 12.1.1.1/32

ip ospf 1 area 0.0.0.0

interface vxlan 1

vsx

system-mac 00:00:01:00:00:00

inter-switch-link lag 100

role primary

vsx-sync evpn mgmd-global vsx-global

!

!

!

Public

Site-2-Fabric1-BorderRR-primary 1990

!

route-map rmap1 permit seq 50

!

router ospf 1

router-id 12.0.1.1

distance 250

redistribute connected

redistribute local loopback

area 0.0.0.0

router bgp 65002

bgp router-id 12.0.1.1

neighbor 11.0.1.1 remote-as 65001

neighbor 11.0.1.1 ebgp-multihop 5

neighbor 11.0.1.1 update-source loopback 0

neighbor 11.0.1.2 remote-as 65001

neighbor 11.0.1.2 ebgp-multihop 5

neighbor 11.0.1.2 update-source loopback 0

neighbor 13.0.1.1 remote-as 65002

neighbor 13.0.1.1 update-source loopback 0

neighbor 14.0.1.1 remote-as 65002

neighbor 14.0.1.1 update-source loopback 0

neighbor 60.1.1.1 remote-as 65001

neighbor 60.1.1.1 ebgp-multihop 5

neighbor 60.1.2.1 remote-as 65001

neighbor 60.1.2.1 ebgp-multihop 5

neighbor 70.1.1.2 remote-as 65002

neighbor 70.1.2.2 remote-as 65002

address-family ipv4 unicast

neighbor 60.1.1.1 activate

neighbor 60.1.2.1 activate

neighbor 70.1.1.2 activate

neighbor 70.1.2.2 activate
redistribute connected

redistribute local loopback

exit-address-family

address-family l2vpn evpn

neighbor 11.0.1.1 next-hop-unchanged

neighbor 11.0.1.1 send-community extended

neighbor 11.0.1.1 activate

neighbor 11.0.1.2 next-hop-unchanged

neighbor 11.0.1.2 send-community extended

neighbor 11.0.1.2 activate

neighbor 13.0.1.1 route-reflector-client

Public

Site-2-Fabric1-BorderRR-primary 1991

neighbor 13.0.1.1 send-community extended

neighbor 13.0.1.1 activate

neighbor 14.0.1.1 route-reflector-client

neighbor 14.0.1.1 send-community extended

neighbor 14.0.1.1 activate

exit-address-family

!

Syntax

show ip mroute <GROUP ADDRESS>

            <SOURCE ADDRESS> [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] detail

Description

Shows multicast routing information from a border router where traffic from a local site is forwarded to
a remote site. Optionally, you can show specific information by VRF. If no options are specified, it shows
information for the default VRF.

Parameter

all‐vrfs

Description

Shows all PIM neighbors information.

vrf <VRF‐NAME>

Shows PIM neighbor information for a specific VRF.

Public

Multicast VXLAN Multi-fabric commands 2089

Usage

The Intra-Inter Forwarded VTEP(s) tunnel field denotes which tunnel the traffic is routed to. This includes
the intra or inter VTEPs based on where the joins are seen. If there is more than one site where the traffic
needs to be forwarded, the corresponding site's VTEPs are listed. The Intra-Inter Forwarded VTEP(s) field is
only visible at the Border router.

There are no outgoing interfaces listed in these Mroutes as they are routing withing the same L3VNI logical
interface. If there are any local receivers at the border routers that are extended via L2/L3 extensions, then
the same Mroute is updated with the outgoing list.

Examples

Multicast route where the border is routing from one VTEP to another VTEP. In this case, VTEP 1.1.1.1 to
3.3.3.3 in L3VNI:

switch# show ip mroute 225.0.0.1 100.100.1.4 all-vrfs detail

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : red

Group Address                 : 225.0.0.1

Source Address                : 100.100.1.4

Neighbor                      : 1.1.1.1

Incoming interface            : vni10000

Intra-Inter Forwarded VTEP(s) : 3.3.3.3

Multicast Routing Protocol    : PIM-SM

Unicast Routing Protocol      : BGP

Metric                        : 0

Metric Pref                   : 200

Uptime (HH:MM:SS)             : 00:00:51
Multicast route where the border is routing from one VTEP to another VTEP in addition to an L3 interface:

switch# show ip mroute 225.0.0.1 100.100.1.4 all-vrfs detail

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : red

Group Address                 : 225.0.0.1

Source Address                : 100.100.1.4
Neighbor                      : 1.1.1.1

Incoming interface            : vni10000

Intra-Inter Forwarded VTEP(s) : 3.3.3.3

Multicast Routing Protocol    : PIM-SM

Unicast Routing Protocol      : BGP

Metric                        : 0

Metric Pref                   : 200

Uptime (HH:MM:SS)             : 00:00:51

Outgoing Interface List

Interface       State

Public

show ip mroute detail 2090

----------------------------

vlan1675        forwarding
Multicast route where the output is at the source connected leaf router. In this case, traffic ingresses on
SVI10 and is routed to VTEP 2.2.2.2:

switch# show ip mroute 225.0.0.1 100.100.1.4 all-vrfs detail

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : red

Group Address                 : 225.0.0.1

Source Address                : 100.100.1.4

Neighbor                      :

Incoming interface            : vlan10

Multicast Routing Protocol    : PIM-SM

Unicast Routing Protocol      : connected

Metric                        : 1

Metric Pref                   : 1

Uptime (HH:MM:SS)             : 00:00:56

Outgoing Interface List

Interface       State          Vteps

----------------------------------------

vni10000        forwarding     2.2.2.2
Multicast route where there is mixed L2-L3 VNI routing. In this case, traffic ingressed from VTEP 1.1.1.1 on
L3VNI is routed to SVI 4007(L2VNI):

switch# show ip mroute 225.0.0.1 100.100.1.4 all-vrfs detail

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : red

Group Address                 : 225.0.0.1

Source Address                : 100.100.1.4

Neighbor                      : 1.1.1.1

Incoming interface            : vni10000

Intra-Inter Forwarded VTEP(s) :

Multicast Routing Protocol    : PIM-SM
Unicast Routing Protocol      : BGP

Metric                        : 0

Metric Pref                   : 200

Uptime (HH:MM:SS)             : 00:00:51

Outgoing Interface List

Interface       State

------------------------------

vlan4007        forwarding

Public

show ip mroute detail 2091

10.12

Modification

Command introduced

Command Information

Platforms

Command context

Authority

Operator ( > ) or Manage
r ( # )

Operators or Administrators or local user group members with
execution rights for this command. Operators can execute this c
ommand from the operator context (>) only.

6400

8100

8325

8325P

8360

10000

ip multicast multi-fabric border

Syntax

ip multicast multi-fabric border

no ip multicast multi-fabric border

Description

Enables PIM's border router functionality. This command must be enabled on the border router when
iBGP-eBGP is not used.

The no form of the command disables forwarding of multicast data on the interface.

NOTE
This configuration is global and is applicable to all host's VRFs configured in that
router.

Examples

Configuring ip multicast multi-fabric border command:

Public

ip multicast multi-fabric border 2092

switch# configure terminal

switch(config)#

switch(config)# ip multicast multi-fabric border <cr>

switch(config)# no ip multicast multi-fabric border <cr>

switch(config)#

switch(config)# ipv6 multicast multi-fabric border <cr>

switch(config)# no ipv6 multicast multi-fabric border <cr>

Command History

Release

10.13

Modification

Command introduced

Command Information

Platforms

Command context

Authority

config‐if‐vlan

Administrators or local user group members with execution righ
ts for this command.

6400

8325

8325P

8360

ip multicast multi-fabric identifier

Syntax

ip multicast multi-fabric identifier-value <IDENTIFIER VALUE> <cr>

no ip multicast multi-fabric <IDENTIFIER-VALUE>

Description

Configures multicast fabric identifier (MFID) that uniquely represents the fabric/site to which this leaf router
belongs. Shown in the PIM hello option field, this command is used by the border router to associate the
neighbor to the fabric MFID. This command is applicable to all the regular leaf routers and not applicable at
the shared border router.

Public

ip multicast multi-fabric identifier 2093

The no form of the command disables forwarding of multicast data on the interface.

NOTE

The same MFID value must be configured on all leaf routers belonging to the
same fabric. However, the same MFID value cannot be reused between fabrics
attached to the same border.

Configuration of an Identifier is optional on the VTEPs. If there is no manually
configured Identifier, it will automatically use and announce the local AS Number
as Identifier.

Parameter

Description

<IDENTIFIER VALUE>

Configures the given value as MFID

Examples

Configuring ip multicast multi-fabric identifier-value command:

switch(config)#

switch(config)# ip multicast multi-fabric identifier <identifier-value> <cr>

switch(config)# no ip multicast multi-fabric identifier <identifier-value>

<cr>

switch(config)#

switch(config)# ipv6 multicast multi-fabric identifier <identifier-value>

<cr>

switch(config)# no ipv6 multicast multi-fabric identifier <identifier-

value> <cr>
Configuring MFID value of 1001 on two VTEPs using the same fabric:

On VTEP1:

vtep1(config)#

vtep1(config)# ip multicast multi-fabric identifier 1001 <cr>
On VTEP2:

vtep2(config)#

vtep2(config)# ip multicast multi-fabric identifier 1001 <cr>

10.13

Debugging and Troubleshooting

The following details the troubleshooting approach and the most common commands to be used for
debugging any multicast traffic issues in multi-fabric deployments.

NOTE

Most of the commands listed here are not just limited to multi-fabric and can be
used for other regular PIM networks.

Subtopics

Check underlay network reachability and VXLAN tunnel between VTEPs
Verify split-horizon rules are disabled at the Border VTEP
Validate PIM Neighbors at the Border VTEP
Validate MRoutes at the Border VTEP
Multicast receivers not receiving traffic
Troubleshooting once the RPF Neighbor towards the RP/Source is found
Troubleshooting Multicast Shared Border Router Issues

Check underlay network reachability and VXLAN tunnel between
VTEPs

Public

Debugging and Troubleshooting 2095

h

Procedure

1.  Ensure that the tunnel source/destination loopback IPs are correctly advertised in the underlay network.

2.  Use extended pings on Border VTEPs to confirm network connectivity between tunnel source/

destination loopback IPs.

3.  Fix underlay connectivity issues if any.

4.  Ensure that the VXLAN tunnels are discovered, and they are operational.

Results

The following is an example of how the interface VXLAN VTEP should appear:

Border_VTEP# show interface vxlan vtep

Source      Destination      Origin   Status          VNI       Routing

VLAN   VRF

----------- -------------- -------- --------------- --------- ---------

----   ----

2.2.2.2     3.3.3.3        evpn     operational     100001    enabled

--     VRF1

2.2.2.2     3.3.3.3        evpn     operational     100002    enabled

--     VRF2

2.2.2.2     1.1.1.1        evpn     operational     100001    enabled

--     VRF1

2.2.2.2     1.1.1.1        evpn     operational     100002    enabled

--     VRF2
If the EVPN tunnel is down, ensure that the correct EVPN configurations are used.

Verify split-horizon rules are disabled at the Border VTEP

Procedure

Ensure that Border VTEPS are configured with  dyn-vxlan-tunnel-bridging-mode ibgp-eb
gp  to disable the split-horizon rules.

Results

NOTE
In the case of multicast, split-horizon rules must be disabled for both L2VNI
and L3VNI at the Border VTEP. Without enabling  dyn-vxlan-tunnel-b
ridging-mode ibgp-ebgp  multicast routing at the Border VTEP is not
functional and operates as a regular border VTEP.

Public

Verify split-horizon rules are disabled at the Bor... 2096

The following is an example of a configuration with dyn-vxlan-tunnel-bridging-mode ibgp-ebgp enabled:

Border_VTEP# show running-config evpn

evpn dyn-vxlan-tunnel-bridging-mode ibgp-ebgp

Validate PIM Neighbors at the Border VTEP

Procedure

Ensure that the Border VTEP learns the intra-site PIM neighbors as well as all other the inter-site Border
VTEPs on the L3VNI interface.

Results

For an L3VNI neighbor, neighbor IP is the underlay VTEP IP in the Default VRF and this is the same for all
the tenant VRFs.

The following example shows the intra and inter PIM neighbor learned on the L3VNI interface:

Border_VTEP# show ip pim neighbor vrf VRF1

PIM Neighbor

VRF : VRF1

Total number of neighbors : 2

IP Address : 1.1.1.1

Interface : vni100001

Up Time (HH:MM:SS) : 00:33:30

Expire Time (HH:MM:SS) : 00:03:01

DR Priority : 1

Hold Time (HH:MM:SS) : 00:03:30

IP Address : 3.3.3.3

Interface : vni100001

Up Time (HH:MM:SS) : 00:33:30
Expire Time (HH:MM:SS) : 00:03:01

DR Priority : 1

Hold Time (HH:MM:SS) : 00:03:30

DR Priority : 1

Hold Time (HH:MM:SS) : 00:03:30

Validate MRoutes at the Border VTEP

Public

Validate PIM Neighbors at the Border VTEP 2097

Ensure multicast routes are formed correctly across VTEPS. The following MRoutes are taken from Border
VTEPs shown in Figure 1.

Figure 1. Receiver and source at different sites

Subtopics

Site-A Border VTEP
Site-B Border VTEP

Site-A Border VTEP

The route entry indicates that it is routing traffic received on the Incoming Interface vni10000(1.1.1.1)
to vni10000(3.3.3.3). The Intra-Inter Forwarded VTEP(s) field in the example below indicates the egress
tunnels where the traffic is forwarded in additional to any additional Outgoing list if it has. Thus, traffic from
Site-A Local border VTEP is forwarded to Site-B’s Border VTEP via tunnel 3.3.3.3.

Border_VTEP# show ip mroute 225.1.1.1 100.100.1.4  vrf red detail

Group Address                             : 225.1.1.1

Source Address                            : 100.100.1.4

Neighbor                                  : 1.1.1.1

Incoming interface                        : vni10000

Intra-Inter Forwarded VTEP(s)             : 3.3.3.3

Multicast Routing Protocol                : PIM-SM

Unicast Routing Protocol                  : BGP

Metric                                    : 0

Metric Pref                               : 200

Uptime (HH:MM:SS)                         : 00:00:51

Public

Site-A Border VTEP 2098

Site-B Border VTEP

The route entry indicates that it is routing traffic received on the Incoming Interface vni10000(3.3.3.3)
to vni10000(4.4.4.4). The Intra-Inter Forwarded VTEP(s) field in the example below indicates the egress
tunnels where the traffic is forwarded in additional to any additional Outgoing list it has. Thus, traffic from
Site-A’s Border VTEP is forwarded to Site-B's Local VTEP via tunnel 4.4.4.4.

Border_VTEP# show ip mroute 225.1.1.1 100.100.1.4  vrf red detail

Group Address                             : 225.1.1.1

Source Address                            : 100.100.1.4

Neighbor                                  : 2.2.2.2

Incoming interface                        : vni10000

Intra-Inter Forwarded VTEP(s)             : 4.4.4.4

Multicast Routing Protocol                : PIM-SM

Unicast Routing Protocol                  : BGP

Metric                                    : 0

Metric Pref                               : 200

Uptime (HH:MM:SS)                         : 00:00:51

Multicast receivers not receiving traffic

Procedure

1.  Check if Mroutes are created from multicast source to client with the show ip mroute command.

2.  Verify that the incoming and outgoing interfaces and or Intra-inter forwarded VTEPS reflect the path

the traffic should take.

3.

If the Mroutes are not present at any VTEP or if the outgoing lists/Intra-Inter Forwarded VTEPS break
the path in any VTEP, start triaging it from the Receiver DR VTEP or from the Source DR VTEP side.

Subtopics

Troubleshooting from the reciver designated VTEP (DR)
Checking the RP for the affected flow
Checking if the VTEP has a reverse path to reach RP or Multicast Source

Troubleshooting from the reciver designated VTEP (DR)

Once the receiver DR is identified ensure that IGMP joins are present in the Receiver VLAN and check the
last IGMP join Uptime in the “show ip igmp snooping” command. If IGMP joins are not present, refer the
IGMP troubleshooting section.

Public

Site-B Border VTEP 2099

Checking the RP for the affected flow

Identify the RP for the given multicast flow using show ip pim rp 235.1.1.1 vrf red.

In the example below, the selected RP for the given group is 235.1.1.1 to 31.1.1.1 as identified by show
ip pim rp 235.1.1.1 vrf red. If show ip pim rp 235.1.1.1 vrf red cannot find an RP, refer to the RP
troubleshooting section.

Site-1-VTEP# show ip pim rp 235.1.1.1 vrf red

VRF: red

PIM-SM Group-to-Resultant RP Mapping Information

Group Address          RP Address         RP Type        Up time (HH:MM:SS)

----------------       ---------------    ------------

--------------------

235.1.1.1              31.1.1.1           bsr            06:18:33

Checking if the VTEP has a reverse path to reach RP or Multicast
Source

Procedure

1.

Identify if the RPF path to the RP, Source or any other interested IP by PIM can be identified using  sho
w ip pim rpf 31.1.1.1 vrf red . In the example below, the RPF nexthop neighbor towards
the RP IP 31.1.1.1 is found to be the 2.2.2.2 (Border VTEP).

2.  Ensure that there is a valid ‘RPF Nexthop’ and a ‘PIM neighbor’ as highlighted below..

Results

Site-1-VTEP# show ip pim rpf 31.1.1.1 vrf red
Multicast RPF Details

Origin Codes: C - connected, SM - static-multicast, SU - static-unicast

              O - OSPF, B - BGP, R - RIP

VRF: red

IP Address  RPF Interface  RPF Nexthop PIM Neighbor  PRF Route/Mask

Origin  In Use

----------  -------------- ----------- ------------  --------------

------  ------

31.1.1.1    -              2.2.2.2     yes           31.1.1.1/32

B       yes

Similarly, check if the VTEP has a path to reach the source.

NOTE

This command only displays IPs that are known by the PIM daemon. For example,
the source traffic is not received bia the RPT, so the VTEP has no knowledge
about the Source IP, and as a result, the output returns empty.

In this example, the Source IP for which the RPF found is 100.50.1.2:

Site-1-VTEP# show ip pim rpf 100.50.1.2 vrf red

Multicast RPF Details

Origin Codes: C - connected, SM - static-multicast, SU - static-unicast

              O - OSPF, B - BGP, R - RIP

VRF: red

IP Address  RPF Interface  RPF Nexthop PIM Neighbor  PRF Route/Mask

Origin  In Use

----------  -------------- ----------- ------------  --------------

------  ------

100.50.1.2  -              2.2.2.2     yes           100.50.1.0/24

B       yes
If the PIM Neighbor field indicates a 'no', then ensure that PIM is enabled on the link segment and a PIM
neighbor with the same IP is learnt. To verify if a PIM neighbor exists with RPF Nexthop IP, use  show ip
pim neighbor 2.2.2.2 vrf red , as shown in the example below:

Site-1-VTEP# show ip pim neighbor 2.2.2.2 vrf red

PIM Neighbor

VRF                      : red

IP Address               : 2.2.2.2

Interface                : vni10000

Up Time (HH:MM:SS)       : 03:20:32

Expire Time (HH:MM:SS)   : 00:03:30

DR Priority              : 1

Hold Time (HH:MM:SS)     : 00:03:30
If it is found that there is a valid neighbor towards the RP/Source, but the RPF Nexthop is still empty
indicating an underlying unicast routing issue. In most cases, the unicast route might be missing, which can
be verfied with  show ip route 31.1.1.1 vrf red .

This can be shown in the example below:

Site-1-VTEP# show ip pim route 31.1.1.1 vrf red

VRF: red

    Prefix      : 31.1.1.1/32            VRF (egress)      : -
    Nexthop     : 2.2.2.2                Interface         : -

    Origin      : bgp                    Type              : bgp evpn

    Distance    : 200                    Metric            : 0

Public

Checking if the VTEP has a reverse path to reach R... 2101

Age         : 03h:23m:39s            Tag               : 0

    Encap type  : vxlan                  Encap Details     : 13vni 10000

Troubleshooting once the RPF Neighbor towards the RP/Source is
found

The RPF Neighbor VTEP is the immediate upstream VTEP for traffic where the PIM joins are sent, log in to
the RPF nexthop VTEP and repeat the troubleshooting steps as required. At each VTEP starting from the
Receiver DR through the Source to ensure that there are corresponding Mroutes for the flow.

Subtopics

Verify if the Source DR has successfully registered the flow with RP
Troubleshooting Source DR registration issues with RP
Troubleshooting RP, RP-Set and BSR Election issues
Troubleshooting multicast on a Two site Multi-fabric topology

Verify if the Source DR has successfully registered the flow with RP

Execute sh ip pim rp-registered-sources vrf red to check if the RP has learned a specific flow from the
Source DR via the PIM register message.

If the corresponding flow is not listed, it indicates a PIM register issue between the source DR and the RP. In
that case, proceed with the next section to determine the cause.

SW1# sh ip pim rp-registered-sources vrf red

Multicast flows registered with this RP

VRF Name: red

Total number of entries: 5

Source Address           Group Address          RP Address

-------------------      -----------------      ---------------

15.1.1.2                 225.1.1.1              1.1.1.1

15.1.1.2                 225.1.1.2              1.1.1.1

15.1.1.2                 225.1.1.3              1.1.1.1

15.1.1.2                 225.1.1.4              1.1.1.1

15.1.1.2                 225.1.1.5              1.1.1.1

Troubleshooting Source DR registration issues with RP

In many cases, the problem lies with the Source traffic registration from Source DR with the RP. The most
common symptoms include missing of Mroutes at the Source DR VTEP or high CPU utilization at the source
DR. In VXLAN networks, the register packet (the unicast packet with destination IP as RP IP) is further
encapsulated with the VXLAN header and is sent to the RP.

NOTE

The Source DR should have a valid route entry towards the RP IP and a virtual-
mac must be configured at the RP.

It is good practice to ensure that the virtual-mac is configured on all PIM enabled VTEPs for aiding the Tx/Rx
of unicast PIM control packets (Register, Register Stop, and CRP Advertisement). Both Unicast route (show
ip route) and PIM RPF (show ip pim rpf) show commands can be used to verify the unicast reachability.

In this example, show ip pim rpf 31.1.1.1 vrf red is used to check if the RPF path is used to reach RP
31.1.1.1 on source DR VTEP.

Site-2-VTEP# show ip pim rpf 31.1.1.1 vrf red

Multicast RPF Details

Origin Codes: C - connected, SM - static-multicast, SU - static-unicast

              O - OSPF, B - BGP, R - RIP

VRF: red

IP Address RPF Interface  RPF Nexthop  PIM Neighbor  RPF Route/Mask

Origin  In Use

----------------------------------------------------------------------------

-------

31.1.1.1   -              3.3.3.3      yes           31.1.1.1/32

B       yes

Site-2-VTEP#
From the RP check the RPF path to reach the Source DR Interface IP. In the example below, the Source DR IP
is 100.50.1.1.

Site-2-BGW# show ip pim rpf 100.50.1.1 vrf red
Multicast RPF Details

Origin Codes: C - connected, SM - static-multicast, SU - static-unicast

              O - OSPF, B - BGP, R - RIP

VRF: red

IP Address  RPF Interface  RPF Nexthop PIM Neighbor  RPF Route/Mask

Origin  In Use

----------------------------------------------------------------------------

------

100.50.1.1  -              4.4.4.4     yes           100.50.1.0/24

B       yes
In the RP interface, the register RX packet counter can be verified. If the registration is successful, the
counter is nonzero and increments at about one packet per flow in a minute. However, if it is not

Public

Troubleshooting Source DR registration issues with... 2103

incrementing or if increments at a high rate, it indicates that the registration is not complete between
the Source DR and RP (Register stop is not sending yet, or is being dropped in transit). In that case, register
packets need to be captured between the Source DR and RP to verify that the Null Register and Register
Stop are exchanged.

In the example below, the RP interface is loopback 10.

Site-2-BGW# show ip pim interface loopback10 counters

Interface  : loopback10

VRF        : red

                                         Rx        Tx        Rx Drops

                                         --------  --------  --------

Hello                                    0         0         0

BSM                                      0         0         0

Register                                 569       0         0

SSM Register                             0         0         0

Register Stop                            0         0         0

SSM Register Stop                        0         0         0

Join/Prune                               0         0         0

SSM Join/Prune                           0         0         0

C-RP Advertisement                       0         0         0

Graft                                    0         0         0

Graft Ack                                0         0         0

Assert                                   0         0         0

State Refresh                            0         0         0

Site-2-BGW#

Troubleshooting RP, RP-Set and BSR Election issues

For an active multicast group, if the RP cannot be found with show ip pim rp 235.1.1.1 vrf red, then it could
be an issue with the BSM RP-set learned or an issue in the local Static-RP configuration based on whether
candidate RP or Static RP is configured.

Public

Troubleshooting RP, RP-Set and BSR Election issues 2104

To verify if a valid RP-Set learned information from EBSR or from Static RP configuration, use show ip pim
rp-set vrf red. If there are n o matching RP addresses for the interested Group Address Prefix, check the
Candidate RP VTEP configuration to include the group prefix. However, if show ip pim rp-set vrf red does
not return any group prefix, it could be an Elected BSR information issue.

Site-1-VTEP# show ip pim rp-set vrf red

VRF: redt

Status and Counters - PIM-SM Learned RP-Set Information

Group Address    Group Mask       RP Address       Hold Time  Expire Time

Priority

---------------  ---------------  ---------------  ---------  -----------

--------

224.0.0.0        240.0.0.0        31.1.1.1         150        118

192
To verify the VTEP in question has the knowledge of the Elected BSR, use show ip pim bsr elected vrf red
and look for the E-BSR Address field. If there are none, then this VTEP is yet to learn the EBSR information
by which only the RP-Set info is learnt. Validate that the VTEP has a valid PIM neighbor to the RPF interface
to the EBSR Address. If not present, follow the same steps that are followed in the RPF Neighbor section.
The interface BSM counters can also be checked to see if this VTEP is receiving BSM packets on the RPF
interface.

NOTE

PIM interface counters are not supported in the case of L3VNI interface, so if the
RPF interface is L3VNI interface, a packet capture might be needed.

Site-1-VTEP# show ip pim bsr elected vrf red

Status and Counters - PIM-SM Elected Bootstrap Router Information

VRF                       : red

E-BSR Address             : 31.1.1.1

E-BSR Priority            : 0

E-BSR Hash Mask Length    : 30

E-BSR Up Time             : 3 hour 46 mins 49 secs

Next Bootstrap Message    : 1 mins 23 secs

Troubleshooting multicast on a Two site Multi-fabric topology

Figure 2 depicts a simplified multi-fabric topology with two sites (Site-1 & Site-2). The Route Reflectors (if
any) and the spines are omitted as they mostly carry the multicast traffic payload as VXLAN encapsulated
and act as a passthrough device and overlay PIM is not enabled on them.

Here, L3 Rop-Ext VTEP is a non-Vtep connected with Site-1 VSX Border VTEP by Rop-1 and Rop-2 Link.

•  Site1 VTEP connected with Site-1 Vsx border VTEP with mclag underlay connection.

•  Site2 Border VTEP and Site-1 Vsx border VTEP connected with mclag underlay connection.

Tunnel details:

•  Site1-Vsx-Border – Site1-VTEP = Intra-fabric type (ibgp).

•  Site1-Vsx-Border - L3 Rop Ext = Fabric terminated and overlay traffic routed to Default VRF.

•  Site1-Vsx-Border – Site2-Border = Inter-fabric type (ebgp).

•  Site2-Border - Site2-VTEP = Intra-fabric type (ibgp).

The Source is connected to Site2-VTEP and clients are attached to both L3Rop-Extension VTEP and Site1-
VTEP. RP is configured at Site2-border VTEP. When the Source starts the traffic, it registers with RP device
(Site-2-border VTEP) as unicast traffic by PIM register tunnel.

Figure 1. Simplified multi-fabric topology with two sites

The following section depicts the Mroute present at each hop from source to receiver VTEP and what to
expect and how to troubleshoot each scenario.

At Site2-VTEP 2106

About this task

This is a local VTEP where the multicast source is connected and it receives the traffic from a source-
connected port and forwards the traffic towards its immediate Border VTEP. Here, the downstream interface
is L3vni interface (Site Border vtep).

Procedure

1.

2.

If there are no mroutes entries here, it could be a Registration issue with RP device. Refer to Verify if the
Source Dr has successfully registered the flow with RP.

If Downstream Interface is empty, it could be No (S,G) Joins received on downstream port from [Rp
device/ clients device]. In this case, troubleshoot from the Client DR side towards the RP/Source.

3.  Verify that the Incoming Interface is the Source connected interface (here, vni10000).

4.  Verify that the Downstream Interfac’ has the L3VNI interface with the VTEP pointing towards the

Border VTEP IP (here, 3.3.3.3).

At Site2-Border VTEP

Here Traffic receives from upstream port [L3vni] of Intra-fabric and bridges to another border VTEP of
Inter-fabric.

If there aren’t any mroutes and since this is the RP VTEP, it could be:

Public

At Site2-Border VTEP 2107

•  Source Registration issue between Source DR and RP. Refer to Troubleshooting Source DR registration

issues with RP.

•  Joins are not received from the Site-1 Border VTEP where the receivers are present. Refer to

Troubleshooting Source DR registration issues with RP.

Traffic received from Site-2 VTEP on L3VNI as indicated in the Incoming Interface (vni10000) field is
routed to Site-1 Border VTEP as indicated in the Intra-Inter Forwared VTEP(s) (2.2.2.2) field.

Site-2-BGW# show ip mroute 235.1.1.1 100.50.1.2 vrf red detail

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF : red

Group Address                            : 235.1.1.1

Source Address                           : 100.50.1.2

Neighbor                                 : 4.4.4.4

Incoming interface                       : vni10000

Intra-Inter Forwarded VTEP(s)            : 2.2.2.2

Unicast Routing Protocol                 : BGP

Metric                                   : 0

Metric  Pref                             : 200

Uptime (HH:MM:SS)                        : 01:25:07

At Site1- Vsx Border VTEP

This VTEP acts as a fabric termination to an L3-Extension VTEP as well as a Border VTEP for Site-1 fabric.
The topology in Figure 2 has receivers joined from both L3-Extension VTEP and from the Site-1 regular
VTEP.

The data-path-auto-include interface configured here is VLAN199, which is mandatory since the L3-
Extension VTEPs are interconnected with the fabric with an ROP interface.

Since the interface between the L3-Extension VTEP and the Site-1’s Broder VSX is an ROP extension, joins
might come to either Primary or Secondary VSX VTEP but not both. In this example below, the joins are
received in the Secondary VSX VTEP. Furthermore, Site-2 source traffic might receive at either Primary
or Secondary VSX based on the undelay load balancing. In the example below, traffic has arrived at the
Secondary VSX as indicated by the‘Incoming interface vni10000 on VSX Secondary output. Traffic then
need to be routed to:

•

Its local VTEP where the receiver is present. This is identified from the ‘Intra-Inter Forwarded VTEP(s)’
1.1.1.1 VTEP which is Site-1 VTEP.

•  The L3 Extension VTEP that also has receivers for the same multicast flow. In this case, since the joins
are received on the Primary VSX, the secondary needs to use the data-path-auto-include interface to

Public

At Site1- Vsx Border VTEP 2108

forward the traffic to Primary VSX. Thus, the same mroute entry has data-path-auto-include VLAN199
in Downstream Interface.

NOTE

In this example, the traffic from Site-2 arrived at the Secondary VSX. For source
traffic that arrives at the Primary VSX (based on underlay load balancing), both
the L3-Extended and local VTEP Clients are served by the Primary Mroute since
the PIM joins are received there.

VSX-Secondary:
