Frequently Asked Questions

1. When does the ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include command need to be enabled?

This command is applicable only in the case of VSX VTEP, where Primary or Secondary has an L3 connection
(P2P or ROP) to the non-VXLAN routed network.

Public

Frequently Asked Questions 2113

2. Is orphan port on primary and secondary VSX VTEP supported?

Yes, orphan ports are supported only in the form of ROP and P2P ports.

NOTE
Additionally, datapath-auto-include needs to be provisioned between the VSX
peers in those scenarios.

3. Which command is specific to Border VTEP?

The dyn-vxlan-tunnel-bridging-mode ibgp-ebgp command is specific to the border VTEP. It must be
configured on the border router to allow multicast routing between iBGP and eBGP. Unlike for unicast
routing, this command must be enabled regardless of whether the VLAN is stretched across fabrics, ensuring
proper functioning of multicast on the border router.

4. Why are redistribute-local-mac or redistribute-local-svi commands required for IGMP/MLD?

IGMP/MLD running in a VSX VTEP is interested in the local MAC address of remote VTEPs for proper
synchronization of IGMP/MLD states between them. These commands are needed when at least one VSX
VTEP is present in the fabric and must be configured on all VTEPs (standalone and VSX VTEPs). These
commands are applicable for both single and multi-fabric topology.

Multicast Routing and Bridging

The multicast datapath feature is related to the forwarding engine, where it consolidates the information
received over a multicast control plane such as PIM or any other multicast routing protocol to formulate the
multicast forwarding information base. It is responsible for:

•  Forwarding multicast packets

•  Registering with the multicast routing protocols to learn the entry and status set by the control plane

•  Handling data-driven events that must be sent to the control plane

•  Maintaining the programming status of multicast routing and bridging entries

This section provides additional verification commands that can be used to verify L2 and L3 multicast
forwarding entries. Continue to utilize the verification commands in the PIM and IGMP chapters as required.

Supported platforms and standards

Multicast routing and bridging is supported by the 5420, 6200, 6300, 6400, 8100, 8320, 8325, 8360, 8400,
9300, 9300s,and 10000 Switch Series.

Subtopics

Multicast datapath commands for IPv4
Multicast datapath commands for IPv6

Public

Multicast Routing and Bridging 2114

Multicast datapath commands for IPv4

Select a command from the list in the left navigation menu.

Subtopics

show ip multicast bridging datapath
show ip multicast routing datapath

show ip multicast bridging datapath

Syntax

show ip multicast bridging datapath {group <GROUP-IP> {source <SOURCE-IP>

{vlan <VLAN-ID>}}}[vrf <VRF_NAME>] [vsx-peer]

Description

Displays the multicast bridge control forwarding entries on a device including replication details and
hardware programming status. Displays bridging datapath details for the specified multicast flow (the group,
source, VLAN, and VRF).

Parameter

Description

group <GROUP‐IP>

decimal number from 0 to 255.

Specifies a group IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is
a

Shows bridging datapath details for the specified group.

Specifies a source IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is
a decimal number from 0 to 255.

source <SOURCE‐IP>

Shows bridging datapath details for the specified source.

vlan <VLAN‐ID>

Specifies a VLAN. Values: 1‐4094.

Shows bridging datapath details for the specified VLAN.

Specifies a VRF.

Public

Multicast datapath commands for IPv4 2115

Parameter

vrf <VRF‐NAME>

vsx‐peer

Usage

Description

Shows datapath information for groups joined in the specified V
RF. If the <VRF‐NAME> is not specified, it shows the default V
RF information.

Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do
not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f
rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai
lable on switches that support VSX.

A multicast bridge control forwarding table comprises of the IP multicast destination address, source
address and its association with a list of ports on which the multicast packets are replicated in a Layer
2 domain. The switch floods/ replicates the multicast packet on these ports by matching the multicast
destination address, source address, and the virtual local network identifier (VLAN ID). The show ip
multicast bridging datapath command displays the multicast bridge control forwarding entries on a device
including replication details and hardware programming status.

Examples

Showing detailed bridging datapath information for the specified multicast flow:

switch# show ip multicast bridging datapath group 232.1.1.10 source

100.100.1.10 vlan 10

Multicast Bridging Datapath Details

VRF                        : default

Source                     : 100.100.1.10/32

Group                      : 232.1.1.10/32

Replication Group Index    : 70

Hardware Status            : active

Error Code                 : None

Retries for programming    : 0

Vlan                       : VLAN10

State

                   : forwarding

VNI                        : vni1000

State

                   : operational

Replication Details:

Tunnel Endpoints     State

----------------     ----------

2.2.2.2              operational

4.4.4.4              operational

show ip multicast routing datapath

Syntax

show ip multicast routing datapath {group <GROUP-IP> {source <SOURCE-IP>

{port <INCOMING-PORT>}}}[vrf <VRF_NAME>] [vsx-peer]

Public

show ip multicast routing datapath 2117

Description

Displays the multicast Layer 3 forwarding entries with replication details and hardware programming status.
Displays routing datapath details for the specified multicast flow (group, source, incoming port, and VRF).

Parameter

Description

group <GROUP‐IP>

decimal number from 0 to 255.

Specifies a group IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is
a

Shows datapath details for the specified group.

Specifies a source IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is
a decimal number from 0 to 255.

source <SOURCE‐IP>

Shows datapath details for the specified source.

port <INCOMING‐PORT>

Specifies the incoming port.

Shows datapath details for the specified port.

vrf <VRF‐NAME>

Specifies a VRF.

Shows datapath information for the specified VRF. If the <VRF
‐NAME> is not specified, it shows the default VRF information
.

Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do
not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f
rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai
lable on switches that support VSX.

vsx‐peer

Usage

A multicast routing protocol such as PIM populates the multicast Layer 3 forwarding table and forwards
multicast traffic to clients throughout the network. A multicast forwarding table comprises of the IP
multicast destination address, source address, interface on which the traffic is received, and the list of
interfaces on which multicast traffic is replicated. The show ip multicast routing datapath command
displays the multicast Layer 3 forwarding entries with replication details and hardware programming status.

Examples

Showing detailed routing datapath information for the specified multicast flow:

switch# show ip multicast routing datapath group 225.20.0.1 source 20.0.0.2
port vlan20

Multicast Routing Datapath Details

VRF                        : default

Source                     : 20.0.0.2/32

Public

Type                       : route

Replication Group Index    : 38923

Hardware Status            : active

Error Code                 : None

Retries for programming    : 0

Upstream Interface         : vlan20

State              : forwarding

Replication Details:

L2 Ports        State

-----------     ----------

1/1/4           forwarding

Downstream Interface       : vlan30

State              : forwarding

Replication Details:

L2 Ports        State

-----------     ----------

1/1/1           forwarding

Downstream Interface       : vlan40

State              : forwarding

Replication Details:

L2 Ports        State

-----------     ----------

1/1/2           forwarding

1/1/3           forwarding

NOTE

For more information on features that use this command, refer to the Multicast
Guide for your switch model.

A multicast bridge control forwarding table comprises of the IP multicast destination address, source

destination address, source address, and the virtual local network identifier (VLAN ID). The show ipv6

show ipv6 multicast bridging datapath 2122

show ipv6 multicast routing datapath

Syntax

show ipv6 multicast routing datapath {group <GROUP-IP> {source <SOURCE-IP>

{port <INCOMING-PORT>}}} [vrf <VRF_NAME>] [vsx-peer]

Description

Displays the multicast Layer 3 forwarding entries with replication details and hardware programming status.
Displays routing datapath details for the specified multicast flow (group, source, incoming port, and VRF).

Parameter

Description

group <GROUP‐IP>

Specifies a group IP address in IPv6 format (xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xx
xx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number from
0 to F.

Shows datapath details for the specified group.

Specifies a source IP address in IPv6 format (xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xx
xx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number from
0 to F.

Public

show ipv6 multicast routing datapath 2123

Parameter

Description

source <SOURCE‐IP>

Shows datapath details for the specified source.

port <INCOMING‐PORT>

Specifies the incoming port.

Shows datapath details for the specified port.

vrf <VRF‐NAME>

Specifies a VRF.

Shows datapath information for groups joined in the specified V
RF. If the <VRF‐NAME> is not specified, it shows the default V
RF information.

Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do
not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f
rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai
lable on switches that support VSX.

vsx‐peer

Usage

A multicast routing protocol such as PIM populates the multicast Layer 3 forwarding table and forwards
multicast traffic to clients throughout the network. A multicast forwarding table comprises of the IP
multicast destination address, source address, interface on which the traffic is received, and the list of
interfaces on which multicast traffic is replicated. The show ipv6 multicast routing datapath command
displays the multicast Layer 3 forwarding entries with replication details and hardware programming status.

Examples

Showing detailed routing datapath information for the specified multicast flow:

switch# show ip multicast routing datapath group 225.20.0.1 source 20.0.0.2

port vlan20

Multicast Routing Datapath Details

VRF                        : default

Source                     : 20.0.0.2/32
Group                      : 225.20.0.1/32

Primary Upstream Interface : vlan20

From                       : pim_sm

Type                       : route

Replication Group Index    : 38923

Hardware Status            : active

Error Code                 : None

Retries for programming    : 0

Upstream Interface         : vlan20

State              : forwarding

Replication Details:

L2 Ports        State

Public

show ipv6 multicast routing datapath 2124

-----------     ----------

1/1/4           forwarding

Downstream Interface       : vlan30

State              : forwarding

Replication Details:

L2 Ports        State

-----------     ----------

1/1/1           forwarding

Downstream Interface       : vlan40

State              : forwarding

Replication Details:

L2 Ports        State

-----------     ----------

1/1/2           forwarding

1/1/3           forwarding

NOTE

For more information on features that use this command, refer to the Multicast
Guide for your switch model.

Multicast Scenario-based Debugging

The following procedures describe workflows for debugging common multicast issues.

Issue: A user switching channels impacts traffic on other channels.

Figure 1. Workflow overview

Step one: check IGMP joins on the affected VLANs.

Static routes

NOTE

Static multicast routing is not supported on the 9300 switch series.

NOTE

Route summarization is not supported on the 8400 and 9300 switch series.

Static multicast routes can be configured by specifying the incoming interface, source, group, and outgoing
interfaces. Multicast static routes can be used to configure a static multicast flow and program it directly into
the IP multicast forwarding tables instead of running dynamic multicast protocols. Unlike PIM, this feature
does not depend on Unicast Routing Protocols to build the Multicast tree. Instead, it works as a standalone
Multicast Route provisioning feature that can interoperate with the dynamic Multicast protocols like PIM-SM
and L2 protocols like IGMP.

Subtopics

Protocol and Feature Details
Supported platforms and standards
Static multicast scenarios
Inter-operability
Considerations and Best Practices
Use cases
Static Route commands
Debugging and Troubleshooting
Frequently Asked Questions

Protocol and Feature Details

Static multicast routing avoids processing overhead on routers by providing the flexibility to program a
specific path for mutlicast traffic from the source to the client without having to rely on underlying protocols.
Static multicast routes require users to explicitly specify the incoming interface, source address, group
address, and the set of downstream interfaces where the traffic needs to be replicated.

A static multicast route for both IPv4 and IPv6 protocol stacks can be programmed in the following
configurations:

•  On a specified source. Traffic is forwarded from a specific source or a summarized entry for a specific

multicast group address from any source.

•  No specified outgoing interface. This is used if there is a need to drop traffic without relaying traffic to

any of the downstream interfaces or to bridge traffic on the upstream interface.

Public

Static routes 2156

A static multicast route is considered active as far as the incoming interface and the port(s) on which the
incoming traffic is replicated is operational. The interfaces that are down are not included in the forwarding
port list of the multicast entry programmed.

When a static multicast route is configured, it stays until the user removes it. It is recommended to use static
multicast routes for persistent traffic flows or in scenarios where resource usage needs to be optimized by
summarizing the static multicast route. Static multicast route does not timeout based on traffic inactivity and
the user should unconfigure the static multicast route configured promptly to avoid stale routes or unwanted
traffic misdirection.

To match on any source for a group, any can be specified, instead of a IP address for the source. Multiple
multicast routes having the same portset (incoming interface, group, outgoing interfaces and l2) can
be summarized to a single route to avoid multiple routes and efficiently utilize the hardware resources.
Summarization is enabled by default and is applied only on /32 or 0.0.0.0/0 multicast routes.

When a multicast static route is configured on the source connected router, PIM registration process for that
route will be suppressed and PIM joins will be dropped.

When a multicast static route is configured on the RP router, it sends a PIM register stop message if it
receives a PIM register message and clears pending registrations for that source and group. This feature can
be used for handling SSDP traffic in a network.

Summarized static routes can be used in the end-to-end path between multicast source and clients in such
cases. It is highly recommended to not use static multicast routes to replace PIM in existing deployments for
other group addresses and usecases.

The following is an example of a typical static multicast route:

[Incoming interface, Source, Group] > [Set of downstream interfaces]

In this example, the Incoming interface is the L3 interface from which the traffic is received on the router,
Source is the source IP address of the stream device, and Group is the multicast IP address of the group
for which the traffic is streamed. The downstream interfaces are the L3 interfaces on which PIM joins are
received or to which the receivers are connected.

Figure 1. Configuring summarized static multicast route to prevent high resource utilization

Public

Protocol and Feature Details 2157

Subtopics

Deployments
Static-only mode
Hybrid mode with PIM
Redistribution mode into PIM

Deployments

Static Multicast Routes can be used in static-only mode, hybrid mode with PIM, and Redistribution into PIM.

Static-only mode

Only multicast static routes are used to route multicast packets between Source and Client designated
router. All the devices are configured with static multicast routes for all the multicast flows, and PIM does not
need not be enabled on the network. IGMP/MLD is enabled on this network to learn joins from clients. This
may not be deployed in common and only small-sized networks where the multicast source and clients are
only a few hops apart should be configured to run in this mode. Dynamic multicast routing protocols like PIM
are not used in this network for multicast routing. IGMP/MLD is enabled on this network to learn joins from
clients.

Figure 1. Static-only mode

Public

Deployments 2158

Hybrid mode with PIM

In this mode, multicast static routes are configured on all devices between Source and Client designated
routers to route multicast packets for some specific flows. Dynamic multicast routing protocols, like PIM,
are also enabled in these devices to route multicast traffic for other flows. The multicast routers have both
static multicast routes and PIM mroutes programmed in the multicast forwarding table, but the entries are
mutually exclusive. IGMP/MLD is enabled on this network to learn joins from clients. Only the flows that have
scope of summarization or the flows for which the multicast network seldom changes can be picked to be
configured as static multicast routes in all the devices along the path.

In hybrid networks, the routers including RP and all routers towards Source from RP should be part of the
PIM enabled network. Static multicast routing can be enabled on the routers connected towards Client side.
If deployed in hybrid mode, all the border routers between Static and PIM networks operate in hybrid mode.
This mode works with PIM-SM, PIM-DM and PIM-SSM.

Public

Hybrid mode with PIM 2159

Figure 1. Hybrid mode with PIM

Redistribution mode into PIM

In this mode, a combination of PIM and multicast static routes are used in the network to route multicast
packets from Source to clients that are interested in multicast traffic. In this mode, the routers, including
RP and all routers towards Source from RP, should be part of the PIM enabled network. Static multicast
routing is enabled on the routers connected towards Client side with a redistributing router that is enabled
for static multicast route stitching the PIM and Static multicast route domains. IGMP/MLD is enabled on this
network to learn joins from clients. If deployed in redistribution mode, all the border routers between Static
and PIM networks should operate in redistribution mode and care should be taken while configuring to avoid
duplicate packets caused by multiple routers redistributing PIM joins for the same multicast flow. This mode
is recommended in cases where the device is connected towards the multicast receivers that are affected by
join/leave latency issues due to high multicast route scale.

Figure 1. Redistribution mode into PIM

Public

Redistribution mode into PIM 2160

Supported platforms and standards

Multicast static routes is supported by the 5420, 6200, 6300, 6400, 8100, 8320, 8325, 8360, 8400, and
10000 Switch Series. Summarized multicast static routes are not supported on the 8400 switch series.

Subtopics

Standards

Standards

The AOS-CX multicast static route is a proprietary feature and there are no existing standards.

Public

Supported platforms and standards 2161

Static multicast scenarios

Subtopics

Overlapping Static Multicast Routes
PIM + Static Scenarios
VSX Scenarios
ECMP scenarios

Overlapping Static Multicast Routes

Static multicast routes should be configured with care as they can cause traffic loss or duplicate traffic if
not properly configured. Configuring static multicast routes with overlapping source and group address on
a router can cause such issues. Overlapping static multicast routes occure when the source IP address and
the group IP address are matching or overlapping even if the upstream/incoming interface is different. A
summarized static route {*,G} always overlap with all the {S1..n, G} static multicast routes configured. If {*, G}
and {S, G} static multicast routes are configured on the same device, traffic from Source S always chooses
the {S, G} route to forward the traffic based on the longest prefix match (LPM).

Subtopics

Scenario 1: Traffic is dropped due to {S, G} preferrence over {*, G}
Scenario 2: Traffic is duplicated due to {S, G} and {*, G} from different upstreams

Scenario 1: Traffic is dropped due to {S, G} preferrence over {*, G}

In this scenario, a summarized static multicast route {*, G} is configured with the outgoing interface as
vlan40, while {S,G} entry is configured with the outgoing interface as vlan30 on the same device. Due to
longest prefix match (LPM), traffic from Source S is not forwarded to vlan40 as it always chooses the {S, G}
route configured. The multicast static route should be configured as {I, S, G} > vlan30, vlan40 where I is the
incoming interface.

Figure 1. Traffic drop due to {S, G} preferrence over {*, G}

Public

Static multicast scenarios 2162

Scenario 2: Traffic is duplicated due to {S, G} and {*, G} from
different upstreams

In this scenario, a summarized static multicast route {*, G} is configured on Router1 and {S, G} is configured
on Router2 where the downstream of these routers intersects at a certain point. Traffic coming on both
Router1 and Router2 forwards traffic to the downstream and the device where these traffic intersects sends
duplicate traffic downstream. Overlapping multicast static routes should not be configured in these cases to
avoid duplicate traffic.

Figure 1. Traffic is duplicated due to {S, G} and {*, G} from different upstreams

PIM + Static Scenarios

This section explains the multicast traffic flow and protocol behavior for various combinations of PIM and
Static multicast route enabled in a network. These scenarios are not recommended.

The following notations are used in this section:

•  Static {*, G}: Summarized static route “ip multicast-static-route <upstream_interface> any <group_ip ==

G> <downstream_interface>” configured on the device.

•  Static {S, G}: Summarized static route “ip multicast-static-route <upstream_interface> <source_ip == S>

<group_ip == G> <downstream_interface>” configured on the device.

•  PIM: PIM is enabled on all interfaces of the device.

•  PIM-SSM: PIM-SSM is enabled for the group G.

Public

PIM + Static Scenarios 2164

•  Redistribute: Static multicast routes are redistributed into PIM domain on this device.

•  Forward: Traffic sent from the Source is received by the Client successfully.

•  Drop: Traffic sent from the Source is not received by the Client.

•  RPT: Root Path Tree.

•  SPT: Source Path Tree.

Figure 1. Sample multicast network

Subtopics

PIM + Static without redistribution
PIM + Static with redistribution

Based on PIM configuration and alternate links, joins may be sent over PIM enabled paths even when static
multicast routes are configured. Configure static multicast routes carefully to avoid duplicate traffic.

VSX Scenarios
Static multicast route configuration on VSX based networks is supported. There are configuration
constraints related to static multicast routes on the primary and secondary devices in the VSX pair. On
a symmetric VSX network, traffic is always routed by the primary device on a mc-lag and the secondary
device programs bridge entries for multicast traffic. In cases where the the primary fails, the other device in
the VSX pair starts routing traffic on mc-lag.
The following notations are used in this section:
•  Symmetric Upstream: Symmetric upstream indicates the upstream interface for multicast traffic is the
same on both primary and secondary devices for the VSX pair.
•  Asymmetric Upstream (Primary): Asymmetric upstream indicates the upstream interface for multicast
traffic is not the same on both primary and secondary devices for the VSX pair. The multicast source is
connected on an upstream interface which is specific to VSX primary device.
| Public |     |     |     | VSX Scenarios |     | 2173 |
| ------ | --- | --- | --- | ------------- | --- | ---- |

•  Asymmetric Upstream (Secondary): Asymmetric upstream indicates the upstream interface for multicast
traffic is not the same on both primary and secondary devices for the VSX pair. The multicast source is
connected on an upstream interface which is specific to VSX secondary device.
•  Symmetric Downstream: Symmetric downstream indicates that the downstream interface for multicast
traffic is the same on both primary and secondary devices for the VSX pair. The multicast receivers are
reachable through the same interface from both VSX primary and secondary devices.
•  Asymmetric Downstream: Asymmetric downstream indicates that the downstream interface on which
the multicast receivers are connected is specific to either VSX primary or secondary device.
•  Route: Indicates configuration of static multicast route with an upstream interface and one of more
downstream interfaces.
•  Bridge: Indicates configuration of static multicast route with only upstream interface so that traffic
matching this flow will be bridged only on the ports of upstream interface.
Table 3, VSX Scenarios describes various scenarios and the configuration principle of static multicast route
in VSX deployments.
Table 1. VSX Scenarios

| ----------------- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | --- |
| Symmetric Upstrea | Route | Route | Route | Route |     |
m

ECMP scenarios

Static multicast routes are not recommended for use in deployments where ECMP towards upstream/
downstream is present, as they can cause duplicate traffic. It is not recommended to configure static
multicast routes on the networks where PIM solves duplicate packets with solutions such as Designated
Forwarder Election.

Subtopics

ECMP downstream
ECMP upstream with static multicast routes
ECMP upstream with PIM
ECMP downstream with summary route as backup

ECMP downstream

ECMP downstream occurs when the same multicast receivers are reachable from a router over multiple
paths. When a static multicast route is configured with multiple downstream interfaces on this device to

Public

ECMP scenarios 2192

route traffic through these paths, duplicate packets are seen on the multicast receiver. Such networks with
strict requirement of backup paths should not be considered for static multicast routes.

Figure 1. ECMP downstream on R1 with static multicast routes

ECMP upstream with static multicast routes

Consider a network with end-to-end static multicast routes configured to forward multicast traffic for a
specific flow. If there are multiple equal cost paths to reach the multicast source on a router, configuring
multicast routes with both these upstreams can cause traffic to be received from both paths, resulting in
duplicate packets.

Figure 1. ECMP upstream on R4 with static multicast routes

Public

ECMP upstream with static multicast routes 2193

In Figure 29, ECMP upstream on R4 with static multicast routes R4 is the device for which there is ECMP
upstream. The multicast static route configuration on routers R1, R3, and R4 are marked as invalid to
showcase the duplicate traffic data path. Refer to ECMP downstream with summary route as backup for
more information on achieving redundancy.

ECMP upstream with PIM

Duplicate traffic may be seen when static multicast route is redistributed into the upstream PIM domain.
If multiple PIM routers in the ECMP path receive joins directly from the redistributing router on the
downstream, duplicate traffic is received by the multicast receivers.

Figure 1. ECMP upstream with redistribute static multicast routes

Public

ECMP upstream with PIM 2194

ECMP downstream with summary route as backup

For a router with two ECMP paths to reach the multicast receiver(s) and if there is a unique source (and no
multiple sources) sending traffic to a specific group address, configuring an {S, G} route and a {*, G} route
for the same group proves redundancy in cases of local link failures. When such overlapping static multicast
routes are configured on a device, traffic is forwarded using the {S, G} entry based on the longest prefix
match on path1. If the interface configured as downstream for {S, G} goes down, {*, G} route is used for
traffic forwarding through the alternate path. Since this configuration cannot provide redundancy in case of
path failures and selects backup path only on local link failures, the application scenarios are very limited.

Figure 1. ECMP downstream with summary route as backup

Public

ECMP downstream with summary route as backup 2195

Inter-operability

Multicast routing protocols like PIM and IGMP are responsible for construction of the multicast delivery
tree on overlay networks. These traditional multicast routing protocols achieve this by exchanging mroutes
in control packets along with configuring policies across all devices in the network. Static multicast routes
interoperate with PIM, IGMP, and other vendors.

Subtopics

Inter-operability with PIM
Inter-operability with IGMP
Inter-operability with other vendors

Inter-operability with PIM

Subtopics

Arbitration with PIM
Redistribution with PIM

Arbitration with PIM

Arbitration with PIM is needed when a statically configured multicast route is also learnt by PIM. User
configured multicast routes take precedence over the PIM learnt routes. If a static multicast route is
configured for a flow and is active, PIM does not listen for any updates of this specific flow and does not
process the PIM control packets or corresponding data packets corresponding on the incoming configured
interface. If a summarized static mroute is configured, the multicast packets from any source or matching
PIM control packets for the con figured group address do not be processed by PIM. If PIM has already

Public

Inter-operability 2196

learned an mroute and a matching static multicast route is configured, the multicast route learned by PIM is
removed from the forwarding table and static multicast route is programmed.

Redistribution with PIM

Static multicast routes give the flexibility to the user to program a specific path for multicast traffic from
the source to the client without having to rely on the underlying protocols to build a multicast route. They
can be configured on all the routers in the path or on a specific section of routers. The remaining section of
routers can be configured to run the native PIM protocol. In Figure 1 static multicast routes are configured
on routers R4 and R5 whereas PIM protocol builds the multicast routes on routers R1 and R2. R3 acts as a
gateway where the static multicast routes terminate and PIM initiates.

Figure 1. Redistributing Static Mroutes into PIM domain

In Figure 1, on R3, a static mroute is programmed as follows:

Flow

S,G

Intercoming interface

Outgoing Interface

Iface 1

Iface 2

In Figure 1, on R4, a static mroute is programmed as follows:

Public

Redistribution with PIM 2197

Flow

S,G

Intercoming interface

Outgoing Interface

Iface 2

Iface 3

In the example in Figure 1, the PIM protocol is enabled on R3, but not on R4. The source registers to R2 (RP)
through the native PIM path. As a result, R1 and R2 contain the multicast routes. On R3, where the static
multicast rotues terminate, a redistribute command is configured to inform PIM about the static multicast
route. This ensures that PIM creates an Upstream (S,G) state and sends an S, G join to R2 through Iface1.
This join reaches R1, which forwards the source traffic to R2. R2 starts routing the (S, G) traffic to Iface1.
This traffic is routed on R3, R4, and R5 based on the static multicast routes programmed to reach the clients.

Subtopics

PIM-SM RP Positioning

PIM-SM RP Positioning

The rendezvous point (RP) should be located close to the multicast source. The following are
recommendations for RP:

•

If static multicast routes are redistributed into PIM network, the PIM network should be closer to the
Source, while Static multicast enabled network should be closer to the Client(s). In this case, RP should
be part of the PIM enabled network.

•  RP should not be chosen as the redistributing router, as this can cause PIM control packet drops and

eventually traffic loss.

Inter-operability with IGMP

Static multicast route does not require IGMP to be enabled on routers connected to receivers. Traffic
is flooded on the downstream interface configured for the static multicast route. However, with IGMP
and IGMP snooping enabled, traffic is sent only to the port on which join is received. Static multicast is
inter-operable with both IGMPv2 and IGMPv3.

For an IGMP enabled device, on which IGMPv3 joins are learned (applicable for MLDv2 as well), configuring
a summarized static route {any, G} causes the traffic not to be forwarded to the clients sending IGMPv3
joins. In such cases, static multicast routes must be configured with the specific source, such as {S,G}, for the
traffic to flow. This also ensures that traffic from unwanted source for the same group is not forwarded to the
clients sending IGMPv3 joins.

Public

PIM-SM RP Positioning 2198

Inter-operability with other vendors

The AOS-CX multicast static route is a proprietary feature and does not inter-operate with other vendors.
However, the router that is redistributing static multicast routes into PIM domain can be connected to
another vendor device in the upstream that supports standards-based protocols such as PIM-SM (RFC
4601) or PIM-SSM (RFC 4601). Similarly the AOS-CX Router with static multicast can be connected to a
competitor device supporting RFC 3376 and RFC 2236 to learn IGMP joins from the multicast clients.

Considerations and Best Practices

Static multicast routes must be configured with care to avoid causing traffic drop or duplicate packets. The
following are considerations and best practices for multicast static routes:

•  Static multicast route configuration on network with ECMP on downstream/upstream is not supported.

Refer to Static multicast scenarios for more information.

•  Static multicast routes should not be used in networks where duplicate traffic issues are resolved by PIM

solves using Assert/Designated Forwarder.

•

It is recommended to configure static multicast routers on all required routers before sending multicast
traffic for configured flows. This is applicable for all modes. Momentary traffic drop or duplicate packets
may occur if static multicast routes are configured on the fly on networks where multicast traffic is
already flowing.

•  Static multicast routes is not a substitute for PIM. It is recommended for configuration on smaller

networks as it can cause administrative overhead. It must be configured only when there is a scope
of summarization to resolve high resource usage, or in cases where it can solve PIM join/leave latency
issues. Refer to Use cases for more information.

◦  Refer to Static multicast scenarios for supported scenarios when both PIM and Static multicast

routes are configured on the network.

•  All downstream interfaces should be explicitly configured for a static multicast route where multicast

traffic needs to be forwarded.

•  When static multicast routes are configured to redistribute into PIM domain, the device elected as RP on

a PIM network should not be configured as the redistributing router.

•  With static multicast routes, traffic is not guaranteed to flow in the shortest path tree. Instead, it follows
the path configured using the static multicast routes on each device in the network. Refer to Static
multicast scenarios for more information.

•  When static multicast routes are redistributed into PIM, a PIM Join corresponding to the configured

static multicast route is sent on the RPF interface regardless of the configured upstream interface. PIM
Joins are sent periodically based on the configured PIM join/prune interval.

Public

Inter-operability with other vendors 2199

•  Static multicast routes are not automatically deleted with traffic inactivity and should be explicitly

removed if they are no longer in use for optimal resource usage.

•  Static multicast routes are not supported with VxLAN and GRE tunnels as upstream/downstream

interfaces.

•

It is recommended to keep number of static multicast routes on a device limited.

•  Some deployments might already be using ACLs or policies to null multicast traffic for specific groups or
sources. If a static multicast route is also configured to drop/allow traffic along with these policies, traffic
is forwarded/routed only if both static multicast route and policy are configured to allow traffic. If at least
one of these configurations is applied to drop the traffic, traffic is dropped.

Use cases

Use cases

Select a use case from the list in the left navigation menu.

The use cases provide examples of networks using multicast static routes.

Subtopics

Use case 1: SSDP enabled networks
Use case 2: Fixed channel subscription scenarios
Use case 3: IP video surveillance
Use case 4: Precision time protocol
Use case configuration

Use case 1: SSDP enabled networks

The Simple Service Discovery Protocol (SSDP) is an application layer protocol and one of the key protocols
that implement Universal Plug and Play (UPnP). SSDP enables network devices to discover and advertise
network services by sending multicast discovery and advertisement messages to multicast IPv4 group
address 239.255.255.250:1900 or multicast IPv6 group address FF0x::C[1]. With UPnP, each device
generates a unique multicast flow (Source IP, SSDP Group IP). In a multicast network with a large number
of end user devices, this can bloat up the multicast hardware and software resources as each device creates
a unique (S, G) flow and the resources are limited. In networks, where there is a need to control, drop, or
minimize SSDP traffic, summarized static multicast routes can be configured to save network resources and
to avoid denial of services.

Figure 1. Sample Static multicast route on an SSDP enabled network

Public

Use cases 2200

Use case 2: Fixed channel subscription scenarios

Static multicast routing can be used to avoid interruptions and delay due to mroute updates in cases where
there is a fixed source and set of clients, such as video conferencing. Static mroute works for a fixed path
through which the route is configured on all the devices or by redistributing static multicast routes into PIM
domain. The static multicast route should be promptly removed by the user once the multicast traffic is no
longer needed by the clients. In such cases, static multicast routes should be configured for a smaller-scale
network within a single multicast administrative domain for ease of management.

Use case 3: IP video surveillance

IP multicast video surveillance deployments are dictated by requirements such as fast convergence, fault
tolerance, and scalability. If the path between the surveillance cameras (multicast source) and the recorders
or storage systems (multicast receivers) is fixed, configuring static multicast route provides stability and
improved convergence time. Since the topology for IP video surveillance networks seldom changes, end-to-
end static multicast routes configuration for the required flows can prevent issues like surveillance blackout
caused by higher convergence time or join/leave latency caused by PIM.

Figure 1. Static multicast route end-to-end and redistribution for IP video surveillance

Public

Use case 2: Fixed channel subscription scenarios 2201

Use case 4: Precision time protocol

In the multicast transport mode, precision time protocol (PTP) uses dedicated multicast destination IP
address 224.0.1.129 as per IEEE 1588 standards for communication between devices. The primary clock
transmits the clock synchronization messages to the PTP clients as multicast packets. The number of PTP
sources and clients can be large, which can cause a large number of hardware resources to be used up in the
multicast routers which forward the PTP control packets. In networks where there is a need to optimize the
hardware resource usage, summarized static multicast routes can be configured to save network resources
and to avoid delay caused by control plane overheads.

In this use case, PIM active active is enabled. Static multicast route [ANY] is configured for both IPv4 and
IPv6, redistribute the static into PIM as CORE and EDGE-CORE are enabled with only PIM, and no static
multicast route is configured on them. VLAN 3005 and VLAN 3006 are client connected VLANs.

6410 distro-primary-static configuration

In this use case, PIM active active is enabled, static multicast route [S,G] is configured for both ipv4 and ipv6,
and no redistribute is configured on Distro as Redistribute is configured on CORE. VLAN 3005 and VLAN
3006 are client connected VLANs.

6410 distro-primary-static ANY configuration

In this use case, PIM active active is enabled. Static multicast route [ANY] is configured for both IPv4 and
IPv6, redistribute the static into PIM as CORE and EDGE-CORE are enabled with only PIM, and no static
multicast route is configured on them. VLAN 3005 and VLAN 3006 are client connected VLANs.

6410 distro-secondary only static no redistribute configuration

In this use case, PIM active active is enabled, static multicast route [S,G] is configured for both IPv4 and IPv6,
no redistribute is configured on Distro as REdistribute is configured on CORE, and VLAN 3005 and VLAN
3006 are client connected VLANs.

6410 distro-secondary configuration

In this use case, PIM active active is enabled, static multicast route [S,G] is configured for both IPv4 and IPv6,
redistribute the static into PIM as CORE and EDGE-CORE are enabled with only PIM and no static multicast
route is configured on them. VLAN 3005 and VLAN 3006 are client connected VLANs.

vlan 50

vlan 3005

name 6300-SA-Rig-Acc-Uplink
vsx-sync

ip igmp snooping enable

ipv6 mld snooping enable

vlan 3006

Public

vsx-sync

description uplink-6300-standalone-right

ipv6 mld snooping enable

8360-Edge-Core2#

Static Route commands

Select a command from the list in the left navigation menu.

Subtopics

ip multicast-static-route
ip multicast-static-route (l3vni)
ipv6 multicast-static-route
ipv6 multicast-static-route (l3vni)
redistribute static
show capacities multicast static route
show ip multicast-static-route
show ip multicast-static-route detail
show ip multicast-static-route (group)
show ipv6 multicast-static-route
show ipv6 multicast-static-route (group)
show ipv6 multicast-static-route detail
show running-config

ip multicast-static-route

Syntax

ip multicast-static-route <INCOMING-INTERFACE-NAME> {<SOURCE-ADDRESS |

ANY>} <GROUP-ADDRESS> [<OUTGOING-INTERFACE-NAME>] vrf <VRF-NAME>

no ip multicast-static-route <INCOMING-INTERFACE-NAME> {<SOURCE-ADDRESS |

ANY>} <GROUP-ADDRESS> [<OUTGOING-INTERFACE-NAME>] vrf <VRF-NAME>

Description

Configures a multicast static route for (source, group) on an incoming interface with outgoing interface(s)
not involving L3VNI. If a vrf is not specified, default vrf is used.

The no form of this command removes the configuration.

Public

Static Route commands 2341

Parameter

Description

Specifies the multicast stream incoming interface name.

<INCOMING‐INTERFACE‐NAME>

<SOURCE‐ADDRESS>

<ANY>

<GROUP‐ADDRESS>

Selects the IPv4 source address.

Selects any source address.

Specifies the IPv4 multicast group address.

Specifies the outgoing interface name.

<OUTGOING‐INTERFACE‐NAME>

vrf <VRF‐NAME>

Configures the specified VRF. The default is default vrf.

Usage

If the incoming interface is attached to different vrf than the vrf spcified in the multicast static route
command, route will be made inactive. If the vrf mismatch occurs for the outgoing interface, that particular
outgoing interface will be made inactive. If no outgoing interface is specified, the multicast traffic is not
routed and is bridged on the interface (SVI) on which the traffic is received.

Examples

Configuring Multicast Static Route with a outgoing interface:

switch(config)# ip multicast-static-route vlan10 10.10.1.2 239.255.255.250

vlan20

Removing the configured Multicast Static Route with a outgoing interface:

switch(config)# no ip multicast-static-route vlan10 10.10.1.2

239.255.255.250 vlan20

Public

ip multicast-static-route 2342

Configuring Multicast Static Route without an outgoing interface to bridge traffic only in incoming interface
in default vrf:

switch(config)# ip multicast-static-route vlan30 30.30.1.2 239.255.255.250

Removing the configured Multicast Static Route without an outgoing interface to bridge traffic only in
incoming interface in default vrf:

switch(config)# no ip multicast-static-route vlan30 30.30.1.2

239.255.255.250

Configuring Multicast Static Route without an outgoing interface to bridge traffic only in incoming interface
in non-default vrf:

switch(config)# ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan30 30.30.1.2

239.255.255.250 vrf red

Removing the configured Multicast Static Route without an outgoing interface to bridge traffic only in
incoming interface in non-default vrf:

switch(config)# ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan30 30.30.1.2

239.255.255.250 vrf red

Configuring Multicast Static Route with multiple outgoing interfaces:

switch(config)# ip multicast-static-route vlan40 40.40.1.2 239.255.255.250

vlan50 vrf red

switch(config)# ip multicast-static-route vlan40 40.40.1.2 239.255.255.250

vlan60 vrf red

switch(config)# ip multicast-static-route vlan40 40.40.1.2 239.255.255.250

1/1/1 vrf red

Removing configured Multicast Static Route with multiple outgoing interfaces:

switch(config)# no ip multicast-static-route vlan40 40.40.1.2

239.255.255.250 vlan60 vrf red
switch(config)# no ip multicast-static-route vlan40 40.40.1.2

239.255.255.250 vrf red

Configuring Static (\*, Group) Multicast Route with multiple outgoing interfaces:

switch(config)# ip multicast-static-route vlan50 any 239.255.255.250 vlan70

vrf red

switch(config)# ip multicast-static-route vlan50 any 239.255.255.250 vlan80
vrf red

Removing configured Static (\*, Group) Multicast Route with multiple outgoing interfaces:

Public

ip multicast-static-route 2343

switch(config)# no ip multicast-static-route vlan50 any 239.255.255.250

vlan70 vrf red

switch(config)# no ip multicast-static-route vlan50 any 239.255.255.250

vlan80 vrf red

execution rights for this command. Operators can execute this c
ommand from the operator context (>) only.

ip multicast-static-route (l3vni)

Syntax

ip multicast-static-route <INCOMING-INTERFACE-NAME> {<SOURCE-ADDRESS |

ANY>} <GROUP-ADDRESS>  vrf <VRF-NAME>

ip multicast-static-route <VXLAN1> {<SOURCE-ADDRESS | ANY>} <GROUP-ADDRESS>

[<OUTGOING-INTERFACE-NAME>]vrf <VRF-NAME>

no ip multicast-static-route <INCOMING-INTERFACE-NAME> {<SOURCE-ADDRESS |

ANY>} <GROUP-ADDRESS>  vrf <VRF-NAME>

no ip multicast-static-route {<SOURCE-ADDRESS | ANY>} <GROUP-ADDRESS>

[<OUTGOING-INTERFACE-NAME>]vrf <VRF-NAME>

Description

Configures a multicast static route for (source, group) involving L3VNI tunnels. If a vrf is not specified,
default vrf is used.

The no form of this command removes the configuration.

Parameter

Description

Specifies the multicast stream incoming interface name.

<INCOMING‐INTERFACE‐NAME>

Selects the IPv4 source address.

Selects any source address.

Specifies the IPv4 multicast group address.

Specifies the outgoing interface name.

<SOURCE‐ADDRESS>

<ANY>

<GROUP‐ADDRESS>

<OUTGOING‐INTERFACE‐NAME>

Public

ip multicast-static-route (l3vni) 2345

Parameter

vrf <VRF‐NAME>

Usage

Description

Configures the specified VRF. The default is default vrf.

If the incoming interface is attached to different vrf than the vrf spcified in the multicast static route
command, route will be made inactive. If the vrf mismatch occurs for the outgoing interface, that particular
outgoing interface will be made inactive.

Examples

Configuring Multicast Static Route with L2VN1 to SVI:

switch(config)# ip multicast-static-route vlan10 10.10.1.2 239.255.255.250

vlan30

Removing the configured Multicast Static Route with L2VN1 to SVI:

switch(config)# no ip multicast-static-route vlan10 10.10.1.2

239.255.255.250 vlan30

ipv6 multicast-static-route

Syntax

ipvy multicast-static-route <INCOMING-INTERFACE-NAME> {<SOURCE-ADDRESS |

ANY>} <GROUP-ADDRESS> [<OUTGOING-INTERFACE-NAME>] vrf <VRF-NAME>

no ipv6 multicast-static-route <INCOMING-INTERFACE-NAME> {<SOURCE-ADDRESS |

ANY>} <GROUP-ADDRESS> [<OUTGOING-INTERFACE-NAME>] vrf <VRF-NAME>

Description

Configures a multicast static route for (source, group) on an incoming interface with outgoing interface(s)
not involving L3VNI. If a vrf is not specified, default vrf is used.

The no form of this command removes the configuration.

Parameter

Description

Specifies the multicast stream incoming interface name.

<INCOMING‐INTERFACE‐NAME>

<SOURCE‐ADDRESS>

Selects the IPv6 source address.

Selects any source address.

Public

ipv6 multicast-static-route 2347

Parameter

<ANY>

Description

Specifies the IPv6 multicast group address.

Specifies the outgoing interface name.

<GROUP‐ADDRESS>

<OUTGOING‐INTERFACE‐NAME>

vrf <VRF‐NAME>

Configures the specified VRF. The default is default vrf.

Usage

If the incoming interface is attached to different vrf than the vrf spcified in the multicast static route
command, route will be made inactive. If the vrf mismatch occurs for the outgoing interface, that particular
outgoing interface will be made inactive.

Examples

Configuring Multicast Static Route with a outgoing interface:

switch(config)# ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan10 2001::1 ff02::c vlan20

Removing the configured Multicast Static Route with a outgoing interface:

switch(config)# no ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan10 2001::1 ff02::c vlan20

Configuring Static (\*, Group) Multicast Route with multiple outgoing interfaces:

switch(config)# ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan50 any ff0e::c vlan70 vrf

red
switch(config)# ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan50 any ff0e::c vlan80 vrf

red

Removing configured Static (\*, Group) Multicast Route with multiple outgoing interfaces:

switch(config)# no ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan50 any ff0e::c vlan70

vrf red

switch(config)# no ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan50 any ff0e::c vlan80
vrf red

ipv6 multicast-static-route (l3vni) 2350

Examples

Configuring Multicast Static Route with L2VN1 to SVI:

switch(config)# ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan10 2001::1 ff02::c vlan30

Removing the configured Multicast Static Route with L2VN1 to SVI:

switch(config)# no ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan10 2001::1 ff02::c vlan30

Public

ipv6 multicast-static-route (l3vni) 2351

redistribute static

Syntax

redistribute static

no redistribute static

Description

Exports all static multicast routes configured on a VRF to the corresponding PIM router. The no form of this
command disables the redistribution.

Usage

Static multicast routes give the flexibility to program a specific path for multicast traffic from the source
to the client without having to rely on the underlying protocols to build a multicast route. They can be
configured on all the routers in the path or on a specific section of routers. The remaining section of routers
can be configured to run the native PIM protocol. In such cases, the static multicast route is exported to PIM
domain as PIM joins using this command.

Examples

Configuring redistribute static routes to PIM for IPv4:

switch(config)# router pim

switch(config-pim)# redistribute static

Disabling redistribute static routes to PIM for IPv4:

switch(config)# router pim

switch(config-pim)# no redistribute static

Configuring redistribute static routes to PIM for IPv4 on vrf red:

switch(config)# router pim vrf red

switch(config-pim)# redistribute static

Disabling redistribute static routes to PIM for IPv4 on vrf red:

switch(config)# router pim vrf red

switch(config-pim)# no redistribute static

Configuring redistribute static routes to PIM for IPv6:

switch(config)# router pim6
switch(config-pim)# redistribute static

Disabling redistribute static routes to PIM for IPv6:

Public

redistribute static 2352

switch(config)# router pim6

switch(config-pim)# no redistribute static

Configuring redistribute static routes to PIM for IPv6 on vrf red:

switch(config)# router pim6 vrf red

switch(config-pim)# redistribute static

Disabling redistribute static routes to PIM for IPv6 on vrf red:

switch(config)# router pim6 vrf red

switch(config-pim)# no redistribute static

Debugging and Troubleshooting

The recommended static multicast route troubleshooting flow is provided in Figure 1, Multicast static route
troubleshooting flow. Additional tips and guidance for each step are provided below.

Figure 1. Static multicast route troubleshooting flow

Step 1: Check the network reachability between Source DR and the multicast receivers.

1.  Ensure there is connectivity between Source DR and Client DR devices where static multicast routes are

configured.

Public

Debugging and Troubleshooting 2371

2.  Since static multicast routes use L3 interfaces to forward the traffic, the IPv4 or IPv6 address has to be

configured.

3.

It is not mandatory to run unicast routing protocols between Source DR and Client DR devices to achieve
static multicast routing.

Step 2: Validate static multicast configuration on all the routers from Source DR to Client DR.

1.  Ensure there are static multicast routes configured on all devices between Source DR and Client DR

devices so that they form a forwarding path.
switch# show running-config

Current configuration:

!

-------------------------------------------------------------------------

-----

ip multicast-static-route vlan10 10.10.1.2 239.255.255.250 vlan20

ip multicast-static-route vlan10 10.10.1.2 239.255.255.250 1/1/2

ip multicast-static-route 1/1/1 10.10.1.2 239.255.255.250 1/1/2

ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan10 2001::1 ff02::c vlan20

ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan50 any ff0e::c vlan70 vrf red

ipv6 multicast-static-route vlan50 any ff0e::c vlan80 vrf red

Step 3: Check for ovelapping static multicast routes.

1.  Configuration of overlapping static multicast routes can cause duplicate traffic or traffic drop.

2.  Traffic forwarding is based on longest prefix match, so {S, G} static routes are preferred over {*, G}

routes even when both entries are configured.

3.  Configuration of overlapping static multicast routes on the device should be avoided if possible.

switch# show running-config

Current configuration:

!

-------------------------------------------------------------------------
-----

ip multicast-static-route vlan4016 10.100.1.2 231.0.0.1 vlan4020

ip multicast-static-route vlan4016 any 231.0.0.1 vlan4021

Step 4: Check if multicast static route is operational.

1.  Ensure that the static multicast routes configured are operation.

a.

b.

If the upstream interface of the configured static multicast route is not operationally up, the static
multicast route is considered non-operational and is not programmed into the hardware.

If the upstream interface of the configured static multicast route is not with an IP address, the static
multicast route is considered non-operational and is not programmed into the hardware.

Public

Debugging and Troubleshooting 2372

c.

d.

If the downstream interface(s) of the configured static multicast route is not operationally up, the
static multicast route may be programmed as a bridge entry in the hardware.

If the downstream interface(s) of the configured static multicast route is not configured with an IP
address, the static multicast route may be programmed as a bridge entry in the hardware.

switch# show ip multicast-static-route 239.255.255.250 40.40.40.2 all-

vrfs

VRF: blue

Group Address               : 239.255.255.250

Source Address              : 40.40.40.2

Route type                  : Static

Incoming interface          : vlan40

Outgoing interface list     :

Interface         State

----------        -----

vlan20            forwarding

vlan30            forwarding

Step 5: Check for redistribute configuration if PIM network is also enabled for the same flow.

1.  For the redistribute mode where static multicast routes are redistributed into PIM domain as joins, the  r

edistribute  command should be configured in the route PIM context.

2.  While redistributing the static multicast routes, do not configure static multicast routes on ECMP

upstream paths as this can cause duplicate traffic.

3.  Verify PIM mroutes on the neighboring router to the redistributing device to ensure successful

redistribution.
switch# show running-config

Current configuration:

!

router pim

redistribute static

-------------------------------------------------------------------------

-----

ip multicast-static-route vlan4016 10.100.1.2 231.0.0.1 vlan4020

switch2# show ip mroute

IP Multicast Route Entries

VRF: default

Total number of entries: 1

Group Address          : 231.0.0.1

Source Address         : 10.100.1.1

SSM Mroute             : False

Neighbor               :

Public

Debugging and Troubleshooting 2373

Uptime                 : 00:15:51

State                  : route

Incoming interface     : vlan100

Outgoing interface List:

Interface          State

---------          -----

vlan4016           forwarding

Step 6: Check multicast datapath to ensure route installation in hardware.

1.  Check multicast datapath command on all the devices to verify the routes are successful programmed.

2.  Check mulitcast datapath command on all the devices to verify if the static multicast routes are active

and that traffic is being forwarded using the static multicast route.
switch# show ip multicast routing datapath group 239.1.1.1 source

10.1.1.2 port vlan10

Multicast Routing Data-path Details

VRF                          : default

Source                       : 10.1.1.2/32

Group                        : 239.1.1.1/32

Primary Upstream Interface   : vlan10

From                         : static

Type                         : route

Replication Group Index      : 4097

Hardware Status              : active

Error Code                   : None

Retries for programming      : 0

Upstream Interface           : vlan10

     State                   : forwarding

Downstream Interface         : vlan20

     State                   : forwarding

Frequently Asked Questions

1. Can I enable both PIM and Static Multicast Route for the same flow in a network?

No, if a static multicast route is configured and operational, PIM does not learn the matching flow, and hence
does not produce an mroute for this flow. Once static multicast route is deleted, PIM starts learning this flow
and programs mroute into the forwarding table.

2. Does Static Multicast Route rely on the underlying unicast routing protocol?

Static multicast routes do not rely on the underlying unicast routing protocols to be operational. They rely
on the configured path to forward the traffic from multicast source to receivers. For example, if the network

Public

Frequently Asked Questions 2374

only requires multicast forwarding using static multicast routing, OSPF/BGP for unicast traffic forwarding is
not required. Currently, static multicast route do not use RPF checks or ensure reachability to Source before
programming an mroute into the forwarding table.

3. Are there are any platform differences to be aware of?

Static Multicast Routing is supported on the 5420, 6200, 6300, 6400, 8100, 8320, 8325, 8360, 8400 and
10000 platforms. However, summarized static multicast route is not supported on the 8400 platform.

4. Is the Active-Active command required on VSX deployments?

Static multicast route feature is agnostic to Active-Active configuration in VSX devices. With static multicast
route, VSX-primary routes the packet while VSX-Secondary programs a bridge entry in the forwarding table
irrespective of active-active configuration.

5. Does the solution work for all types of interfaces?

The solution works with all types of underlay interfaces that are currently supported in AOS-CX. Static
multicast on VxLAN and GRE tunnels are not supported.

6. Can I replace PIM with Static Multicast route on my network?

No. Static multicast route is not a substitute for PIM. It should be used in small networks under a single
administrative domain and for very specific cases as mentioned in Use cases.

7. What is the difference between static multicast routing as described in this chapter versus the
following command?

switch# router pim

ip mroute 10.1.1.1/32 10.2.2.2
The  router pim  command configures multicast routing to use a separate path for RPF, which is
different from unicast routing. It still requires a multicast protocol, like PIM-SM, to route the packet. Static
multicast routing as described in this chapter program multicast forwarding directly into the hardware
without PIM.

8. How can I avoid flooding when static multicast routing is defined?

Configure IGMP Snooping at the mutlicast receiver network.

Accessing Updates

You can access updates from the HPE Aruba Networking Support Portal at https://
networkingsupport.hpe.com.

Some software products provide a mechanism for accessing software updates through the product interface.
Review your product documentation to identify the recommended software update method.

To subscribe to eNewsletters and alerts:

https://networkingsupport.hpe./notifications/subscriptions (requires an active HPE Aruba Networking
Support Portal account to manage subscriptions). Security notices are viewable without an HPE Aruba
Networking Support Portal account.

Regulatory Information

Additional regulatory information

HPE Aruba Networking is committed to providing our customers with information about the chemical
substances in our products as needed to comply with legal requirements, environmental data (company
programs, product recycling, energy efficiency), and safety information and compliance data, (RoHS and
WEEE). For more information, see https://www.arubanetworks.com/company/about-us/environmental-
citizenship/.

Documentation Feedback

HPE Aruba Networking is committed to providing documentation that meets your needs. To help us improve
the documentation, send any errors, suggestions, or comments to Documentation Feedback (docsfeedback-
switching@hpe.com). When submitting your feedback, include the document title, part number, edition, and
publication date located on the front cover of the document. For online help content, include the product
name, product version, help edition, and publication date located on the legal notices page.
